facets.q: What are the mechanisms of body colour change in praying mantises and orthopteran insects?
Can praying mantises regenerate amputated limbs, and how does regenerative capacity change across nymphal instars?

results.title: The pigment transporter Redboy confers programmed body colour transition in orchid mantises
results.author: Xiaojin Pei
results.takeaway: The pigment transporter Redboy in orchid mantises, which promotes ecological adaptation by coordinating gene family evolution and hormone regulation, contributes to the transition from black-red to white body color in later instars.

results.title: Current evidence of climate‐driven colour change in insects and its impact on sexual signals
results.author: Md Tangigul Haque
results.takeaway: Climate change may drive changes in insect colors, impacting their physiology and interactions with conspecifics and heterospecifics.

results.title: Differentiation in the body color of Creobroter gemmata under red and green background at an early stage
results.author: S. Liu
results.takeaway: Creobroter gemmata praying mantises can differentiate their body colors based on background color, with more significant differentiation in early stages.

results.title: The evolution of insect body coloration under changing climates.
results.author: S. Clusella‐Trullas
results.takeaway: Insect coloration is evolving in response to climate change, but understanding multiple factors is crucial for assigning color variation as an evolutionary response.

results.title: Evolutionary genomics of camouflage innovation in the orchid mantis
results.author: Guang-Ming Huang
results.takeaway: The orchid mantis' unique camouflage is influenced by the Scarlet gene, Wnt signaling, and the Trypsin gene family, providing insights into phenotypic innovation and adaptation.

results.title: Coloration in a Praying Mantis: Color Change, Sexual Color Dimorphism, and Possible Camouflage Strategies
results.author: L. Y. Rosenheim
results.takeaway: Praying mantis exhibit sexual color dimorphism, with highly mobile males showing generalist coloration and more sedentary females showing specialist coloration, potentially reflecting differences in camouflage strategies.

results.title: Physiological and Biochemical Mechanisms of Insect Color Change Towards Understanding Molecular Links
results.author: M. Moriyama
results.journal: Pigments, Pigment Cells and Pigment Patterns
results.takeaway: Insect color changes are achieved through a complex system involving diverse mechanisms, including perception, integration systems, neuroendocrine signaling, and color-generating apparatuses.

results.title: Combinatorial expression of ebony and tan generates body color variation from nymph through adult stages in the cricket, Gryllus bimaculatus
results.author: Shintaro Inoue
results.takeaway: Combinatorial expression of ebony and tan genes, which encode enzymes that synthesize melanin and yellow sclerotin, determines stage-specific body color patterns in the cricket Gryllus bimaculatus during postembryonic development.

results.title: Replicated Differential Expression Analysis in a Green‐Brown Polymorphic Grasshopper Reveals Role of Beta‐Carotene‐Binding Protein in Body Coloration
results.author: Chongyi Jiang
results.takeaway: Beta-carotene-binding proteins (CBPs) play a key role in the differentiation between green and brown morphs in the club-legged grasshopper Gomphocerus sibiricus, suggesting a Caelifera-specific mechanism for green body coloration.

results.title: Pigmentation and color pattern diversity in Odonata.
results.author: Genta Okude
results.takeaway: Ommochrome and pteridine pigments are essential for light-blue coloration in Odonata, with gene-functional analysis warranting future studies.

results.title: Different transcriptional levels of Corazonin, Elevenin and PDF according to the body-color of the two-spotted cricket, Gryllus bimaculatus.
results.author: Hitomi Seike
results.takeaway: Corazonin and Elevenin contribute to body color changes in two-spotted crickets, Gryllus bimaculatus, but their melanization mechanisms are differently controlled than in Locusta migratoria.

results.title: Ontogeny of color development in two green–brown polymorphic grasshopper species
results.author: Mahendra Varma
results.takeaway: Green-brown color morphs in grasshoppers begin developing during the second nymphal stage and remain stable throughout the life of an individual, with their effectiveness varying depending on the specific predator.

results.title: Reversible colour change in Arthropoda
results.author: K. Umbers
results.takeaway: Reversible color change in arthropods is diverse and occurs through diverse mechanisms, with limited behavioral and ecological data, requiring phylogenetically controlled approaches for better understanding.

results.title: Crypsis by background matching and disruptive coloration as drivers of substrate occupation in sympatric Amazonian bark praying mantises
results.author: João Vitor de Alcantara Viana
results.takeaway: Amazonian bark praying mantises with background matching and disruptive coloration effectively enhance their survival against predators, increasing search time and reducing encounter distance.

results.title: Plastic background colour matching in the springbok mantis
results.author: N. Burke
results.takeaway: The springbok mantis exhibits strong developmental plasticity in coloration, with green backgrounds promoting all-green phenotypes and brown backgrounds producing mixed phenotypes, likely due to habitat diversity and sedentary lifestyle.

results.title: Benefits of insect colours: a review from social insect studies
results.author: Oluwatobi Badejo
results.takeaway: Insect colors aid in body protection, signaling, and physiological adaptations, and can be used for species identification, individual quality, and ecological research.

results.title: Natural history and phenotypic variation of Metaphotina brevipennis (Sassure 1872) (Acontistidae, Mantodea)
results.author: Drielly Queiroga
results.takeaway: Metaphotina brevipennis is highly plastic in its strategies and highly adapts to Cerrado seasonality, using color variation for survival and behavioral differences in habitat choice.

results.title: Coloration in Argia damselflies of Central Mexico responds to changes in herbaceous vegetation
results.author: Teresa L. Ramos‐Merino
results.takeaway: Urbanization and reduced vegetation in Central Mexico's Argia damselflies lead to a significant relationship between body lightness and the percentage of herbaceous vegetation.

results.title: Mechanism of Color Pattern Formation in Insects
results.author: Yuichi Fukutomi
results.journal: Pigments, Pigment Cells and Pigment Patterns
results.takeaway: Insects' color patterns are shaped by spatial information, genes, and cis-regulatory evolution, with a comprehensive model still needed for validation.

results.title: Author Correction: Crypsis by background matching and disruptive coloration as drivers of substrate occupation in sympatric Amazonian bark praying mantises
results.author: João Vitor de Alcantara Viana
results.takeaway: Crypsis by background matching and disruptive coloration drive substrate occupation in sympatric Amazonian bark praying mantises, similar to other small avian predators that forage on trunks.

results.title: Species-specific oxygen sensing governs the initiation of vertebrate limb regeneration
results.author: Georgios Tsissios
results.takeaway: Species-specific oxygen sensing capacity can unlock latent regenerative programs in mammals, explaining why mammals cannot regenerate limbs like amphibians do.

results.title: Unravelling the limb regeneration mechanisms of Polypedates maculatus, a sub-tropical frog, by transcriptomics
results.author: Cuckoo Mahapatra
results.takeaway: Polypedates maculatus frogs exhibit species-specific limb regeneration mechanisms, offering insights into alternative tactics for regenerative medicine and engineering.

results.title: Investigating the developmental onset of regenerative potential in the annelid Capitella teleta
results.author: Alicia A. Boyd
results.takeaway: Larvae of Capitella teleta exhibit intermediate regeneration potential and show some features of juvenile regeneration, but do not successfully replace all lost structures.

results.title: Comparison of Leg Regeneration Potency Between Holometabolous Helicoverpa armigera (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) and Hemimetabolous Locusta migratoria manilensis (Orthoptera: Acrididae)
results.author: Qingpo Yang
results.takeaway: Helicoverpa armigera can regenerate intact legs when excised at early larval stages, while Locusta migratoria has weak partial regeneration ability, with regeneration negatively impacting larval development.

results.title: The Capacity for Limb Regeneration During Metamorphosis is Broadly Conserved in the Coccinellidae
results.author: J. Michaud
results.journal: Annals of the Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration capacity is widely conserved in the Coccinellidae family, contributing to ecological success and influencing the distribution and behavior of these species.

results.title: Systemic Cell Cycle Re‐entry Following Amputation in Axolotl: Consequence and Mechanism
results.author: Jessica L. Whited
results.takeaway: Axolotl salamanders show a two-step model for limb regeneration, with systemic activation before local refinement at the injury site.

results.title: Regrowing the growth zone: metamorphosis kickstarts regeneration in the annelid Capitella teleta.
results.author: Alicia A. Boyd
results.takeaway: Metamorphosis in Capitella teleta larvae enables juveniles to regrow lost structures, highlighting the importance of metamorphosis in developmental plasticity.

results.title: The Regeneration of Legs in Rhodnius Prolixus (Hemiptera)
results.author: M. Lüscher
results.takeaway: Partial regeneration of legs in Rhodnius prolixus is possible, with regeneration occurring at the femur-tibia joint and increasing gradually in a distal direction, but it ceases after the fourth post-amputation moult.

results.title: Effect of larval thermal conditions on limb regeneration in a ladybird beetle, Cheilomenes sexmaculata.
results.author: Shriza Rai
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part A, Ecological and integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Larval thermal conditions affect limb regeneration in ladybird beetles, with higher temperatures accelerating recovery time after limb amputation.

results.title: A developmentally regulated switch from stem cells to dedifferentiation for limb muscle regeneration in newts
results.author: H. Tanaka
results.takeaway: Newts have evolved novel strategies to maintain their limb regeneration ability after metamorphosis, switching from stem/progenitor-based to dedifferentiation-based mechanisms.

results.title: THE LOSS AND RESTORATION OF REGENERATIVE CAPACITY IN THE LIMBS OF TAILLESS AMPHIBIA 1
results.author: L. W. Polezhayev
results.takeaway: Tadpoles' limbs can be restored by dedifferentiation of mesodermal tissue in the distal part of the stump, allowing for repeated regeneration up to the completion of metamorphosis.

results.title: A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice
results.author: M. Abrams
results.takeaway: Feeding moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice with L-leucine and insulin/sugar increases appendage regeneration, suggesting a conserved strategy across animal species.

results.title: To regenerate or not to regenerate: Vertebrate model organisms of regeneration-competency and -incompetency
results.author: Can Aztekin
results.journal: Wound repair and regeneration : official publication of the Wound Healing Society [and] the European Tissue Repair Society
results.takeaway: Studying model organisms with varying regenerative abilities can help uncover factors contributing to regeneration-incompetency in mammals and test functional restoration strategies.

results.title: The hidden costs of regeneration in ladybird beetles.
results.author: Sanjeev Kumar
results.takeaway: Regeneration in ladybird beetles leads to developmental and reproductive trade-offs, with reduced mating success and reproductive output.

results.title: Altered metabolic state impedes limb regeneration in salamanders
results.author: Zhengrong Peng
results.takeaway: Under extreme physiological stress, salamanders can exploit alternative strategies to secure blastema formation for limb regeneration, but limb outgrowth and patterning are significantly attenuated.

results.title: Advances in Decoding Axolotl Limb Regeneration
results.author: B. Haas
results.takeaway: Axolotl limb regeneration has been decoded using mRNA and functional experimentation, providing valuable insights for regenerative medicine.

results.title: Harem size and horn symmetry in oribi
results.author: P. Arcese
results.takeaway: Adult zig-zag ladybird beetles with all limbs can survive limb amputations, but some repair may occur during the pupal stage.

results.title: A histological study of normal and pathological limb regeneration in the Mexican axolotl Ambystoma mexicanum.
results.author: Vivien Bothe
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Imperfect regeneration in larval axolotls leads to diverse limb and digit abnormalities, contradicting the notion of regeneration generally resulting in perfect limbs.

results.title: Gill regeneration in the mayfly Cloeon uncovers new molecular pathways in insect regeneration
results.author: C. Martin-Blanco
results.takeaway: The mayfly Cloeon can regenerate lost abdominal gills faster than normal development, revealing new molecular pathways in insect regeneration.

results.title: Sexual selection drives the evolution of limb regeneration in Harmonia axyridis (Coleoptera: Coccinellidae).
results.author: S. Wang
results.takeaway: Sexual selection drives the evolution of limb regeneration in Harmonia axyridis beetles, with 75% regenerating a forelimb during pupation, and regenerating males being preferred by females and producing more viable progeny.

55.state: Mantis body-colour change and its genetic and ecological basis are an active field: recent work ties mantid camouflage to specific pigment genes (Scarlet, the Redboy transporter), Wnt signalling and climate-driven colour evolution, and confirms background-dependent colour change in several species. Przibram's twin themes — colour change and appendage regeneration in the mantis — are both live, though this founding paper goes uncited.

facets.q: Is animal pigmentation produced enzymatically by tyrosinase acting on DOPA to form melanin?
What physiological and chemical mechanisms control animal coloration?

results.title: MAMMALIAN TYROSINASE AND DOPA OXIDASE
results.author: G. H. Hogeboom
results.takeaway: The mechanism of melanin production in mammals involves a specific enzyme, dopa oxidase, and a rich source of tyrosinase, but the exact enzyme responsible for melanin formation remains inconclusive.

results.title: Tyrosinases from two different loci are expressed by normal and by transformed melanocytes.
results.author: M. Jiménez
results.takeaway: The brown and albino loci of mice encode similar tyrosinase proteins, suggesting that melanogenesis in mammals is controlled by multiple gene products.

results.title: A second tyrosinase‐related protein, TRP‐2, is a melanogenic enzyme termed DOPAchrome tautomerase.
results.author: K. Tsukamoto
results.takeaway: TRP-2, a tyrosinase-related protein, encodes a protein with DOPAchrome tautomerase activity, which plays a role in regulating the proportion of carboxylated subunits in melanin biopolymer.

results.title: Mammalian tyrosinase catalyzes three reactions in the biosynthesis of melanin.
results.author: A. Körner
results.takeaway: Mammalian tyrosinase plays a crucial role in the biosynthesis of melanin, with its unique ability to catalyze three distinct reactions within a single biochemical pathway.

results.title: Tyrosinase and related proteins in mammalian pigmentation
results.author: V. del Marmol
results.takeaway: Tyrosinase and related proteins play a crucial role in mammalian pigmentation by converting tyrosine to melanin, with recent findings suggesting a common ancestor for these genes.

results.title: Mammalian tyrosinase; preparation and properties.
results.author: A. Lerner
results.takeaway: Mammalian tyrosinase, an enzyme that catalyzes melanin production, has been confirmed to exist and is involved in the conversion of tyrosine to melanin in mammals.

results.title: Determination of Melanin Synthetic Pathways
results.author: V. Hearing
results.takeaway: The melanin synthetic pathway involves tyrosinase, a key enzyme, and a complex network of enzymes, resulting in various melanin types and their unique properties.

results.title: Tyrosinase related protein 1 (TRP1) functions as a DHICA oxidase in melanin biosynthesis.
results.author: Takeshi Kobayashi
results.takeaway: TRP1 plays a crucial role in melanin biosynthesis by oxidizing DHICA to a carboxylated indole-quinone, essential for the further metabolism of DHICA to a pigmented biopolymer.

results.title: Mammalian tyrosinase. A comparison of tyrosine hydroxylation and melanin formation.
results.author: V. Hearing
results.takeaway: In the absence of dopa cofactor, mammalian tyrosinase can perform tyrosine hydroxylation, but with minimal dopa oxidation and melanin formation, potentially playing a role in intracellular regulation of melanin formation.

results.title: Mammalian tyrosinase; melanin formation by ultraviolet irradiation.
results.author: T. Fitzpatrick
results.takeaway: UV irradiation activates mammalian tyrosinase, enabling the formation of melanin from tyrosine in various tissues.

results.title: Enzymatic control of pigmentation in mammals
results.author: V. Hearing
results.takeaway: Recent studies show that mammalian melanogenesis is not solely regulated by tyrosinase, and additional factors can modulate pigmentation in either a positive or negative manner.

results.title: The role of peroxidase vs. the role of tyrosinase in enzymatic conversion of tyrosine to melanin in melanocytes, mast cells and eosinophils.
results.author: M. R. Okun
results.takeaway: Peroxidase may initiate melanin synthesis, while tyrosinase may function primarily as a dopa oxidase, suggesting that peroxidase may initiate melanin synthesis.

results.title: Chemical characterization of pheomelanogenesis starting from dihydroxyphenylalanine or tyrosine and cysteine. Effects of tyrosinase and cysteine concentrations and reaction time.
results.author: Hiroyuki Ozeki
results.takeaway: Lower tyrosinase activity favors pheomelanogenesis, indicating that tyrosinase activity is the major factor controlling the course of melanogenesis.

results.title: Tyrosinase Nanoparticles: Understanding the Melanogenesis Pathway by Isolating the Products of Tyrosinase Enzymatic Reaction
results.author: P. Varghese
results.takeaway: Tyr-MB nanoparticles show potential for large-scale production of dopachrome and melanin-related products, potentially aiding in treatments for oculocutaneous albinism-inherited diseases.

results.title: Study of tyrosine and dopa enantiomers as tyrosinase substrates initiating l‐ and d‐melanogenesis pathways
results.author: Pedro Fernandez-Julia
results.takeaway: D-tyrosine acts as a real substrate of tyrosinase with low catalytic efficiency, causing slower d-melanogenesis than l-melanogenesis from l-tyrosine.

results.title: A revised concept of mammalian melanogenesis: the possible synergistic functions of aerobic dopa oxidase and peroxidase. A review.
results.author: M. Okun
results.journal: The Yale Journal of Biology and Medicine
results.takeaway: Mammalian melanogenesis may involve a synergistic function of aerobic dopa oxidase and peroxidase, potentially allowing for the production of melanin in a more efficient manner.

results.title: Visualization of Intracellular Tyrosinase Activity in vitro
results.author: Riddhi Atul Jani
results.takeaway: This study presents a method to visualize intracellular tyrosinase activity in mouse melanocytes, aiding in understanding the role of tyrosinase in skin pigmentation.

results.title: Natural and synthetic flavonoid derivatives as new potential tyrosinase inhibitors: a systematic review
results.author: R. Obaid
results.takeaway: Natural and synthetic flavonoid derivatives show potential as effective tyrosinase inhibitors for treating pigmentation and dermatological disorders.

results.title: TYR Gene in Llamas: Polymorphisms and Expression Study in Different Color Phenotypes
results.author: M. Anello
results.takeaway: TYR gene expression levels play a role in llama pigmentation, with c.1-26C > T polymorphism partially associated to diluted phenotypes and lower expression levels in white llamas.

results.title: The 5,6-dihydroxyindole-2-carboxylic acid (DHICA) oxidase activity of human tyrosinase.
results.author: C. Olivares
results.takeaway: Human tyrosinase functions as a DHICA oxidase, with a broader substrate specificity than mouse tyrosinase, potentially partially responsible for the incorporation of DHICA units into human eumelanins.

results.title: Animal colour change: proximate mechanisms, evolutionary ecology and response to anthropogenic impacts.
results.author: Rafael C. Duarte

results.title: Biochemical regulation of pigment motility in vertebrate chromatophores: a review of physiological color change mechanisms
results.author: Russell A. Ligon
results.takeaway: Different hormones, neurotransmitters, and signals direct pigment reorganization in vertebrate chromatophores, enabling physiological color change in various species.

results.title: A mechanism for red coloration in vertebrates
results.author: Matthew B. Toomey
results.takeaway: Two enzymes, CYP2J19 and BDH1L, catalyze the conversion of yellow carotenoids into red ketocarotenoids in vertebrates, with TTC39B enhancing this process in red-cone photoreceptors.

results.title: Metabolism, Function, Molecular Mechanism, and Application of Carotenoids in Coloration of Aquatic Animals
results.author: Yongguan Liao
results.takeaway: Carotenoids play a crucial role in aquatic animal coloration, with their metabolism and gene involvement influencing ornamental fish breeding and aquaculture.

results.title: The physical and cellular mechanism of structural color change in zebrafish
results.author: Dvir Gur
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Norepinephrine-induced color change in zebrafish is driven by a coordinated 20° tilting of intracellular crystals, with dynein and cAMP regulating the process.

results.title: Chemical and biochemical control of skin pigmentation with special emphasis on mixed melanogenesis
results.author: K. Wakamatsu
results.takeaway: Mixed melanogenesis, a complex process involving eumelanin and pheomelanin synthesis, is influenced by factors such as chemical regulation, melanosomal pH regulation, and gene expression.

results.title: The biology of color
results.author: I. Cuthill
results.takeaway: Advances in color biology are revolutionizing our understanding of animal coloration and its role in social signaling, defense, thermoregulation, and protection.

results.title: Interactions between colour-producing mechanisms and their effects on the integumentary colour palette
results.author: M. Shawkey
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Combining pigments and structural integumentary tissues can create colors and optical properties unachievable by either mechanism alone, expanding the animal color palette and highlighting the complexity of color classification.

results.title: Avian Pigment Pattern Formation: Developmental Control of Macro- (Across the Body) and Micro- (Within a Feather) Level of Pigment Patterns
results.author: Masafumi Inaba
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Avian plumage color patterns are mainly controlled by melanocytes, with non-melanin chemical pigments and structural colors also contributing to the available palette.

results.title: Rapid color change in fish and amphibians – function, regulation, and emerging applications
results.author: Helen Nilsson Sköld
results.takeaway: Fish and amphibians exhibit rapid color change for background matching, thermoregulation, and signaling, with external factors playing a key role in regulating this process.

results.title: Pterin-based pigmentation in animals
results.author: P. Andrade
results.takeaway: Pterin-based pigmentation in animals offers unique opportunities to study coloration biology and its evolution, with potential for future research on laboratory models and natural variation.

results.title: The thermal dependence and molecular basis of physiological color change in Takydromus septentrionalis (Lacertidae)
results.author: Kun Guo
results.takeaway: Physiological color change in lizards, like Takydromus septentrionalis, helps regulate body temperature by altering pigment arrangement and solar radiation absorption/reflection.

results.title: The formation and mechanism of plumage color diversity based on carotenoid pigmentation
results.author: B. Xue
results.takeaway: Birds' plumage color diversity is driven by carotenoid pigmentation, which serves as a signal of individual condition and provides opportunities for evolution driven by natural and sexual selection.

results.title: Body coloration and mechanisms of colour production in Archelosauria: the case of deirocheline turtles
results.author: Jindřich Brejcha
results.takeaway: Archelosaurs use vertical layering of pigment cell types and carotenoids/pterins for coloration, while also employing novel mechanisms like nano-organization of dermal collagen, shared with mammals.

results.title: The physiological cost of colour change: evidence, implications and mitigations.
results.author: Ateah Alfakih
results.takeaway: Rapid color change in animals is more costly due to production, translocation, and maintenance of pigments, with potential implications for their behavior and evolution.

results.title: The Vitamin A–Redox Hypothesis: A Biochemical Basis for Honest Signaling via Carotenoid Pigmentation
results.author: G. Hill
results.takeaway: The vitamin A-redox hypothesis suggests that carotenoid pigmentation in animals is linked to cellular efficiency and performance, such as immune function, rather than resource trade-offs.

results.title: The integrative biology of pigment organelles, a quantum chemical approach.
results.author: Florent Figon
results.takeaway: Quantum chemistry provides insights into how pigment properties, from color to reactivity, are modulated by their organellar environment, leading to a greater understanding of animal coloration.

results.title: Coloration in Mammals
results.author: T. Caro
results.takeaway: Mammalian coloration is driven by developmental factors, background matching, intra- and interspecific signaling, and physiological influences.

results.title: Unraveling the true complexity of costly color signaling
results.author: D. Kemp
results.takeaway: Animal coloration is a complex and diverse biological signal, influenced by both chemical colorants and optically active surface structures.

results.title: Pigmentation and colour changing mechanism in fishes
results.author: Mintumoni Pegu
results.takeaway: Fish pigmentation is influenced by genetic, morphological, physiological, and environmental factors, with genes and nutritional contents playing crucial roles in pigment production and color change.

157.state: Przibram's demand for a chemical theory of colour was answered: melanin is produced enzymatically by tyrosinase and its related proteins (TRP-1/2, DOPA oxidase and tautomerase) acting on tyrosine and DOPA — the enzymatic pathway he and Brecher were chasing. It is now standard pigment-cell biology.

facets.q: Is insect growth across successive moults described by a constant ratio (Dyar's law or Przibram's rule)?
Do quantitative allometric growth laws hold across arthropod postembryonic development?

results.title: Towards a mechanistic understanding of insect life history evolution: oxygen‐dependent induction of moulting explains moulting sizes
results.author: S. M. Kivelä
results.journal: Biological Journal of The Linnean Society

results.title: Dyar's rule and multivariate allometric growth in nine species of waterstriders (Heteroptera: Gerridae)
results.author: C. Klingenberg
results.takeaway: Dyar's rule is inconsistent in waterstrider species, but growth ratios vary within a limited range, with head width growth ratios differing more between moults than multivariate size.

results.title: A Quantitative Analysis of Growth and Size Regulation in Manduca sexta: The Physiological Basis of Variation in Size and Age at Metamorphosis
results.author: L. Grunert
results.takeaway: Manduca sexta larvae grow progressively from instar to instar, with temperature-dependent declines in mass-specific growth rate, and different adult body sizes affect larval growth and development time.

results.title: The Progression Factor in Insect Growth
results.author: F. S. Bodenheimer
results.takeaway: The growth factor in insect growth is a general rule of 2 for weight and i.26 for length, with deviations in complex cases being only deviations from this principle.

results.title: Relationship of Size of Fecal Pellets to Larval Molts in Lepidoptera
results.author: J. B. Sardesai
results.journal: Annals of The Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: The size of fecal pellets in Lepidoptera larvae can be used as an easy and reliable index for determining larval molt size, providing a more reliable method for entomologists than head-capsule size.

results.title: Growth of Insects with Reference to Progression Factors for Successive Growth Stages
results.author: F. Harries
results.journal: Annals of The Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: This study demonstrates that Cicadellidae species show a consistent growth pattern, with a progression factor for each growth stage, supporting the Dyar rule and Przibram principle.

results.title: Growth ratios in holometabolous and hemimetabolous insects.
results.author: B. Cole
results.journal: Annals of The Entomological Society of America

results.title: Instar determination by constrained gaussian mixture models according to Dyar’s rule
results.author: Sungmin Ji
results.takeaway: This study proposes a method to efficiently determine insect instars using Dyar's rule and common variance assumptions, improving accuracy in ecological studies and pest control.

results.title: THE ONTOGENY OF ASYMMETRY IN EARWIG FORCEPS
results.author: J. L. Tomkins
results.takeaway: Asymmetries in male earwig forceps can be maintained through compensatory growth, persistent growth, or uncorrelated growth, with the latter two being more sensitive to asymmetry changes.

results.title: Geometrics of growth in four species of Spodoptera
results.author: M. Ramaiah
results.takeaway: The linear growth ratio in Spodoptera species varies, with S. exigua showing the maximum progression factor between 2nd and 3rd instars.

results.title: Per‐stage growth rates of head and body sizes in scarab larvae (Coleoptera: Scarabaeidae) decrease across ontogeny following a species‐specific pattern
results.author: S. Hoshizaki
results.takeaway: The per-stage growth rate of body size does not follow Dyar's Rule in a wide range of insects, suggesting developmental change in this rate varies among insect groups.

results.title: Morphometrics of Spodoptera litura on tomato
results.author: S. Vashisth
results.takeaway: Spodoptera litura caterpillars on tomato show a geometrical progression in head capsule width, aiding in identification of larval instars.

results.title: Dyar's Rule and the Investment Principle: optimal moulting strategies if feeding rate is size-dependent and growth is discontinuous
results.author: J. Hutchinson
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B
results.takeaway: The Investment Principle predicts optimal size increases and instar durations for animals with feeding rate dependent on moulting structures, offering a new adaptive explanation for Dyar's Rule and common deviations from it.

results.title: Studies on the growth of Pediculus (Anoplura)
results.author: P. A. Buxton
results.takeaway: Pediculus larvae grow allometrically or heterogonically, with differences in proportions between larvae stages, and growth rates vary between sexes at maturity.

results.title: Size, Food-finding, and Dyar's Constant
results.author: F. Enders
results.takeaway: Spiders and insects with low Dyar's constant have advantages in hunting agility, suggesting convergent evolution resulting from the need for agility during hunting.

results.title: DEVELOPMENTAL TRAIT EVOLUTION IN TRILOBITES
results.author: G. Fusco
results.takeaway: Trilobite developmental traits were relatively labile during evolutionary history, with growth rates varying independently among species and conforming to Dyar's rule.

results.title: Comparative analysis of larval growth in Lepidoptera reveals instar‐level constraints
results.author: S. M. Kivelä

results.title: Effect of temperature on larval growth of Ecdyonurus dispar (Ephemeroptera: Heptageniidae) from two English lakes
results.author: U. Humpesch
results.takeaway: Temperature significantly affects larval growth of Ecdyonurus dispar in English lakes, with one generation per year achieved at 20°C.

results.title: An equation for geometric progression in Brooks-Dyar growth ratios
results.author: T. Hawes
results.takeaway: A simple exponential equation can provide a more realistic representation of growth increments and quantify the degree of geometric progression in development, replacing transformations and providing a more precise method for evaluating agreement with the Brooks-Dyar rule.

results.title: Decorrelation, Diversity, and Emergent Intelligence: The Isomorphism Between Social Insect Colonies and Ensemble Machine Learning
results.author: Ernest Fokou'e

results.title: Allometric growth in two species of Ectobius (Dictyoptera: Blattidae)
results.author: V. Brown
results.takeaway: Allometric growth in Ectobius species shows significant variations, with few structures growing by simple allometry, and the choice of reference dimension and statistical model affects growth data estimates.

results.title: The allometry of the central nervous system during the postembryonic development of the spider Eratigena atrica.
results.author: T. Napiórkowska
results.takeaway: In postembryonic development of Eratigena atrica spider, the central nervous system grows smaller than the body, with subesophageal ganglion and neuropil increasing in volume, while brain and cortex decrease.

results.title: Allometric growth in the Praying mantis Stagmatoptera biocellata
results.author: Ernesto Rodriguez
results.takeaway: Allometric growth patterns in the Praying mantis Stagmatoptera biocellata show adaptative value and support the hypothesis of anatomic relationships between parts involved in the mantis strike.

results.title: A multivariate analysis of postembryonic growth in two species of Ectobius (Dictyoptera: Blattidae)
results.author: R. G. Davies
results.takeaway: Postembryonic growth in Ectobius species shows four or five orthogonal patterns, with the major component being a set of independently definable allometric growth ratios.

results.title: Allometric growth of two species of Ephemeroptera from Neotropical mountains streams
results.author: Gabriel de Paula Paciencia
results.journal: Annales De Limnologie-international Journal of Limnology
results.takeaway: Positive allometric growth in the mesothorax and wing pad of Ephemeroptera species Massartella brieni and Thraulodes sp. is related to the transition from aquatic to adult stages of development.

results.title: The pace of growth: An allometric approach to comparative embryonic and post embryonic growth
results.author: W. Calder
results.takeaway: Allometric analysis offers a promising approach for understanding comparative growth dynamics between embryonic and post-embryonic stages, improving understanding of physiological time scales.

results.title: Allometric Changes Across Horseshoe Crab Moults Evidence Developmentally Controlled Ecological Shifts and Possible Exoskeletal Modularity
results.author: R. Bicknell
results.takeaway: Allometric changes in American horseshoe crabs reveal developmental ecological shifts and possible exoskeletal modularity, likely in response to increased predation pressures.

results.title: The allometry of the arcuate body in the postembryonic development of the giant house spider Eratigena atrica
results.author: T. Napiórkowska
results.takeaway: The arcuate body in Eratigena atrica spiders develops differently than in orb-weaver and wandering spiders, but in adult spiders, its proportion in brain volume remains similar regardless of behavior.

results.title: Allometric growth of limb and body proportions in crocodylians
results.author: M. Iijima
results.takeaway: Crocodylians show non-uniform growth patterns in limb architecture, with hindlimb length growing with negative allometry in non-gavialid species and femur circumference scaling negatively against humerus circumference in most species.

results.title: Quantitative developmental data in a phylogenetic framework.
results.author: N. Giannini
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Allometry in rodents has a strong functional and adaptive basis, with common ancestry explaining little variation and all variation being independent of body mass.

results.title:
Larval growth and allometry in the cabbage butterfly
 Pieris brassicae
 (Lepidoptera: Pieridae)
results.author: Arianna Springolo
results.takeaway: Larval growth in the cabbage butterfly Pieris brassicae shows ontogenetic allometry, with size at hatching significantly determining size at all subsequent stages, and developmental control becomes more effective at stage 3.

results.title: Ontogenetic drivers of morphological evolution in monitor lizards and allies (Squamata: Paleoanguimorpha), a clade with extreme body size disparity
results.author: Carlos J. Pavón-Vázquez
results.takeaway: Postnatal ontogeny in monitor lizards and allies is labile and adaptive, with heterochrony driving morphological divergence at shallow evolutionary scales and habitat use transitions influencing change.

results.title: Multi-scaling allometry in human development, mammalian morphology, and tree growth
results.author: Madhur Mangalam
results.takeaway: Multi-scale allometry reveals that growth strategies in various species vary based on their centile within the trait distribution, highlighting the inherent growth processes that underlie morphological diversity.

results.title: Traits controlling body size in copepods: separating general constraints from species-specific strategies
results.author: N. Banas
results.takeaway: Copepod growth rate is influenced by temperature, but not by temperature-corrected development rate, and the ratio of growth and development rates is a better predictor of adult size than either rate alone.

results.title: Growth Models of Complex Allometries in Holometabolous Insects
results.author: H. Nijhout
results.takeaway: Allomeries among adult holometabolous insects result from normal developmental processes during metamorphosis, with imaginal disks competing for nutrients, potentially playing a role in the evolution of body proportions in ants and other insects.

results.title: Size and shape: the developmental regulation of static allometry in insects
results.author: A. Shingleton
results.takeaway: Recent advances in growth regulation and endocrinology suggest that insulin/IGF signaling may play a central role in regulating organismal shape and size in insects.

results.title: Allometric scaling laws linking biomass and rooting depth vary across ontogeny and functional groups in tropical dry forest lianas and trees.
results.author: Chris M. Smith‐Martin
results.takeaway: Mature lianas follow the same allometric scaling laws as trees, but juveniles and mature individuals do not, and evergreen trees have the deepest roots, which are necessary for maintaining canopy during dry seasons.

results.title: Allometry and Biomechanics: Limb Bones in Adult Ungulates
results.author: T. McMahon
results.takeaway: Allometric equations can effectively describe growth and body size differences in adult ungulates, but their usefulness is limited by the lack of explanation for why some anatomical or physiological parameters grow at different rates of interest than others.

61.state: The search for a constant growth ratio per moult (Dyar's rule, 'Przibram's rule') continues, and modern measurements find it only approximate: ratios stay within a limited range but vary by body part and species, from waterstriders to Manduca to leafhoppers. The quantitative-growth program is alive, but the single universal law has become a family of allometries.

facets.q: Can a regenerated insect antenna develop into a leg (antenna-to-leg homeosis), and which Hox genes control it?
How does developmental stage affect the form of regenerated arthropod appendages?

results.title: Control of antennal versus leg development in Drosophila
results.author: F. Casares
results.takeaway: In Drosophila, Antennapedia promotes leg development by repressing homothorax and nuclear Exd, resulting in distinct antennal and leg structures.

results.title: Comparative analysis of leg and antenna development in wild-type and homeotic Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: M. Cummins
results.takeaway: The developmental programs of leg and antenna in Drosophila are partially conserved, but regulatory relationships and interactions between genes differ, resulting in positional specificity of homeotic transformations.

results.title: The Hox gene Antennapedia is essential for wing development in insects.
results.author: Chunyan Fang
results.takeaway: Insect wing development is influenced by the Hox gene Antennapedia, leading to reduced and malformed adult wings in Bombyx, Drosophila, and Tribolium.

results.title: Developmental biology: Pulling the fly's leg
results.author: G. Morata
results.takeaway: Antp gene does not specify leg development, but prevents the functioning of homothoraxgene, which is responsible for antenna development in fruit flies.

results.title: The mouse Hox-1.3 gene is functionally equivalent to the Drosophila Sex combs reduced gene.
results.author: J. Zhao
results.takeaway: Mouse Hox-1.3 is functionally equivalent to the Drosophila Sex combs reduced gene and can participate in the regulatory hierarchy of insect organogenesis.

results.title: Drosophila fushi tarazu. a gene on the border of homeotic function.
results.author: U. Löhr
results.takeaway: Drosophila ftz evolved from an ancestral homeotic gene to exclusively function in segmentation pathways, mainly due to changes in expression regulation and protein-coding region alterations.

results.title: Role of the Hox Genes, Sex combs reduced, Fushi tarazu and Antennapedia, in Leg Development of the Spider Mite Tetranychus urticae
results.author: Xiang Luo
results.takeaway: Three Hox genes,,, and, are required for normal leg development in spider mites, and their loss leads to abnormalities in leg development.

results.title: Functional analyses of tiptop and Antennapedia in the embryonic development of Oncopeltus fasciatus suggests an evolutionary pathway from ground state to insect legs
results.author: S. Herke
results.takeaway: Tiptop plays a crucial role in defining leg identity in Oncopeltus fasciatus, suggesting a significant difference in leg specification mechanisms between the two species and a potential evolutionary pathway for insect legs.

results.title: Characterizing Hox genes in mayflies (Ephemeroptera), with Hexagenia limbata as a new mayfly model
results.author: C. J. Gonzalez
results.takeaway: Mayfly Hox genes are conserved with other insects, and may play a role in gill development in early instar nymphs.

results.title: A common mechanism for antenna-to-Leg transformation in Drosophila: suppression of homothorax transcription by four HOM-C genes.
results.author: Liqun Yao
results.takeaway: The Scr, Antp, Ubx, and abd-A HOM-C genes all suppress homothorax transcription in the antenna disc, causing antenna-to-leg transformations in Drosophila.

results.title: The YPWM motif links Antennapedia to the basal transcriptional machinery
results.author: Frédéric Prince
results.takeaway: The YPWM motif in Antennapedia is essential for eye-to-wing transformation in adult Drosophila, with BIP2 playing a key role in linking Antennapedia to the basal transcriptional machinery.

results.title: A homoeotic mutation transforming leg to antenna in Drosophila
results.author: G. Struhl
results.takeaway: Homoeotic mutations in the Antp locus can transform the second leg into an antenna in Drosophila, promoting an atavistic condition.

results.title: Divergent role of the Hox gene Antennapedia in spiders is responsible for the convergent evolution of abdominal limb repression
results.author: S. Khadjeh
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: The Hox gene Antennapedia in spiders plays a unique role in abdominal leg repression, contributing to the convergent evolution of abdominal leg repression in arthropods.

results.title: The evolving role of Hox genes in arthropods.
results.author: M. Akam
results.takeaway: Hox genes in arthropods have evolved faster than homeotic genes, with changes in regulation playing a significant role in segment diversification and their role in early development.

results.title: Hox and HOM: homologous gene clusters in insects and vertebrates.
results.author: M. Akam
results.takeaway: Insects and vertebrates share a common ancestor, suggesting that both groups inherited a conserved molecular representation of front, mid-dle, and back from a common ancestor.

results.title: Brief communication. An Antennapedia-like mutation in Nasonia vitripennis
results.author: J. Werren
results.takeaway: Antennapedia-like mutations in Nasonia vitripennis may provide insights into the evolution of developmental patterns in invertebrates and vertebrates.

results.title: Evolution of the insect Hox gene cluster: Comparative analysis across 243 species
results.author: P. Mulhair
results.takeaway: The insect Hox gene cluster shows evolutionary conservation but not absolute conservation, with differences in gene order, gene number, and genomic organization across 243 species.

results.title: Functional similarity in appendage specification by the Ultrabithorax and abdominal‐A Drosophila HOX genes.
results.author: F. Casares
results.takeaway: Misexpression of Ultrabithorax, abdominal-A, and Abdominal-B HOX genes in fruitflies leads to similar transformations in appendage development, suggesting that HOX genes may have been initially used for body axis specification.

results.title: Control of tissue morphogenesis by the HOX gene Ultrabithorax
results.author: María-del-Carmen Díaz-de-la-Loza
results.takeaway: The HOX gene Ultrabithorax controls tissue morphogenesis by repressing expression of two genes in the haltere, and inducing expression of another gene, which prevents basal extracellular matrix remodelling.

results.title: The role of Hox genes in shaping embryonic external morphology of the primitive insect Thermobia domestica (Zygentoma)
results.author: Hu-Na Lu
results.takeaway: Hox genes play crucial roles in shaping embryonic external morphology and survival in primitive ametabolous insects, such as Thermobia domestica.

results.title: The extent of regeneration is impacted by the stage of amputation in ladybird beetles: a case study in Cheilomenes sexmaculata
results.author: Hera Alam
results.takeaway: The extent of regeneration in ladybird beetles is stage-specific, with early larval stages showing higher regenerative potency and causing longer development periods, but no significant difference in adult body weight.

results.title: Developmental coupling of larval and adult stages in a complex life cycle: insights from limb regeneration in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum
results.author: Alison K Lee
results.takeaway: Larval and adult limb patterning in the red flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum, show continuity and may be developmentally coupled, suggesting the evolution of imaginal discs may have removed developmental constraints between larval and adult phenotypes.

results.title: Explosive regeneration and anamorphic development of legs in the house centipede Scutigera coleoptrata
results.author: Iulia Barutia
results.takeaway: House centipedes can regenerate their long legs within one molting cycle, making them unique among land arthropods and rare in marine taxa.

results.title: Developmental onset of planarian whole-body regeneration depends on axis reset
results.author: C. Booth

results.title: Developmental stage dependent effects of posterior and germline regeneration on sexual maturation in Platynereis dumerilii
results.author: Bria Metzger
results.takeaway: Developmentally younger Platynereis dumerilii worms have higher regeneration success and sexual maturation success than older worms, but older amputees grow faster and mature earlier.

results.title: Appendage regeneration in anamniotes utilizes genes active during larval‐metamorphic stages that have been lost or altered in amniotes: The case for studying lizard tail regeneration
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration in anamniotes is influenced by specific evolutionary histories, with aquatic vertebrates having regenerative ability due to metamorphic stages, while terrestrial vertebrates lack regeneration.

results.title: Keeping at arm’s length during regeneration
results.author: Valerie A. Tornini
results.takeaway: Adult amphibians and fish can regenerate lost appendages, demonstrating remarkable developmental patterning, by maintaining positional identity and determining tissue completion.

results.title: The Papilla Stage as a Critical Molecular Transition: Antp and Sex-Regulatory Network Orchestrate Cheliped Regeneration in Eriocheir sinensis
results.author: Benzhen Li
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI

results.title: Appendage loss and regeneration in arthropods: a comparative view
results.author: Diego Maruzzo
results.takeaway: Arthropods can lose and regenerate appendages, with autotomy being limited to isopods, decapods, and some basal pterygotes, and regeneration widespread across all major groups.

results.title: Caudal fin shape imprinted during late zebrafish embryogenesis is re-patterned by the Sonic hedgehog pathway
results.author: Eric Surette
results.takeaway: Sonic hedgehog a (shha) overexpression during late embryonic development can alter caudal fin shape, but this effect is limited to a critical window before differentiation of skeletogenic cells.

results.title: Perspective: Appendage regeneration in amphibians and some reptiles derived from specific evolutionary histories.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Amphibian regeneration and lizard regeneration are derived from specific evolutionary histories, and regenerative medicine could potentially induce limb regeneration in humans by creating soft, hyaluronate-rich blastemas.

results.title: Repeated removal of developing limb buds permanently reduces appendage size in the highly-regenerative axolotl.
results.author: D. M. Bryant
results.takeaway: Repeated removal of developing limb buds in axolotls permanently reduces appendage size, with this altered morphology preserved even after amputation and regeneration.

results.title: The influence of fundamental traits on mechanisms controlling appendage regeneration
results.author: A. Seifert
results.takeaway: Fundamental traits like body size, aging, life stage, and growth pattern may constrain appendage regeneration in animals, potentially impacting regenerative medicine.

results.title: Tokay gecko tail regeneration involves temporally collinear expression of HOXC genes and early expression of satellite cell markers
results.author: L. Nurhidayat
results.takeaway: Tokay gecko tail regeneration involves activation of resident stem cells guided by pre-existing positional information, unlike embryonic tail development.

results.title: Changes in the Inflammatory Response to Injury and Its Resolution during the Loss of Regenerative Capacity in Developing Xenopus Limbs
results.author: A. Mescher
results.takeaway: In Xenopus hindlimbs, postamputation inflammation and resolution changes during development, potentially interfering with blastema patterning and affecting cellular reprogramming.

results.title: Effect of larval thermal conditions on limb regeneration in a ladybird beetle, Cheilomenes sexmaculata.
results.author: Shriza Rai
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part A, Ecological and integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Larval thermal conditions affect limb regeneration in ladybird beetles, with higher temperatures accelerating recovery time after limb amputation.

results.title: Appendage regeneration is context dependent at the cellular level
results.author: Can Aztekin
results.takeaway: Appendage regeneration is context-dependent at the cellular level, but requires signaling centers, stem/progenitor cell types, and a regeneration-permissive environment for cross-species and paradigm comparisons.

results.title: Intercalary regeneration in imaginal wing disk of Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: J. Haynie
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal disk cells can undergo pattern regulation through regeneration and duplication when parts are removed, similar to how amphibian and cockroach limbs can regenerate.

results.title: Regeneration, Regengrow and Tissue Repair in Animals: Evolution Indicates That No Regeneration Occurs in Terrestrial Environments but Only Recovery Healing
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: In terrestrial animals, regeneration is limited to aquatic or amphibious species due to changes in genomes and environmental conditions, rather than a complete loss of regeneration.

results.title: Morphological, Molecular, and Hormonal Basis of Limb Regeneration across Pancrustacea.
results.author: Sunetra Das
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in insects and crustaceans involves conserved pathways and transcription factors, with hormone signaling playing a crucial role in blastemal proliferation.

66.state: The homeotic antenna-to-leg switch Przibram observed is now understood molecularly: in Drosophila the Hox gene Antennapedia imposes leg identity by repressing homothorax/extradenticle, and antenna and leg programs are partly shared. Regenerate form still depends on stage, but the genetic control is mapped — without citing the 1917 result.

facets.q: Does a single allometric growth law describe postembryonic growth across different praying mantis species?
How do body size and eye ommatidia number scale across mantis development?

results.title: Allometric growth in the Praying mantis Stagmatoptera biocellata
results.author: Ernesto Rodriguez
results.takeaway: Allometric growth patterns in the Praying mantis Stagmatoptera biocellata show adaptative value and support the hypothesis of anatomic relationships between parts involved in the mantis strike.

results.title: Postembryonic development of the predatory strike mechanics in a praying mantis (Insecta: Mantodea)
results.author: Thies H. Büscher
results.takeaway: Praying mantises' predatory strike mechanics change with body weight, indicating varying nutritional demands across stages and sexes.

results.title: Allometric growth in two species of Ectobius (Dictyoptera: Blattidae)
results.author: V. Brown
results.takeaway: Allometric growth in Ectobius species shows significant variations, with few structures growing by simple allometry, and the choice of reference dimension and statistical model affects growth data estimates.

results.title: Manual of praying mantis morphology, nomenclature, and practices (Insecta, Mantodea)
results.author: Sydney K. Brannoch
results.takeaway: This study proposes new standards for praying mantis morphology, standardized foreleg femoral and tibial spine presentation, and provides guidelines for proper specimen preservation and research methods.

results.title: Allometry of Ingestion Among Habitat Mimicking Praying Mantises
results.author: C. Oufiero
results.takeaway: Large praying mantises can ingest more resources than needed based on their metabolic rate, potentially impacting their role in the ecosystem.

results.title: A multivariate analysis of postembryonic growth in two species of Ectobius (Dictyoptera: Blattidae)
results.author: R. G. Davies
results.takeaway: Postembryonic growth in Ectobius species shows four or five orthogonal patterns, with the major component being a set of independently definable allometric growth ratios.

results.title: Allometric growth in reef-building corals
results.author: M. Dornelas
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Coral growth rates can be best predicted from colony size and morphology, rather than species, simplifying the task of projecting community responses to disturbance and climate change.

results.title: Comparative Mitogenomic Analyses of Praying Mantises (Dictyoptera, Mantodea): Origin and Evolution of Unusual Intergenic Gaps
results.author: Hong-li Zhang
results.takeaway: Praying mantises have unique intergenic gaps introduced through gene rearrangement and duplication, suggesting gene rearrangement and duplication have shaped their evolution.

results.title: Complete mitochondrial genomes of four species of praying mantises (Dictyoptera, Mantidae) with ribosomal second structure, evolutionary and phylogenetic analyses
results.author: Yan Shi
results.takeaway: The complete mitochondrial genomes of four praying mantises support the taxonomic system of Schwarz and Roy, with Metallyticus sp. being sister to the remaining mantises.

results.title: Allometric scaling laws linking biomass and rooting depth vary across ontogeny and functional groups in tropical dry forest lianas and trees.
results.author: Chris M. Smith‐Martin
results.takeaway: Mature lianas follow the same allometric scaling laws as trees, but juveniles and mature individuals do not, and evergreen trees have the deepest roots, which are necessary for maintaining canopy during dry seasons.

results.title: Reconsidering mandibular and metabolic allometries in males of the stag beetle Cyclommatus mniszechi (Coleoptera, Lucanidae)
results.author: G. C. Packard
results.takeaway: The study of mandibular and metabolic allometries in male stag beetle Cyclommatus mniszechi (Coleoptera, Lucanidae) should have examined untransformed data, as it may have revealed more complex patterns of variation.

results.title: Allometric Scaling of Metabolism, Growth, and Activity in Whole Colonies of the Seed‐Harvester Ant Pogonomyrmex californicus
results.author: James S. Waters
results.takeaway: Whole colonies of the seed-harvester ant Pogonomyrmex californicus exhibit negative allometric scaling of metabolic rate with colony mass, suggesting that behavioral organization within colonies significantly influences colony-level metabolism and growth trajectories.

results.title: EVOLUTION OF STATIC ALLOMETRIES: ADAPTIVE CHANGE IN ALLOMETRIC SLOPES OF EYE SPAN IN STALK‐EYED FLIES
results.author: K. Voje
results.takeaway: Static allometry may constrain eye-span adaptation at shorter time scales, but adaptive evolution in stalk-eyed flies shows slow tracking of "optima" based on sexual dimorphism and relative male trait size.

results.title: Allometry and phylogenetic divergence: Correspondence or incongruence?
results.author: Andrea Cardini
results.takeaway: Allometric changes in craniofacial proportions of Old World monkeys show no clear correlation with phylogenetic divergence, but large and representative samples are crucial for accurate morphometric research.

results.title: Re‐evolution of a morphological precursor of crypsis investment in the newly revised horned praying mantises (Insecta, Mantodea, Vatinae)
results.author: Gavin J. Svenson
results.takeaway: The revised horned praying mantises reveal a distinct pattern of correlated evolution of morphological characters linked to crypsis, with posteroventral lobes on thoracic femora being the precursors to multiple positive rate shifts in cryptic feature accumulation.

results.title: ALLOMETRIC CONSTRAINTS AND THE EVOLUTION OF ALLOMETRY
results.author: K. Voje
results.takeaway: Allometry may constrain morphological trait evolution across taxa, with limited evidence for microevolutionary changes in static slopes.

results.title:
Self‐thinning and size‐dependent flowering of the grass
 Spartina alterniflora
 across space and time
results.author: Wenwen Liu
results.takeaway: Saltmarsh grass Spartina alterniflora plants adjust their size and reproductive effort in response to selection pressures, with high density plants being smaller and reproducing at a smaller size than low density plants.

results.title: The nature of allometry in an exaggerated trait: The postocular flange in Platyneuromus Weele (Insecta: Megaloptera)
results.author: A. Ramírez-Ponce
results.takeaway: Sexual selection may explain the parallel or divergent growth of the postocular flange in Platyneuromus dobsonflies, with a female-biased sexual dimorphism.

results.title: Nutrient‐dependent allometric plasticity in a male‐diphenic mite
results.author: Flor T. Rhebergen
results.takeaway: Food-dependent allometric plasticity in male bulb mites allows populations to track nutritional conditions, potentially facilitating rapid evolution of allometric scaling relationships.

results.title: Patterns of morphological evolution in the raptorial appendages of praying mantises
results.author: Alejandro Izquierdo-López
results.takeaway: The evolution of raptorial appendages in praying mantises suggests a potential transitional link between walking and raptorial morphotypes, with new fossil records expanding the mantodean fossil record.

results.title: Multi-scale relationships between numbers and size in the evolution of arthropod body features.
results.author: A. Minelli
results.takeaway: The evolution of arthropod body features involves a complex interplay of selective forces and developmental constraints, with diverse size/number relationships allowing for diverse generalizations.

results.title: Sex, Head Size, and Male Mate Location Behavior Affect Allometric Scaling of the Eyes in Centris pallida (Hymenoptera: Apidae) Bees
results.author: M. Barrett

results.title: A Novel Form of Stereo Vision in the Praying Mantis.
results.author: V. Nityananda
results.takeaway: Praying mantis use a different computational algorithm for estimating distance to moving targets, revealing distance even in complex visual scenes with camouflaged targets.

results.title: Developmental Patterns in Stagmomantis limbata (Mantodea: Mantidae): Variation in Instar Number, Growth, and Body Size
results.author: M. R. Maxwell
results.takeaway: Female praying mantids with a seven-instar developmental pattern reach adulthood earlier and have larger body size than those with a six-instar pattern.

results.title: Reconstruction of the ancient binocular vision: advanced stereopsis and predatory adaptation in a Cretaceous mantis
results.author: Ryo Taniguchi
results.takeaway: An extinct mantis (Ambermantis wozniaki) in Cretaceous New Jersey amber developed an advanced visual system and advanced prey-recognition system, suggesting it was a highly adaptive visual predator for 90 million years.

results.title: Shaping an optical dome: The size and shape of the insect compound eye.
results.author: F. Pichaud
results.takeaway: Insect compound eyes come in a wide variety of shapes and sizes, adapted to specific ecosystems, with the eye's resolution and visual field determined by the number and size of facet lenses and precise control of the inter facet-lens angle.

results.title: The evolution and development of eye size in flies
results.author: F. Casares
results.takeaway: Understanding the genetic and developmental mechanisms behind natural variations in fly eye size could help us better understand adaptations in fly vision and organ size regulation.

results.title: Small or far away? Size and distance perception in the praying mantis
results.author: V. Nityananda
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Praying mantises do not use their stereo vision to estimate object size, but other selective advantages have driven the evolution of this vision.

results.title: Optical scaling in conspecific Cataglyphis ants
results.author: Christoph P. E. Zollikofer
results.takeaway: Cataglyphis ants maintain a constant visual field despite head size variation, with the number of ommatidia and interommatidial angles maintaining the same pattern in both small and large workers.

results.title: Heterochrony in orthodenticle expression is associated with ommatidial size variation between Drosophila species
results.author: Montserrat Torres-Oliva
results.takeaway: Differential timing of orthodenticle expression contributes to natural variation in ommatidia size between Drosophila species, providing insights into the regulation and evolution of compound eye size in insects.

results.title: Postembryonic Eye Growth in the Seashore Isopod Ligia exotica (Crustacea, Isopoda)
results.author: E. Keskinen
results.takeaway: Larger individuals of Ligia exotica have increased sensitivity in single ommatidia and larger angular visual fields, which aid in night vision and locating cliffs in seawater.

results.title: Dissecting intraspecific variation in body size related traits in Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: A. Chtioui
results.takeaway: This study identifies multiple genes involved in compound eye size regulation in Drosophila melanogaster, providing insights into the development and evolution of complex morphological traits.

results.title: Scaling and development of elastic mechanisms: the tiny strikes of larval mantis shrimp.
results.author: J. Harrison
results.takeaway: Larval mantis shrimp use a latch-mediated spring actuation mechanism to achieve high strike accelerations and speeds, even at their smaller size compared to adult mantis shrimp.

results.title: The genomes of 5 mantises provide insights into sex chromosome evolution and Mantodea phylogeny clarification
results.author: Hangwei Liu
results.takeaway: The high-quality genome assemblies of 5 mantis species provide valuable insights into sex chromosome evolution and help resolve phylogenic controversies in the Deroplatys genus.

results.title: Host-trailing satellite flight behaviour is associated with greater investment in peripheral visual sensory system in miltogrammine flies
results.author: C. Polidori
results.takeaway: Satellite flight behavior in miltogrammine flies is associated with improved visual acuity and stability, as well as reduced antennal length, suggesting an inverse resource allocation between eyes and antennae in this fly subfamily.

results.title: Evolutionary genomics of camouflage innovation in the orchid mantis
results.author: Guang-Ming Huang
results.takeaway: The orchid mantis' unique camouflage is influenced by the Scarlet gene, Wnt signaling, and the Trypsin gene family, providing insights into phenotypic innovation and adaptation.

results.title: Vision-Linked Traits Associated With Antenna Size and Foraging Ecology Across Ants
results.author: Chloe Jelley
results.takeaway: Ants' eye size and ommatidia density are linked to their foraging niche and trophic level, suggesting a resource investment trade-off between visual and olfactory acuity.

results.title: A computational model of stereoscopic prey capture in praying mantises
results.author: James O’Keeffe
results.takeaway: Praying mantises exhibit stereoscopic sensitivity in predatory strikes, with a simple model capturing their broad tuning to size and narrow tuning to stereoscopic disparity.

4.state: Comparative allometric growth in mantises is active: recent studies of Stagmatoptera, Ectobius and others find adaptive but species-variable scaling and propose standardized mantis morphometrics. Przibram's aim of a growth curve spanning mantis species is exactly the modern comparative-scaling question.

facets.q: Does maternal age affect offspring body size in insects?
What are the transgenerational effects of parental age on offspring size and fitness?

results.title: The influence of maternal age and mating frequency on egg size and offspring performance in Callosobruchus maculatus (Coleoptera: Bruchidae)
results.author: C. Fox
results.takeaway: Maternal age influences egg size and offspring performance in Callosobruchus maculatus, with multiple mating and adult feeding increasing egg size, but multiple mating has no detectable impact on offspring development time or body size.

results.title: Maternal body size and age govern reproduction and offspring phenotype in the zig-zag ladybird beetle (Menochilus sexmaculatus)
results.author: Shashwat Singh
results.takeaway: Larger zig-zag ladybird beetle mothers lay more eggs per day, and their offspring develop rapidly, with larger females showing greater ability to adjust life-history traits.

results.title: Triparental ageing in a laboratory population of an insect with maternal care
results.author: H. Cope
results.takeaway: Parental age interactions, involving both maternal and paternal ages, significantly influence offspring outcomes in burying beetle species.

results.title: Variation in parasitoid adult size is related to host egg size, maternal state and developmental time
results.author: Mei Qi
results.takeaway: Anastatus disparis' body size is influenced by host egg size, maternal size, and developmental time, with larger females and shorter offspring development times.

results.title: Maternal rearing condition and age affect progeny fitness in the parasitoid wasp Lysiphlebus fabarum
results.author: P. Najafpour
results.takeaway: Progeny of Lysiphlebus fabarum parasitoid wasps with larger egg size and younger mothers have longer developmental time, with maternal body size being a stronger factor than age.

results.title: Effects of Maternal Age and Egg Weight on Developmental Time and Body Weight of Offspring of Podisus maculiventris (Heteroptera: Pentatomidae)
results.author: J. Mohaghegh
results.journal: Annals of The Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: Maternal age and egg weight significantly impact the developmental time and body weight of Podisus maculiventris offspring, with offspring from large eggs and young females being heavier than those from small or old eggs.

results.title: Maternal effects and their consequences for offspring fitness in the Yellow Dung Fly
results.author: P. Jann
results.takeaway: Maternal adult feeding and egg size are more important than size for increasing reproductive output in the Yellow Dung Fly, Scathophaga stercoraria, with offspring from nutritionally stressed mothers experiencing reduced fitness.

results.title: Maternal size and age affect offspring sex ratio in the solitary egg parasitoid Anaphes nitens
results.author: S. Santolamazza‐Carbone
results.takeaway: Maternal body size significantly influences offspring sex ratio in the solitary egg parasitoid Anaphes nitens, with larger mothers producing more female offspring and starved females producing more females.

results.title: Maternal aging increases offspring adult body size via transmission of donut-shaped mitochondria
results.author: Runshuai Zhang
results.takeaway: Maternal aging increases offspring adult body size by transmitting donut-shaped mitochondria, which are rejuvenated and activate TGF signaling to increase offspring size in humans, Drosophila, and Caenorhabditis elegans.

results.title: Biparental age effects in the burying beetle Nicrophorus vespilloides
results.author: H. Cope
results.takeaway: Paternal and maternal age effects in the burying beetle Nicrophorus vespilloides show no clear evidence of senescence-related effects on egg size, larval weight, or larval survival, or on foster mother outcomes.

results.title: Cryptic maternal effects in Hippodamia convergens vary with maternal age and body size
results.author: G. Vargas
results.takeaway: Maternal signals in ladybird beetles can change through adult life, influencing progeny development and potentially optimizing their fitness for ephemeral aphid outbreaks.

results.title: The contribution of maternal and paternal body size to offspring early adulthood life histories in a parasitoid wasp, Lysiphlebus fabarum
results.author: A. Rasekh
results.takeaway: Large mothers produce larger offspring with quicker developmental times, while paternal body size influences egg load, suggesting direct paternal effects in Lysiphlebus fabarum parasitoid wasps.

results.title: Older mothers produce smaller eggs without compromising offspring quality: a study of a thelytokous mite predator (Acari: Phytoseiidae).
results.author: Keshi Zhang
results.takeaway: Older mothers produce smaller eggs in phytoseiid predatory mites, but their offspring's performance remains unaffected, suggesting possible compensatory mechanisms or alternative maternal provisioning strategies.

results.title: How maternal investment varies with environmental factors and the age and physiological state of wild tsetse Glossina pallidipes and Glossina morsitans morsitans
results.author: J. Hargrove
results.takeaway: Maternal investment in wild tsetse flies varies with environmental factors, offspring size, and maternal age, with larger offspring produced by mothers in better condition and cooler temperatures.

results.title: Disentangling Pre- and Postnatal Maternal Age Effects on Offspring Performance in an Insect with Elaborate Maternal Care
results.author: E. Ivimey-Cook
results.takeaway: Pre- and postnatal maternal age in burying beetles do not affect offspring longevity or larval weight at hatching, and maternal age has no effect on postcare maternal outcomes.

results.title: Host size overrides maternal effects on the development of a secondary hyperparasitoid wasp
results.author: Xianhui Shi
results.takeaway: Host quality is mostly pre-determined, irrespective of maternal size, and the development of a secondary hyperparasitoid wasp is strongly correlated with host size.

results.title: Maternal Age Effects on Embryo Mortality and Juvenile Development of Offspring in the Honey Bee (Hymenoptera: Apidae)
results.author: H. Al-Lawati
results.takeaway: Maternal age significantly increases embryo mortality in honey bees, with older mothers' offspring being more sensitive to stress and having smaller larval sizes.

results.title: Effects of maternal age on reproductive traits and fitness components of the offspring in the bruchid beetle, Callosobruchus chinensis (Coleoptera: Bruchidae)
results.author: Shin‐ichi Yanagi
results.takeaway: Maternal age in Callosobruchus chinensis beetle leads to a decrease in egg size and reproductive effort, which can be explained by the resource depletion hypothesis.

results.title: What happens to offspring when parents are inbred, old or had a poor start in life? Evidence for sex‐specific parental effects
results.author: Regina Vega‐Trejo
results.takeaway: Older mothers produce smaller offspring, while older fathers produce larger genitalia, and parental effects can vary with both parent and offspring sex, changing over time, and sometimes interacting between different traits.

results.title: The relationship between maternal phenotype and offspring quality: do older mothers really produce the best offspring?
results.author: D. Marshall
results.takeaway: Older mothers may produce larger offspring due to changes in their phenotype, but there is little evidence that older mothers produce offspring with higher per capita fitness than younger mothers.

results.title: Beneficial cumulative effects of old parental age on offspring fitness
results.author: Laura. M. Travers
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Old parental age leads to fitter offspring reaching their reproductive peak earlier, with cumulative benefits increasing over six generations, contradicting the theory of a cumulative detrimental 'ageing factor' transfer.

results.title: Transgenerational effects of maternal and grandmaternal age on offspring viability and performance in Drosophila melanogaster.
results.author: M. C. Bloch Qazi
results.takeaway: Maternal age has complex and multigenerational consequences on offspring fitness and traits in Drosophila melanogaster, with older females producing smaller eggs and larger offspring.

results.title: Intergenerational Transfer of Ageing: Parental Age and Offspring Lifespan.
results.author: P. Monaghan
results.takeaway: The 'Lansing effect' negatively affects offspring lifespan, with potential implications for offspring fitness across multiple generations.

results.title: Grandmother Age Effects via Sperm Telomere Length and Egg Telomerase Transcripts
results.author: Sin‐Yeon Kim
results.takeaway: Maternal age can transmit through offspring traits, affecting grandoffspring telomere length and viability, shaping the evolution of life histories.

results.title: The effect of parental age on the quantity and quality of offspring in Syngnathus typhle, a species with male pregnancy
results.author: F. A. Pappert
results.takeaway: Older parents in the broad-nosed pipefish Syngnathus typhle produce more and larger offspring, with paternal lineage significantly influencing gene expression patterns in offspring.

results.title: A multigenerational effect of parental age on offspring size but not fitness in common duckweed (Lemna minor)
results.author: Patrick Barks
results.takeaway: Parental age effects on offspring size are multigenerational in Lemna minor, but not on offspring fitness, supporting the single-generation assumption in evolutionary theory.

results.title: Reduced fitness in progeny from old parents in a natural population
results.author: J. Schroeder
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Older parents negatively impact the lifetime fitness of their offspring, with sex-specific differences observed in wild house sparrows.

results.title: Effects of parental age at conception on offspring life history trajectories in a long-lived bird.
results.author: Héloïse Moullec
results.journal: Evolution; international journal of organic evolution
results.takeaway: Parental age at conception impacts offspring traits, with older mothers and fathers leading to larger offspring and less parasite infestation before fledging, but older fathers having shorter lifespans.

results.title: Sex‐dependent effects of parental age on offspring fitness in a cooperatively breeding bird
results.author: A. Sparks
