results.takeaway: Parental age has sex-dependent effects on offspring fitness, with maternal age negatively impacting female offspring life span and lifetime reproductive success, while paternal age has no effect on female offspring life span or male offspring life span.

results.title: Parental age effects on offspring fitness in a wild population of a short-lived reptile.
results.author: C. Crain
results.takeaway: Parental age has no overall negative effects on offspring fitness in a wild population of brown anoles, with no significant differences between sons and daughters.

results.title: Maternal diet and age alter direct and indirect relationships between life‐history traits across multiple generations
results.author: Jared M. Goos
results.takeaway: Maternal age and diet interact to influence offspring growth and size at maturation, with transgenerational effects persisting across multiple generations and varying between successive generations.

results.title: Do the ages of parents or helpers affect offspring fitness in a cooperatively breeding bird?
results.author: Eve B. Cooper
results.takeaway: The ages of parents and helpers in cooperatively breeding birds impact offspring fitness, with helper ages having stronger effects than parental ages on offspring performance.

results.title: Offspring of older parents are smaller—but no less bilaterally symmetrical—than offspring of younger parents in the aquatic plant Lemna turionifera
results.author: Eric J Ankutowicz
results.takeaway: Offspring of older parents in Lemna turionifera aquatic plants are smaller but not less bilaterally symmetrical than those of younger parents.

results.title: Transgenerational Effects of Maternal Age on Offspring Fitness in Crickets
results.author: Jacob D. Wilson
results.takeaway: Maternal age has varying effects on offspring fitness in Pacific field crickets, with some effects lasting a single generation or several generations.

results.title: Sex‐specific pathways of parental age effects on offspring lifetime reproductive success in a long‐lived seabird
results.author: S. Bouwhuis
results.takeaway: Older parents in the common tern species, the common tern Sterna hirundo, reduce offspring lifetime reproductive success by affecting both offspring's reproductive success and life span.

results.title: Parental early life environments drive transgenerational plasticity of offspring metabolism in a freshwater fish (Danio rerio)
results.author: M. Massey
results.takeaway: Early life exposure to fluctuating temperatures significantly reduces offspring metabolic rates at stressful temperatures, potentially enhancing body sizes under stressful conditions.

results.title: A family history: effects of parental age on offspring life-history traits in bighorn sheep
results.author: E. Marchand
results.takeaway: Maternal age has a significant negative impact on offspring longevity and female lifetime reproductive success in bighorn sheep, with offspring born to 5-6 year old mothers living 2 years longer and weaning 1.5 lambs more than siblings born to 12-13 year old mothers.

results.title: Parental ecological history can differentially modulate parental age effects on offspring physiological traits in Drosophila
results.author: Juliano Morimoto
results.takeaway: Parental ecological history, including developmental and adult social environments, can modulate the effects of parental age on offspring traits, with a potential father-to-daughter effect.

results.title: Transgenerational maternal age effects in nature: Lessons learnt from Asian elephants.
results.author: Foteini Spagopoulou
results.takeaway: Maternal age at birth shapes offspring life-history trajectory in long-lived Asian elephants, with older offspring having lower survival but higher body condition and reproductive success, lasting across generations.

results.title: The effects of parental age difference on the offspring sex and fitness of European blackbirds
results.author: M. Cholewa
results.takeaway: Parental age significantly influences offspring sex and reproductive success in European blackbirds, with younger females bonded with older mates producing more sons and older females mated with younger males producing more daughters.

139.state: Parental-age effects on offspring size are a busy field, and modern insect work often finds the opposite of this paper's negative result: maternal and paternal age shape egg size, offspring size and performance in beetles, ladybirds and parasitoid wasps. The question is wide open, and the clean null is rarely revisited.

facets.q: Can adult holometabolous insects regenerate amputated wings, and what molecular pathways drive insect appendage regeneration?
Do imaginal disc and wing regeneration occur in beetles and other insects?

results.title: Physiological and molecular mechanisms of insect appendage regeneration
results.author: Jiru Zhong
results.takeaway: Insect appendage regeneration is influenced by physiological conditions and internal molecular mechanisms, with research progress focusing on the role of hormones and cytokines in this process.

results.title: The Ras/MAPK pathway is required for regenerative growth of wing discs in the black cutworm Agrotis ypsilon.
results.author: Yuanyuan Xu
results.takeaway: The Ras/MAPK pathway plays a crucial role in regenerative growth of wing discs in black cutworm Agrotis ypsilon, providing molecular clues to cell proliferation regulation during regeneration in lepidopteran insects.

results.title: Gill regeneration in the mayfly Cloeon uncovers new molecular pathways in insect regeneration
results.author: C. Martin-Blanco
results.takeaway: The mayfly Cloeon can regenerate lost abdominal gills faster than normal development, revealing new molecular pathways in insect regeneration.

results.title: The Molecular and Cellular Choreography of Appendage Regeneration.
results.author: E. Tanaka
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration reveals the molecular events that integrate growth control, cell fate programming, and positional information, providing insights for engineers to recreate complex multi-tissue structures.

results.title: A single WNT enhancer drives specification and regeneration of the Drosophila wing
results.author: Elena Gracia-Latorre
results.takeaway: A single WNT enhancer in the wingless gene drives wing specification and regeneration in Drosophila, ensuring robust development and regenerative capacity even under stress conditions.

results.title: Appendage Regeneration in Vertebrates: What Makes This Possible?
results.author: Valentina Daponte
results.takeaway: Understanding the similarities and differences in appendage regeneration in lizards, salamanders, and zebrafish can guide effective regenerative strategies in mammals.

results.title: The role of canonical Wnt signaling in leg regeneration and metamorphosis in the red flour beetle Tribolium castaneum.
results.author: Mita V. Shah
results.takeaway: Canonical Wnt signaling is crucial for proper appendage development and limb regeneration in the red flour beetle Tribolium castaneum, making it an excellent model for studying regeneration in holometabolous insects.

results.title: Molecular aspects of regeneration in insects.
results.author: Shivali Pandita
results.takeaway: Insects exhibit comprehensive regeneration abilities, influenced by signaling molecules and epigenetic regulation, which can advance regenerative medicine and synthetic biology.

results.title: Limb regeneration is blastema dependent in a ladybird beetle, Cheilomenes sexmaculata (Fabricius)
results.author: S. Rawat
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in ladybird beetles relies on blastema, which is essential for regenerating lost limbs during pupation.

results.title: A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice
results.author: M. Abrams
results.takeaway: Feeding moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice with L-leucine and insulin/sugar increases appendage regeneration, suggesting a conserved strategy across animal species.

results.title: Gene expression following induction of regeneration in Drosophila wing imaginal discs. Expression profile of regenerating wing discs
results.author: E. Blanco
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing disc regeneration involves various classes of genes, with signaling pathways playing a role in early and late steps of the regeneration process.

results.title: Does regeneration ability influence reproductive fitness in Menochilus sexmaculatus (Coleoptera: Coccinellidae)?
results.author: S. Saxena
results.takeaway: Regeneration in Menochilus sexmaculatus adults leads to longer pupa duration, increased body mass, and better reproductive fitness compared to unregenerated adults.

results.title: Matrix metalloproteinase 2 contributes to adult eclosion and immune response in the small hive beetle, Aethina tumida
results.author: Lixian Wu
results.takeaway: AtMmp2 is essential for adult eclosion and wing expansion in small hive beetles, and its loss leads to eclosion defects and mortality within 3 days of eclosion.

results.title: Response to comment on 'A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice'
results.author: Yutian Li
results.takeaway: Nutrient modulation can promote appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice, but variations in amputation methods, treatment concentrations, age, and stress management may explain why flies do not show regenerative responses.

results.title: Comparison of Leg Regeneration Potency Between Holometabolous Helicoverpa armigera (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae) and Hemimetabolous Locusta migratoria manilensis (Orthoptera: Acrididae)
results.author: Qingpo Yang
results.takeaway: Helicoverpa armigera can regenerate intact legs when excised at early larval stages, while Locusta migratoria has weak partial regeneration ability, with regeneration negatively impacting larval development.

results.title: Morphological, Molecular, and Hormonal Basis of Limb Regeneration across Pancrustacea.
results.author: Sunetra Das
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in insects and crustaceans involves conserved pathways and transcription factors, with hormone signaling playing a crucial role in blastemal proliferation.

results.title: Divergent mechanisms for regulating growth and development after imaginal disc damage in the tobacco hornworm, Manduca sexta
results.author: M. Rosero
results.takeaway: Imaginal disc damage in tobacco hornworms leads to delayed development, suggesting species differences in mechanisms delaying development.

results.title: Comment on 'A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice'
results.author: Anne Sustar
results.takeaway: A simple dietary supplement can induce appendage regeneration in jellyfish, fruit flies, and mice, challenging the belief that flies and mice lack regeneration capacity after injury.

results.title: Common themes in tetrapod appendage regeneration: a cellular perspective
results.author: Bess M. Miller
results.takeaway: Understanding commonalities between regenerative modes across diverse species can help integrate past observations and modern understanding of complex tissue regeneration.

results.title: Imaginal Disc Regeneration Takes Flight
results.author: I. Hariharan
results.takeaway: Genetic ablation systems in Drosophila enable precise damage and regeneration of imaginal discs, offering new insights into wound healing, regenerative growth, and tissue plasticity.

results.title: Imaginal Disc Regeneration: Something Old, Something New.
results.author: Melanie I. Worley
results.journal: Cold Spring Harbor perspectives in biology
results.takeaway: Imaginal disc regeneration has become more accessible and reproducible through genetic tissue-ablation systems, leading to new insights into wound healing, blastema formation, and cellular plasticity during regeneration.

results.title: Intercalary regeneration in imaginal wing disk of Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: J. Haynie
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal disk cells can undergo pattern regulation through regeneration and duplication when parts are removed, similar to how amphibian and cockroach limbs can regenerate.

results.title: The wing imaginal disc
results.author: B. Tripathi
results.takeaway: The Drosophila wing imaginal disc is a key system for identifying genes and deciphering systems crucial for animal development, contributing to tissue patterning, signal transduction, growth control, regeneration, and morphogenesis.

results.title: Regeneration and duplication in imaginal discs.
results.author: Peter J. Bryant
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal discs show a gradient of developmental capacity, where one fragment regenerates missing parts and the other duplicates existing structures, with a reversal in regenerative ability at the disc's center.

results.title: Ecdysone exerts biphasic control of regenerative signaling, coordinating the completion of regeneration with developmental progression
results.author: F. Karanja
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Ecdysone hormone signaling plays a dual role in coordinating regeneration with developmental progression, allowing regeneration to complete in mutant larvae.

results.title: Regeneration by complementary wing disc fragments of Drosophila melanogaster.
results.author: S. Kauffman
results.takeaway: Complementary Drosophila wing disc fragments predominantly duplicate and regenerate, suggesting positional coding in imaginal discs is carried out through a Cartesian cross-gradient system.

results.title: Cell death-induced regeneration in wing imaginal discs requires JNK signalling
results.author: Cora Bergantinos
results.takeaway: Cell death-induced regeneration in wing imaginal discs requires JNK signaling, which is activated at the leading edges of healing tissue and plays a crucial role in healing and regenerative growth.

results.title: Pattern reorganization occurs independently of cell division during Drosophila wing disc regeneration in situ
results.author: Sandra Díaz-García
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing disc regeneration involves wound healing, cell-fate commitment, and pattern reorganization, even when cell division is arrested, and Wingless is not required.

results.title: Regeneration and duplication following operations in situ on the imaginal discs of Drosophila melanogaster.
results.author: P. Bryant
results.takeaway: In situ operations on Drosophila larvae imaginal discs result in regeneration and duplication, suggesting that regeneration and duplication are identical phenomena based on the properties of the anlagen at the cut edge.

results.title: [Experimental investigations on the regenerating and regulating capacity of the wing imaginal disks inLymantria dispar L. (Lepidoptera)].
results.author: Hans Jürgen Stenzhorn
results.journal: Wilhelm Roux' Archiv fur Entwicklungsmechanik der Organismen
results.takeaway: Wing imaginal disks in Lymantria dispar can initiate regeneration but not complete it due to short time periods before the prepupal stage begins.

results.title: Imaginal Disc Growth Factor 6 (Idgf6) Is Involved in Larval and Adult Wing Development in Bactrocera correcta (Bezzi) (Diptera: Tephritidae)
results.author: Yan Zhao
results.takeaway: Idgf6 plays a crucial role in larval and adult wing development in the guava fruit fly, suggesting it could be a potential target for pest management.

results.title: Necrosis-induced apoptosis promotes regeneration in Drosophila wing imaginal discs
results.author: Jacob W Klemm
results.takeaway: Necrosis-induced apoptosis (NiA) in Drosophila wing imaginal discs promotes regeneration by activating programmed cell death at a distance from the injury site.

results.title: Regeneration and transdetermination in Drosophila imaginal discs.
results.author: Melanie I. Worley
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal discs respond to tissue damage through regeneration, transdetermination, and compensatory proliferation, with potential insights into tissue regeneration and cell plasticity.

results.title: Analysis of the Function of Apoptosis during Imaginal Wing Disc Regeneration in Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: Sandra Díaz-García
results.takeaway: Apoptosis plays a key role in imaginal wing disc regeneration in Drosophila melanogaster, with distinct apoptotic signals acting simultaneously and spreading throughout the disc.

results.title: Absence of distal to proximal intercalary regeneration in imaginal wing discs of Drosophila melanogaster.
results.author: J. Haynie
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing discs show limited regeneration from grafted fragments, suggesting repeated sets of positional information in the wing disc.

results.title: The biology and evolution of the Dilp8-Lgr3 pathway: A relaxin-like pathway coupling tissue growth and developmental timing control.
results.author: Alisson M. Gontijo
results.takeaway: The Dilp8-Lgr3 pathway in Drosophila larval imaginal discs coordinates tissue growth and developmental timing, promoting proportionate individuals and highlighting the importance of this pathway in insect life cycles.

129.state: Whether and how insects regrow wings is live again at the molecular level — the Ras/MAPK and Wnt/wingless pathways drive wing-disc regeneration in moths and Drosophila, and mayflies regrow gills. Early results on adult wing regeneration anticipate this work, uncited.

facets.q: Why do some arthropod lineages retain lifelong regenerative capacity while others lose it after metamorphosis?
Does continued moulting in ametabolous insects such as silverfish support appendage regeneration?

results.title: Speculations on the loss of regeneration derived from developmental modifications during land adaptation in some evolutionary lineages of animals
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: During land adaptation in some animal species, initial larval stages and metamorphosis were lost or altered, leading to the loss of regeneration and the gain of complex immunity and behavior.

results.title: Regeneration among animals: An evolutionary hypothesis related to aquatic versus terrestrial environment.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Regeneration in aquatic animals evolved from larval growth and metamorphosis, while in terrestrial animals, regeneration was largely or completely abolished due to adaptation to dry land conditions.

results.title: Fibroblast dedifferentiation as a determinant of successful regeneration
results.author: Tzi-Yang Lin
results.takeaway: Intrinsic restrictions in connective tissue dedifferentiation limit adult regeneration in frogs, as shown by the failure of transplanted cells to form cartilage.

results.title: Regeneration and regengrow in multicellular animals derive from the presence of processes of organ metamorphosis and continuous growth in their life cycles
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Regeneration is a basal ancestral property of aquatic animals, while terrestrial animals lack regeneration genes, relying on wound healing and scarring for repair.

results.title: OUP accepted manuscript
results.takeaway: Regeneration in purple shore crabs restores fitness in feeding and locomotion, but may lead to tradeoffs in reproduction, potentially leading to the loss of regenerative abilities in derived lineages like mammals and birds.

results.title: A developmentally regulated switch from stem cells to dedifferentiation for limb muscle regeneration in newts
results.author: H. Tanaka
results.takeaway: Newts have evolved novel strategies to maintain their limb regeneration ability after metamorphosis, switching from stem/progenitor-based to dedifferentiation-based mechanisms.

results.title: Regeneration or Scarring Derive from Specific Evolutionary Environmental Adaptations of the Life Cycles in Different Animals
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Regeneration in aquatic animals is influenced by the evolution of larval and intense metamorphic transformations, while terrestrial species have lost most of their regeneration ability due to modified genetic networks.

results.title: The sea spider Pycnogonum litorale overturns the paradigm of the absence of axial regeneration in molting animals
results.author: Georg Brenneis
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: The sea spider Pycnogonum litorale can regrow almost complete segments and the terminal body region after transverse amputation, challenging the assumption of axial regeneration loss in molting animals.

results.title: Progressive modifications during evolution involving epigenetic changes have determined loss of regeneration mainly in terrestrial animals: a hypothesis.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Epigenetic mechanisms during evolution have limited or erased regeneration in terrestrial animals, mainly due to the physical, chemical, and microbial conditions compared to aquatic environments.

results.title: Evolutionary bedfellows: reconstructing the ancestral state of autotomy and regeneration
results.author: L. Dunoyer
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Autotomy and regeneration coevolved at the base of arthropod and reptile trees, with regeneration distribution being more strongly linked to autotomy in reptiles.

results.title: The Capacity for Limb Regeneration During Metamorphosis is Broadly Conserved in the Coccinellidae
results.author: J. Michaud
results.journal: Annals of the Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration capacity is widely conserved in the Coccinellidae family, contributing to ecological success and influencing the distribution and behavior of these species.

results.title: Organ regeneration evolved in fish and amphibians in relation to metamorphosis: Speculations on a post-embryonic developmental process lost in amniotes after the water to land transition.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Annals of anatomy = Anatomischer Anzeiger : official organ of the Anatomische Gesellschaft
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration evolved in fish and amphibians due to metamorphosis, but was lost in amniotes after the water-to-land transition.

results.title: Regrowing the growth zone: metamorphosis kickstarts regeneration in the annelid Capitella teleta.
results.author: Alicia A. Boyd
results.takeaway: Metamorphosis in Capitella teleta larvae enables juveniles to regrow lost structures, highlighting the importance of metamorphosis in developmental plasticity.

results.title: Regeneration, Regengrow and Tissue Repair in Animals: Evolution Indicates That No Regeneration Occurs in Terrestrial Environments but Only Recovery Healing
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: In terrestrial animals, regeneration is limited to aquatic or amphibious species due to changes in genomes and environmental conditions, rather than a complete loss of regeneration.

results.title: Perspective: Appendage regeneration in amphibians and some reptiles derived from specific evolutionary histories.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Amphibian regeneration and lizard regeneration are derived from specific evolutionary histories, and regenerative medicine could potentially induce limb regeneration in humans by creating soft, hyaluronate-rich blastemas.

results.title: Appendage regeneration in anamniotes utilizes genes active during larval‐metamorphic stages that have been lost or altered in amniotes: The case for studying lizard tail regeneration
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration in anamniotes is influenced by specific evolutionary histories, with aquatic vertebrates having regenerative ability due to metamorphic stages, while terrestrial vertebrates lack regeneration.

results.title: “Omics” data unveil early molecular response underlying limb regeneration in the Chinese mitten crab, Eriocheir sinensis
results.author: Jun Wang
results.takeaway: The Chinese mitten crab's strong limb regeneration is facilitated by signal transduction and immune response, with novel genetic pathways identified.

results.title: Neotenic blastemal morphogenesis
results.author: R. Pearson
results.takeaway: The ability to post-maturationally regenerate in arthropods and amphibians is a function of histogenetic juvenilization, with blastemal cell pluripotency being a product of neotenic morphogenesis.

results.title: Regeneration Abilities among Extant Animals Depend on Their Evolutionary History and Life Cycles
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Different animals have different regenerative abilities based on their evolutionary history and life cycles, with some being adapted to terrestrial conditions but lacking cellular plasticity.

results.title: Experimentally induced metamorphosis in axolotls reduces regenerative rate and fidelity
results.author: James R. Monaghan
results.takeaway: Induced metamorphosis in adult axolotls reduces regeneration rate and fidelity, leading to carpal and digit malformations, while body size does not affect regeneration.

results.title: The moulting arthropod: a complete genetic toolkit review
results.author: Giulia Campli
results.takeaway: Arthropods undergo periodic moulting events, requiring tightly regulated processes and highlighting knowledge gaps across diverse taxa.

results.title: Regeneration of appendages and molting among the Thysanur
results.author: H. L. Sweetman
results.journal: Bulletin of the Brooklyn Entomological Society

results.title: Physiological and molecular mechanisms of insect appendage regeneration
results.author: Jiru Zhong
results.takeaway: Insect appendage regeneration is influenced by physiological conditions and internal molecular mechanisms, with research progress focusing on the role of hormones and cytokines in this process.

results.title: Morphological, Molecular, and Hormonal Basis of Limb Regeneration across Pancrustacea.
results.author: Sunetra Das
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in insects and crustaceans involves conserved pathways and transcription factors, with hormone signaling playing a crucial role in blastemal proliferation.

results.title: Hox-determined appendage regeneration restores cockroach courtship rituals
results.author: Xingmei Mai

results.title: Explosive regeneration and anamorphic development of legs in the house centipede Scutigera coleoptrata
results.author: Iulia Barutia
results.takeaway: House centipedes can regenerate their long legs within one molting cycle, making them unique among land arthropods and rare in marine taxa.

results.title: Cyclic renewal in three ectodermal appendage follicles: Hairs, feathers and teeth
results.author: Ping Wu
results.takeaway: Cyclic renewal in amniote appendage follicles, such as teeth, feathers, and hairs, allows them to adapt to various environments and life stages, enhancing their survival across various ecological environments.

results.title: Cytology, function and dynamics of stem and progenitor cells in decapod crustaceans
results.author: G. Vogt
results.takeaway: Decapod crustaceans possess a variety of stem cells, potentially offering insights into anti-aging and anti-cancer treatments for humans.

results.title: Autotomy and Regeneration of Appendages in Crustaceans: A Review
results.author: Lei Liu
results.journal: Journal of Ocean University of China
results.takeaway: Autotomy in crustaceans is a self-protection mechanism that can affect foraging efficiency, growth, and reproduction, with chelicerae removal reducing cannibalism in commercial production.

results.title: The activin signaling transcription factor Smox is an essential regulator of appendage size during regeneration after autotomy in the crayfish
results.author: Junpei Shinji
results.takeaway: The transcription factor Smox plays a crucial role in regulating pereopod growth and size during regeneration in crayfish, supporting evolutionary conservation of this aspect of molecular signaling during regeneration between protostomes and deuterostomes.

results.title: Comment on 'A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice'
results.author: Anne Sustar
results.takeaway: A simple dietary supplement can induce appendage regeneration in jellyfish, fruit flies, and mice, challenging the belief that flies and mice lack regeneration capacity after injury.

results.title: Appendage Regeneration in Vertebrates: What Makes This Possible?
results.author: Valentina Daponte
results.takeaway: Understanding the similarities and differences in appendage regeneration in lizards, salamanders, and zebrafish can guide effective regenerative strategies in mammals.

results.title: Morphological and Molecular Changes during Limb Regeneration of the Exopalaemon carinicauda
results.author: Chaofan Xing
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in the Exopalaemon carinicauda crustacean occurs rapidly and involves a complex network of cells and genes, contributing to our understanding of regeneration mechanisms in crustaceans.

results.title: Response to comment on 'A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice'
results.author: Yutian Li
results.takeaway: Nutrient modulation can promote appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice, but variations in amputation methods, treatment concentrations, age, and stress management may explain why flies do not show regenerative responses.

results.title: Larval leg integrity is maintained by Distal-less and is required for proper timing of metamorphosis in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum.
results.author: Yuichiro Suzuki
results.takeaway: Distal-less gene maintains larval leg integrity and is essential for proper metamorphosis timing in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum.

results.title: A conserved strategy for inducing appendage regeneration in moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice
results.author: M. Abrams
results.takeaway: Feeding moon jellyfish, Drosophila, and mice with L-leucine and insulin/sugar increases appendage regeneration, suggesting a conserved strategy across animal species.

results.title: The innovation of the final moult and the origin of insect metamorphosis
results.author: X. Belles
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B
results.takeaway: The final moult and hemimetaboly are a monophyletic innovation in insect metamorphosis, with the role of E93 as a promoter of wing formation and prothoracic gland degeneration being crucial for their emergence.

results.title: Appendage loss and regeneration in arthropods: a comparative view
results.author: Diego Maruzzo
results.takeaway: Arthropods can lose and regenerate appendages, with autotomy being limited to isopods, decapods, and some basal pterygotes, and regeneration widespread across all major groups.

107.state: Why some lineages keep regenerating while others lose it after metamorphosis is actively debated: recent work links regenerative loss to terrestrial adaptation, metamorphosis, and limits on connective-tissue dedifferentiation. Przibram's choice of ever-moulting silverfish as the informative case fits the modern framing.

facets.q: Can lepidopteran caterpillars regenerate larval structures such as the caudal horn?
What patterns the regeneration of insect larval appendages?

results.title: Comparative genomics reveals the dynamics of chromosome evolution in Lepidoptera
results.author: C. Wright
results.takeaway: Lepidopteran chromosomes are largely intact, with only eight lineages undergoing extensive reorganization through fission or fusion events.

results.title: A review and meta‐analysis of host‐plant selection by lepidopteran larvae: no evidence of diet breadth constraints on larval decision‐making
results.author: Diego Homem‐de‐Carvalho
results.takeaway: Lepidopteran larvae, regardless of diet breadth, are consistently accurate in discriminating among their host plants through olfaction and visual cues, suggesting ecological constraints may vary across insect life stages.

results.title: Caterpillars lack a resident gut microbiome
results.author: T. Hammer
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Caterpillars lack a resident gut microbiome, with only microbes ingested with food present in their guts, potentially benefiting their ecology and evolutionary diversification.

results.title: Diet influences proliferation and stability of gut bacterial populations in herbivorous lepidopteran larvae
results.author: C. Mason
results.takeaway: Bacteria in caterpillar guts are a dynamic component that varies with diet, suggesting that not all diets are equally useful for reducing variance in community structure and interpreting insect-microbe interactions.

results.title: Lepidopteran prolegs are novel traits, not leg homologs
results.author: Yuji Matsuoka
results.takeaway: Lepidopteran larval prolegs are novel traits derived from the activation of a pre-existing modular endite gene-regulatory network in the abdomen using the same Hox gene that represses legs in more lateral positions.

results.title: A Very Hungry Caterpillar: Polyethylene Metabolism and Lipid Homeostasis in Larvae of the Greater Wax Moth (Galleria mellonella).
results.author: C. LeMoine
results.takeaway: Greater wax moth larvae can rapidly degrade low-density polyethylene, maintaining lipid reserves and enhancing fatty acid metabolism, similar to honeycomb-fed larvae.

results.title: Synthetic Peptide Inhibition of Trypsin‐Like Proteases in Spodoptera frugiperda (Lepidoptera: Noctuidae): Evaluating the Influence of Gut Microbiota
results.author: Halina Schultz
results.journal: Archives of Insect Biochemistry and Physiology

results.title: How Glucosinolates Affect Generalist Lepidopteran Larvae: Growth, Development and Glucosinolate Metabolism
results.author: V. Jeschke
results.takeaway: Glucosinolates negatively affect generalist lepidopteran larvae growth and development, but not survival, with aliphatic glucosinolates having stronger effects than indolic glucosinolates.

results.title: Microbial Communities of Lycaenid Butterflies Do Not Correlate with Larval Diet
results.author: Melissa R. L. Whitaker
results.takeaway: Lycaenid butterflies do not rely on specific bacterial symbioses to mediate their diverse diets, suggesting taxonomically depauperate bacterial communities among Lepidoptera.

results.title: Gregariousness in lepidopteran larvae
results.author: Chenyu Qian
results.takeaway: Lepidopteran larvae's gregarious lifestyle is driven by ecological and evolutionary factors, leading to improved larval survival and accelerated development, but may diminish with development.

results.title: Adaptation to Leaf Traits of Individual Trees in a Forest Appears Rare in Caterpillars
results.author: F. Molleman
results.takeaway: Local adaptation to leaf traits of individual trees is rare in Lepidoptera, and phylogenetic isolation does not significantly impact this adaptation.

results.title: A spatiotemporal atlas of the lepidopteran pest Helicoverpa armigera midgut provides insights into nutrient processing and pH regulation
results.author: P. Ioannidis
results.takeaway: The study provides a comprehensive expression atlas of the midgut of the lepidopteran pest Helicoverpa armigera, revealing potential roles for vATPases and SLC9 transporters in nutrient processing and pH regulation.

results.title: Non‐native congeneric trees are poor‐quality host plants for a larval Lepidopteran
results.author: D. Narango
results.takeaway: Non-native congeneric trees are poor substitutes for locally native tree species in supporting specialized Lepidoptera, but non-local, regionally native species in cultivation may serve as adequate host plants.

results.title: Armoured Lepidopteran Caterpillars Preserved in Non-Fossil Resins and What They Tell Us about the Fossil Preservation of Caterpillars
results.author: Joshua Gauweiler
results.takeaway: Two lepidopteran caterpillars from Madagascar and Brazil have been preserved in non-fossil resins, demonstrating that very hairy caterpillars can be preserved in fine detail and increasing the known size range.

results.title: The olfactory system of Pieris brassicae caterpillars: from receptors to glomeruli
results.author: Qi Wang
results.takeaway: The larval olfactory system in Pieris brassicae caterpillars shows similarities to the adult system, with higher expression in the L4 stage and a similar number of olfactory receptor neurons.

results.title: Cis-regulatory evolution of Wnt family genes contributes to a morphological difference between silkworm species
results.author: Kenta Tomihara
results.takeaway: Cis-regulatory changes to Wnt1 and Wnt6 genes contribute to the caudal horn size difference between Bombyx mori and its closest relative Bombyx mandarina silkworm species, enabling modular gene evolution without widespread deleterious effects.

results.title: Ontogenetic Changes in the Feeding Behaviour of Helicoverpa armigera Larvae on Pigeonpea (Cajanus cajan) Flowers and Pods
results.author: Trevor M. Volp
results.takeaway: Helicoverpa armigera larvae show significant changes in feeding behavior as they develop, preferring flowers over pods and showing varying abilities to penetrate pod walls.

results.title: A 100 million-year-old armoured caterpillar supports the early diversification of moths and butterflies
results.author: J. Haug
results.takeaway: A 100 million-year-old armoured caterpillar from Cretaceous Myanmar amber supports early diversification of moths and butterflies.

results.title: The sensory ecology of caterpillars
results.author: Sam J. England
results.journal: Journal of Comparative Physiology. A, Neuroethology, Sensory, Neural, and Behavioral Physiology
results.takeaway: Caterpillar sensory ecology provides valuable insights into ecology, evolution, conservation, and management, but more comparative studies and knowledge gaps are needed.

results.title: When one tail isn't enough: abnormal caudal regeneration in lepidosaurs and its potential ecological impacts
results.author: J. Barr
results.takeaway: Abnormal caudal regeneration occurs in lepidosaurs across various species and populations, potentially impacting their fitness and life history through influencing locomotion, escape mechanisms, anti-predation tactics, and intra- and inter-specific signaling.

results.title: Developmental coupling of larval and adult stages in a complex life cycle: insights from limb regeneration in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum
results.author: Alison K Lee
results.takeaway: Larval and adult limb patterning in the red flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum, show continuity and may be developmentally coupled, suggesting the evolution of imaginal discs may have removed developmental constraints between larval and adult phenotypes.

results.title: Physiological and molecular mechanisms of insect appendage regeneration
results.author: Jiru Zhong
results.takeaway: Insect appendage regeneration is influenced by physiological conditions and internal molecular mechanisms, with research progress focusing on the role of hormones and cytokines in this process.

results.title: Effect of larval thermal conditions on limb regeneration in a ladybird beetle, Cheilomenes sexmaculata.
results.author: Shriza Rai
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part A, Ecological and integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Larval thermal conditions affect limb regeneration in ladybird beetles, with higher temperatures accelerating recovery time after limb amputation.

results.title: Pattern regulation and regeneration.
results.author: V. French
results.journal: Philosophical transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B, Biological sciences
results.takeaway: Insect legs form patterns along polar coordinates, guiding their response to disturbances and regeneration, with two rules governing cellular behavior after disturbances.

results.title: The Ras/MAPK pathway is required for regenerative growth of wing discs in the black cutworm Agrotis ypsilon.
results.author: Yuanyuan Xu
results.takeaway: The Ras/MAPK pathway plays a crucial role in regenerative growth of wing discs in black cutworm Agrotis ypsilon, providing molecular clues to cell proliferation regulation during regeneration in lepidopteran insects.

results.title: Regeneration of appendages in the large milkweed bug, Oncopeltus fasciatus.
results.author: Victoria K. Shaw
results.takeaway: Amputation of appendages in Oncopeltus does not stimulate increased growth rate, but causes reorganization of stump material, which then grows at a normal rate.

results.title: Limb regeneration is blastema dependent in a ladybird beetle, Cheilomenes sexmaculata (Fabricius)
results.author: S. Rawat
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in ladybird beetles relies on blastema, which is essential for regenerating lost limbs during pupation.

results.title: Regeneration, Regengrow and Tissue Repair in Animals: Evolution Indicates That No Regeneration Occurs in Terrestrial Environments but Only Recovery Healing
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: In terrestrial animals, regeneration is limited to aquatic or amphibious species due to changes in genomes and environmental conditions, rather than a complete loss of regeneration.

results.title: Developmental Continuity Between Larval and Adult Leg Patternings in Coccinella septempunctata (Coleoptera: Coccinellidae)
results.author: Pengxiang Wu
results.takeaway: Adult leg patterning in the seven-spotted lady beetle, Coccinella septempunctata, is inherited from the larval leg and does not develop independently of the larval leg.

results.title: Regeneration in the medial-lateral axis of the insect thoracic segment
results.author: V. French
results.takeaway: Grafting beetle larvae legs in different orientations into the thorax reveals that no model fully explains all the results of regeneration in the insect ventral thorax.

results.title: Gill regeneration in the mayfly Cloeon uncovers new molecular pathways in insect regeneration
results.author: C. Martin-Blanco
results.takeaway: The mayfly Cloeon can regenerate lost abdominal gills faster than normal development, revealing new molecular pathways in insect regeneration.

results.title: Larval leg integrity is maintained by Distal-less and is required for proper timing of metamorphosis in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum.
results.author: Yuichiro Suzuki
results.takeaway: Distal-less gene maintains larval leg integrity and is essential for proper metamorphosis timing in the flour beetle, Tribolium castaneum.

results.title: Supernumerary regeneration in the large milkweed bug Oncopeltus fasciatus.
results.author: V. Shaw
results.takeaway: The large milkweed bug Oncopeltus fasciatus can produce supernumerary regenerates in its metathoracic legs after grafting, with the external face of the leg being the only one capable of producing such regenerates.

results.title: Does regeneration ability influence reproductive fitness in Menochilus sexmaculatus (Coleoptera: Coccinellidae)?
results.author: S. Saxena
results.takeaway: Regeneration in Menochilus sexmaculatus adults leads to longer pupa duration, increased body mass, and better reproductive fitness compared to unregenerated adults.

results.title: Nano-CT imaging of larvae in the ant Pheidolehyatti reveals coordinated growth of a rudimentary organ necessary for soldierdevelopment.
results.author: Sophie Koch
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: The rudimentary forewing disc in soldier larvae of the ant Pheidolehyatti coordinates with larval size and other imaginal discs to generate the big-headed soldier caste, demonstrating novel inter-organ coordination patterns.

results.title: Distinct roles of the Hox genes Ultrabithorax and abdominal‐A in scorpionfly embryonic proleg development
results.author: Bing-Peng Liu
results.takeaway: Ultrabithorax and abdominal-A Hox genes play distinct roles in scorpionfly embryonic proleg development, with Ultrabithorax regulating appendage fate and abdominal-A specifying segments and proleg identity.

results.title: Specification and Patterning of Drosophila Appendages
results.author: Mireya Ruiz-Losada
results.takeaway: The evolutionary origin of the insect wing is partially related to the development of ventral appendages in fruit flies.

results.title: Morphological, Molecular, and Hormonal Basis of Limb Regeneration across Pancrustacea.
results.author: Sunetra Das
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in insects and crustaceans involves conserved pathways and transcription factors, with hormone signaling playing a crucial role in blastemal proliferation.

results.title: Epimorphosis and repair processes of schistomelic pedipalps and walking appendages in Tegenaria atrica (Araneae, Agelenidae)
results.author: T. Napiórkowska
results.takeaway: Schistomelic walking legs and pedipalps in Tegenaria atrica spiders undergo regeneration and repair processes, with epimorphosis of pedipalps completed at nymph IV and walking appendages at nymph V.

results.title: Hedgehog signaling regulates imaginal cell differentiation in a basally branching holometabolous insect.
results.author: Carla M Villarreal
results.takeaway: Hedgehog signaling plays a critical role in imaginal cell differentiation and larval appendage regeneration in holometabolous insects, contributing to the evolution of complete metamorphosis.

124.state: Lepidopteran larval-appendage development is studied (proleg gene-regulatory networks, host discrimination), but regeneration of a specific larval structure like the caudal horn is barely revisited. What patterns a regenerated larval organ remains an open developmental question.

facets.q: Do light wavelength and background colour determine butterfly pupal coloration?
What is the developmental and hormonal mechanism of environmentally cued pupal colour plasticity in Lepidoptera?

results.title: Pupal polymorphism in the butterfly Danaus Chrysippus (L.): environmental, seasonal and genetic influences
results.author: David A S Smith
results.journal: Biological Journal of The Linnean Society
results.takeaway: The pupa color of the tropical butterfly Danaus chrysippus is influenced by environmental factors, genetic factors, and seasonal cycles, with environmental factors playing a key role in pupa development.

results.title: Two different sensory mechanisms for the control of pupal protective coloration in butterflies.
results.author: Sota Hiraga
results.takeaway: In Graphium sarpedon, pupal coloration is determined by the illuminant difference between dorsal and ventral light, while in Papilio xuthus, it is determined by tactile signals from a rough surfaced pupation site.

results.title: Color preference for pupation sites of the butterfly larvaeInachis io and the significance of the pupal melanization reducing factor
results.author: G. Starnecker
results.takeaway: Butterfly larvae Inachis io can adapt to various light and dark background colors, with a preference for certain colors, and the pupal melanization reducing factor plays a role in this adaptation process.

results.title: Identifying the proximal cue(s) for pupal color variation in the bordered patch butterfly, Chlosyne lacinia (Lepidoptera: Nymphalidae)
results.author: Jamie M. Phelps
results.journal: Annals of the Entomological Society of America
results.takeaway: Chlosyne lacinia pupae with higher melanization on black backgrounds have longer developmental periods, suggesting a trade-off between defense and developmental time.

results.title: Pupal colour plasticity in the butterfly Catopsilia pomona (Lepidoptera: Pieridae)
results.author: Tarunkishwor Yumnam
results.journal: Biological Journal of the Linnean Society
results.takeaway: Pupal color plasticity in the butterfly Catopsilia pomona helps pupae match their substrate colors, with leaf-borne pupae tending to be greener and off-leaf pupae browner, influenced by leaf position, midrib thickness, and leaf length.

results.title: Environmental factors influencing pupal colour determination in Lepidoptera. II. Experiments with Pieris rapae, Pieris napi and Pieris brassicae
results.author: A. Smith
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B. Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Light stimuli, particularly yellow and blue spectral wavelengths, play a crucial role in determining pupal coloration in Lepidoptera species.

results.title: A meta-analysis of butterfly structural colors: their color range, distribution and biological production.
results.author: Rachel C. Thayer
results.takeaway: Butterfly structural colors are ubiquitous in short wavelengths but rare in long wavelengths, with blue wavelengths being more prevalent and diverse than other hues.

results.title: Abiotic and biotic predictors of macroecological patterns in bird and butterfly coloration
results.author: R. Dalrymple
results.takeaway: Bird and butterfly species exhibit more reflective colors in better-lit environments, more diverse colors in regions with greater net primary production, and longer growing seasons, with community diversity being the most powerful predictor of color pattern variation.

results.title: Interactions of environmental factors influencing pupal coloration in swallowtail butterfly Papilio xuthus.
results.author: Sota Hiraga
results.takeaway: Under sufficient light, swallowtail butterfly pupae produce green pupae on smooth surfaces and brown pupae on rough surfaces, with tactile signals playing a key role in inducing brown coloration.

results.title: Induced Changes in the Pigmentation of the Pupoe of the Butterfly Pieris Napi L., and their Inheritance
results.author: J. Harrison
results.journal: Proceedings of The Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Exposure to orange light before pupation in Pieris Napi L. butterfly larvae leads to inherited green pupal pigmentation, affecting the appearance of the chrysalids.

results.title: Seasonal change in pupation behaviour and pupal mortality in a swallowtail butterfly
results.author: C. Stefanescu
results.takeaway: Seasonal changes in swallowtail butterfly pupation behavior and mortality rates suggest that pupation sites in litter and above ground level evolved to avoid strong bird predation on hostplants and to protect against both visual and non-visual predators.

results.title: Thermobiological effects of temperature‐induced color variations in Aglais urticae (Lepidoptera, Nymphalidae)
results.author: G. Markl
results.takeaway: Temperature-induced color variations in adult butterflies provide thermobiological benefits, with lower temperatures leading to darker coloration and higher temperatures leading to lighter individuals.

results.title: Pupal colour dimorphism in a desert swallowtail (Lepidoptera: Papilionidae) is driven by changes in food availability, not photoperiod
results.author: Daniel Piñones‐Tapia
results.takeaway: Pupal coloration in desert swallowtail butterflies is driven by food availability, not photoperiod, with matching pupation site coloration potentially aiding in field survival.

results.title: Role of Larval Stemmata in Control of Pupal Color and Pupation Site Preference in Swallowtail Butterflies Papilio troilus, Papilio polyxenes, Eurytides marcellus, and Papilio glaucus (Lepidoptera: Papilionidae)
results.author: Kristen Mellencamp
results.takeaway: Larval stemmata play a crucial role in controlling pupal color and pupation site preference in swallowtail butterflies, with differential stimulation of green and blue photoreceptors potentially playing a role in this process.

results.title: Molecular Mechanisms Underlying Pupal Protective Color Switch in Papilio polytes Butterflies
results.author: S. Yoda
results.takeaway: Papilio polytes butterflies' pupal colors are regulated by melanin synthesis genes, tyrosine hydroxylase, and laccase 2, with green coloration mainly influenced by multiple bilin binding protein and juvenile hormone binding protein genes.

results.title: Pupal colour–pupation substrate correlation in butterflies
results.author: H. Mayekar
results.takeaway: Pupal color plasticity in butterflies is strongly correlated with pupation substrate choice, potentially protecting them against predation by matching their background.

results.title: Why ‘false’ colours are seen by butterflies
results.author: A. Kelber
results.takeaway: Butterflies perceive 'false' colors based on light polarization, which may help them find optimal oviposition sites.

results.title: A long noncoding RNA at the cortex locus controls adaptive coloration in butterflies
results.author: L. Livraghi
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: A long noncoding RNA, cortex, plays a key role in adaptive diversification of wing patterns in butterflies.

results.title: Ultraviolet and yellow reflectance but not fluorescence is important for visual discrimination of conspecifics by Heliconius erato
results.author: Susan D. Finkbeiner
results.takeaway: Ultraviolet and yellow reflectance, but not fluorescence, are important for visual discrimination of conspecifics in Heliconius erato butterflies, suggesting sexual selection rather than predation.

results.title:
Effect of density on pupal colour plasticity in the butterfly
 Mycalesis mineus
 (Lepidoptera, Nymphalidae)
results.author: H. Mayekar
results.takeaway: High larval densities in the butterfly Mycalesis mineus lead to a higher proportion of brown pupae, but results differed between two experiments.

results.title: The proximate control of pupal color in swallowtail butterflies: implications for the evolution of environmentally cued pupal color in butterflies (Lepidoptera: Papilionidae).
results.author: Mattia Jones
results.takeaway: The control of pupal color by PMRF has evolved independently in the swallowtail and nymphalid-pierid lineages, with green pupal color potentially evolving through facultative inhibition of PMRF release.

results.title: Pupal colour plasticity in a tropical butterfly, Mycalesis mineus (Nymphalidae: Satyrinae)
results.author: H. Mayekar
results.takeaway: Tropical satyrine butterfly Mycalesis mineus exhibits pupal color plasticity, with brown pupae more common at low relative humidity and faster development, and pupation substrate choice influencing pupal color.

results.title: Environmental factors influencing pupal colour determination in Lepidoptera I. Experiments with Papilio polytes, Papilio demoleus and Papilio polyxenes
results.author: A. Smith
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B. Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Pupal coloration in Lepidoptera is influenced by substrate texture, environmental conditions, and hormone secretion, with background color playing a lesser role.

results.title: Pupal Color Polyphenism Regulated by Temperature and Photoperiod in the Asian Comma Butterfly, Polygonia C-aureum (Lepidoptera: Nymphalidae)
results.author: A. Yamanaka
results.takeaway: Pupal color polyphenism in the Asian comma butterfly, Polygonia c-aureum, is mainly regulated by temperature and to a small degree by photoperiod, contributing to phenotypic plasticity in life history traits.

results.title: Die Auslösung der Umfärbung durch das Häutungshormon bei Cerura vinula L. (Lepidoptera, Notodontidae)☆☆☆
results.author: D. Bückmann
results.takeaway: The color change and pupal moult in Cerura vinula larvae are caused by the same hormone, ecdyson, but at different concentrations, with the normal course of events likely being a slow increase in hormone concentration.

results.title: Pupation site preference and environmentally cued pupal colour dimorphism in the swallowtail butterfly Papilio polyxenes Fabr. (Lepidoptera: Papilionidae)
results.author: W. Hazel
results.journal: Biological Journal of The Linnean Society
results.takeaway: In the swallowtail butterfly Papilio polyxenes, genetic variation in pupal color dimorphism and pupation site preference are not correlated, suggesting environmental cues play a role in habitat selection.

results.title: The genetic and environmental basis of pupal colour dimorphism in Papilio zelicaon (Lepidoptera: papilionidae)
results.author: S. Sims
results.takeaway: Pupal color dimorphism in Papilio zelicaon butterflies is influenced by environmental factors, with short days and cool temperatures promoting diapause brown pupae and long days and warm temperatures favoring continuous development and cryptic color matching.

results.title: Regulatory mechanisms in phenotypic plasticity of diapause and nondiapause pupal colouration of the swallowtail butterfly Papilio machaon
results.author: A. Yamanaka
results.takeaway: The swallowtail butterfly Papilio machaon responds to environmental cues of pupation sites, with hormonal factors in the larval central nervous system regulating pupal coloration based on photoperiodic conditions.

results.title: The ivory lncRNA regulates seasonal color patterns in buckeye butterflies
results.author: R. A. Fandino
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: The ivory lncRNA plays a crucial role in regulating seasonal color patterns in buckeye butterflies, with its regulation affecting diverse aspects of color pattern evolution and development.

results.title: Neuroendocrine Mechanism Involved in Pupal Colour Dimorphism in Swallowtail Papilio Xuthus L.
results.author: L. Awiti
results.journal: International Journal of Tropical Insect Science
results.takeaway: The brain in Papilio xuthus larvae selects pupation environmental conditions based on its environmental preferences, releasing browning hormone when pupation is favorable for brown pupa formation.

results.title: aristaless1 has a dual role in appendage formation and wing color specification during butterfly development
results.author: Erick X. Bayala
results.takeaway: Aristaless1 plays a dual role in butterfly development, controlling wing color specification and appendage formation, with high levels during early pupation promoting melanic pigmentation and reduced levels promoting yellow pigmentation.

142.state: Environmentally cued pupal colour is well-studied: light wavelength, dorsal/ventral illumination and background drive pupal colour and melanization across many butterflies (Danaus, Graphium, Catopsilia), with developmental trade-offs. This pupal-plasticity result sits squarely in the active literature.

facets.q: Can adult flies such as Drosophila regenerate wings after the imaginal stage?
What are the limits of regeneration in fully differentiated adult Diptera?

results.title: Imaginal Disc Regeneration Takes Flight
results.author: I. Hariharan
results.takeaway: Genetic ablation systems in Drosophila enable precise damage and regeneration of imaginal discs, offering new insights into wound healing, regenerative growth, and tissue plasticity.

results.title: The wing imaginal disc
results.author: B. Tripathi
results.takeaway: The Drosophila wing imaginal disc is a key system for identifying genes and deciphering systems crucial for animal development, contributing to tissue patterning, signal transduction, growth control, regeneration, and morphogenesis.

results.title: Gene expression following induction of regeneration in Drosophila wing imaginal discs. Expression profile of regenerating wing discs
results.author: E. Blanco
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing disc regeneration involves various classes of genes, with signaling pathways playing a role in early and late steps of the regeneration process.

results.title: Chromatin status is key for regeneration.
results.author: S. Forcales
results.takeaway: Chromatin status plays a crucial role in regeneration of drosophila imaginal discs, with genetic ablation systems providing new insights into wound healing and regenerative growth mechanisms.

results.title: The pioneer transcription factor Zelda controls the exit from regeneration and restoration of patterning in Drosophila
results.author: Anish Bose
results.takeaway: Zelda plays a crucial role in regulating the transition from regeneration to normal gene expression in Drosophila wing discs, highlighting a fundamental difference between developmental and regeneration patterning.

results.title: Drosophila Wnt and STAT Define Apoptosis-Resistant Epithelial Cells for Tissue Regeneration after Irradiation
results.author: Shilpi Verghese
results.takeaway: Differential gene expression within identical epithelial cells can create a subpopulation with greater resistance to apoptosis, enabling tissue regeneration after damage without the need for special damage-resistant cell types like stem cells.

results.title: Long non-coding RNAs involved in Drosophila development and regeneration
results.author: Carlos Camilleri-Robles
results.takeaway: The lncRNA may play a role in Drosophila development and regeneration, restoring wing regeneration in damaged wing discs.

results.title: Ets21C sustains a pro-regenerative transcriptional program in blastema cells of Drosophila imaginal discs
results.author: Melanie I. Worley
results.takeaway: Ets21C plays a crucial role in maintaining regeneration in damaged Drosophila wing discs, potentially promoting aberrant growth in tumors.

results.title: Regenerative growth in Drosophila imaginal discs is regulated by Wingless and Myc
results.author: Rachel K. Smith-Bolton
results.takeaway: Wingless and Myc are crucial for regenerative growth in Drosophila larvae, and ectopic Myc enhances regeneration when other growth drivers fail, making it suitable for large-scale genetic screens.

results.title: Transdetermination: Drosophila imaginal disc cells exhibit stem cell-like potency.
results.author: K. McClure
results.journal: The international journal of biochemistry & cell biology
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal disc cells exhibit stem cell-like potency during regeneration, with some cells switching fate during regeneration, enhancing our understanding of developmental plasticity in stem cells.

results.title: Cell death-induced regeneration in wing imaginal discs requires JNK signalling
results.author: Cora Bergantinos
results.takeaway: Cell death-induced regeneration in wing imaginal discs requires JNK signaling, which is activated at the leading edges of healing tissue and plays a crucial role in healing and regenerative growth.

results.title: The Drosophila Duox maturation factor is a key component of a positive feedback loop that sustains regeneration signaling
results.author: S. J. Khan
results.takeaway: A positive feedback loop involving Duox maturation factor NIP and moladietz gene is crucial for maintaining regeneration signaling in damaged tissue.

results.title: Pattern formation in the imaginal wing disc of Drosophila melanogaster: fate map, regeneration and duplication.
results.author: P. Bryant
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing disc fragments show regeneration from one cut surface and duplication from the other during metamorphosis, with some fragments showing incomplete regeneration and a high tendency to duplicate.

results.title: Intercalary regeneration in imaginal wing disk of Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: J. Haynie
results.takeaway: Drosophila imaginal disk cells can undergo pattern regulation through regeneration and duplication when parts are removed, similar to how amphibian and cockroach limbs can regenerate.

results.title: Necrosis-induced apoptosis promotes regeneration in Drosophila wing imaginal discs
results.author: Jacob W Klemm
results.takeaway: Necrosis-induced apoptosis (NiA) in Drosophila wing imaginal discs promotes regeneration by activating programmed cell death at a distance from the injury site.

results.title: Temporal dynamics of apoptosis-induced proliferation in pupal wing development: implications for regenerative ability
results.author: Sara Ahmed-de-Prado
results.takeaway: Apoptosis-induced proliferative response in pupal wings can continue until 34 hours after puparium formation, but beyond this time, the regenerative response is diminished, mediated in part by the ecdysone-responsive transcription factor E93.

results.title: Expression dynamics of long non-coding RNAs during imaginal disc development and regeneration in Drosophila
results.author: Carlos Camilleri-Robles
results.takeaway: The lncRNA CR40469 plays a potential role in trans during damage response in Drosophila wing imaginal discs, with CR34335 partially compensating for its function in the absence of CR40469.

results.title: Regenerative growth is constrained by brain tumor to ensure proper patterning in Drosophila
results.author: Syeda Nayab Fatima Abidi
results.takeaway: Brain tumor (Brat) constrains regenerative growth in Drosophila wings to ensure proper tissue organization and prevent aberrant growth.

results.title: Developmental regulation of regenerative potential in Drosophila by ecdysone through a bistable loop of ZBTB transcription factors
results.author: Karine Narbonne-Reveau
results.takeaway: Ecdysone signaling in Drosophila larvae regulates regenerative potential by switching epithelial progenitors from a self-renewing to a differentiation-prone state.

results.title: A switch to non-proliferative growth sustains Drosophila wing development during the early pupal stage.
results.author: Khaoula El Marzkioui
results.takeaway: Drosophila wing development continues during the larva-to-pupa transition, with a switch to non-proliferative growth during the early pupal stage driven by increased cell volume.

results.title: Plasticity and reprogramming of differentiated cells in amphibian regeneration
results.author: J. Brockes
results.takeaway: Amphibian regeneration is primarily driven by plasticity and reprogramming of differentiated cells, rather than a'reserve-cell' mechanism.

results.title: Fibroblast dedifferentiation as a determinant of successful regeneration
results.author: Tzi-Yang Lin
results.takeaway: Intrinsic restrictions in connective tissue dedifferentiation limit adult regeneration in frogs, as shown by the failure of transplanted cells to form cartilage.

results.title: Insect Gut Regeneration.
results.author: Peng Zhang
results.journal: Cold Spring Harbor perspectives in biology
results.takeaway: Insect gut regeneration involves various molecular mechanisms, including stress sensing, intestinal stem cell-intrinsic functions, and active culling of healthy epithelial cells.

results.title: Morphometric and molecular insights into Bactrocera dorsalis (Hendel, 1912) (Diptera: Tephritidae) infestation on Ziziphus mauritiana Lamk. (Indian Jujube)
results.author: K. Palanivelu

results.title: Crop-pollinating Diptera have diverse diet and habitat needs in both larval and adult stages.
results.author: Abby E. Davis
results.journal: Ecological applications : a publication of the Ecological Society of America
results.takeaway: Crop-pollinating Diptera have diverse diet and habitat needs, highlighting the need for conservation strategies that consider non-floral resources like wet organic materials and dung.

results.title: Beyond Adult Stem Cells: Dedifferentiation as a Unifying Mechanism Underlying Regeneration in Invertebrate Deuterostomes
results.author: C. Ferrario
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Local dedifferentiation is a key cellular mechanism contributing to regeneration in invertebrate deuterostomes, offering valuable models for studying adult regeneration and regenerative medicine.

results.title: Regeneration and regengrow in multicellular animals derive from the presence of processes of organ metamorphosis and continuous growth in their life cycles
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Regeneration is a basal ancestral property of aquatic animals, while terrestrial animals lack regeneration genes, relying on wound healing and scarring for repair.

results.title: Pluripotent, germ cell competent adult stem cells underlie cnidarian regenerative ability and clonal growth
results.author: Áine Varley
results.takeaway: Cnidarian adult stem cells (i-cells) are pluripotent, enabling regenerative, plant-like clonal growth and aging-defying clonal growth in these animals.

results.title: Developmental onset of planarian whole-body regeneration depends on axis reset
results.author: C. Booth

results.title: The Regenerating Adult Zebrafish Retina Recapitulates Developmental Fate Specification Programs
results.author: M. Lahne
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Adult zebrafish retinas can regenerate neurons, resetting developmental cell specification programs to adapt to lost cell types.

results.title: Evolutionary dynamics of whole-body regeneration across planarian flatworms
results.author: Miquel Vila‐Farré
results.takeaway: Planarian flatworms show multiple transitions between robust whole-body regeneration and restricted regeneration, with the Wnt pathway linked to regeneration defects and diverse roles in their reproductive systems.

results.title: The Influence of the Nerve in Regeneration of the Amphibian Extremity
results.author: M. Singer
results.takeaway: The regeneration process in amphibians is influenced by the nerve, which plays a crucial role in regenerating damaged extremities and organs, with no clear distinction between embryonic and adult regeneration processes.

results.title: Consistent differences in tissue oxygen levels across 15 insect species reflect a balance between oxygen supply and demand and highlight a hitherto unknown adaptation for extracting sufficient oxygen from water
results.author: J. Birrell
results.takeaway: Tissue oxygen levels in insects vary greatly, reflecting a balance between oxygen supply and demand, with aquatic species having lower levels and inactive insects needing more oxygen.

results.title: Evaluating old truths: Final adult size in holometabolous insects is set by the end of larval development.
results.author: Lisa Hanna
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Final adult size in holometabolous insects is set at the end of larval development, with some exceptions like fruit flies and carpenter ants, where size fluctuates but does not significantly change.

results.title: Regeneration of the larval sea star nervous system by wounding induced respecification to the Sox2 lineage
results.author: M. Zheng
results.takeaway: Larval sea star nervous systems can regenerate after decapitation using a combination of lineage-restricted stem cells, respecification into neural lineages, and partial reuse of developmental gene regulatory networks.

results.title: Analysis of the Influence of Changing and Fixed Temperatures on the Growth and Pteridine Content in the Head of Adults Sarcophaga crassipalpis (Diptera: Sarcophagidae)
results.author: Fernand Jocelin Ngando
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: Temperature and gender are the main factors influencing the pteridine content in the head of adult Sarcophaga crassipalpis flies, with variations across different temperatures.

results.title: Highly regenerative species-specific genes improve age-associated features in the adult Drosophila midgut
results.author: Hiroki Nagai
results.takeaway: Highly regenerative species-specific genes can improve stem cell functions and promote a healthy lifespan in aging animals, such as Drosophila.

results.title: Regeneration of swimming antennae after partial amputation in long- and short-lived Daphnia
results.author: Charles A. Schumpert
results.takeaway: Daphnia's short-lived species, D. pulex, has significantly better regenerative ability than its long-lived counterpart, D. pulicaria, suggesting a useful invertebrate model for understanding regeneration and aging.

results.title: Neurogenesis in the adult Drosophila brain
results.author: Kassi L. Crocker
results.takeaway: Adult Drosophila brains can repair themselves after traumatic brain injury, potentially offering insights into human brain repair processes.

results.title: Characterization of the dynamics and variability of neuronal subtype responses during growth, degrowth, and regeneration of Nematostella vectensis
results.author: Jamie A. Havrilak
results.takeaway: The Nematostella nerve net scales with body size and neuronal subtypes display differential regenerative responses, maintaining functionality as it scales.

130.state: The Drosophila wing imaginal disc is now a premier regeneration model — genetic ablation systems, chromatin regulation and factors like Zelda dissect disc regeneration in detail. The question of whether the adult fly can rebuild a wing is answered with tools Przibram could not have imagined.

facets.q: Do aquatic insect larvae form symbioses with algae or microbes?
What is the ecological role of microbial and algal symbionts in dragonfly (odonate) larvae?

results.title: Exploring Changes in the Microbiota of Aedes albopictus: Comparison Among Breeding Site Water, Larvae, and Adults
results.author: F. Scolari
results.takeaway: Larvae and adult Asian tiger mosquitoes have different microbial communities than adults, with breeding site water showing higher bacterial diversity and larvae showing a more diverse microbial community than adults.

results.title: Marine Invertebrate Larvae Associated with Symbiodinium: A Mutualism from the Start?
results.author: M. Mies
results.takeaway: The mutualistic relationship between animal hosts and algal symbionts in marine invertebrates is often not established during larval development, with symbiont identity potentially influencing this outcome.

results.title: Culex quinquefasciatus larvae development arrested when fed on Neochloris aquatica
results.author: M. F. Gil
results.takeaway: Neochloris aquatica microalga negatively affects the development of Culex quinquefasciatus larvae, making it a potential biological control agent for controlling this mosquito population.

results.title: Bacterial symbionts support larval sap feeding and adult folivory in (semi-)aquatic reed beetles
results.author: Frank Reis
results.takeaway: Bacterial symbionts support larval sap feeding and adult folivory in reed beetles, providing essential amino acids and vitamins, but may constrain their adaptive potential by promoting specialization.

results.title: Culex pipiens and Culex restuans larval interactions shape the bacterial communities in container aquatic habitats
results.author: Teresia M Njoroge
results.takeaway: Mosquito larvae significantly shape bacterial communities in container aquatic habitats, with intra- and interspecific interactions shaping their microbiota.

results.title: Microorganism-Based Larval Diets Affect Mosquito Development, Size and Nutritional Reserves in the Yellow Fever Mosquito Aedes aegypti (Diptera: Culicidae)
results.author: R. Souza
results.takeaway: Larvae fed yeast show high plasticity to feed, able to develop under different microorganism-based diets, but bacteria and microalgae diets cause delays in development and adult emergence.

results.title: Mining the diversity and functional profile of bacterial symbionts from the larvae of Chironomus circumdatus (bloodworms)
results.author: Chaitali M. Shaha
results.takeaway: The larvae of Chironomus circumdatus (bloodworms) harbor a variety of bacteria that help them adapt and survive in polluted waters, potentially aiding in bioremediation and reducing the spread of deadly human diseases.

results.title: Links between metamorphosis and symbiosis in holometabolous insects
results.author: T. Hammer
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B
results.takeaway: Holometabolous insects have unique constraints on symbiont maintenance, but also have the flexibility to decouple symbiotic associations over development, potentially benefiting from microbial protection, nutrients, and pupation cues.

results.title: Comparative assessment of the bacterial communities associated with Anopheles darlingi immature stages and their breeding sites in the Brazilian Amazon
results.author: Katherine D. Mosquera
results.takeaway: The bacterial communities in Anopheles darlingi larvae and breeding water in the Brazilian Amazon reflect the environment, with larval microbiota reflecting the aquatic habitat where they developed.

results.title: Repressed Beauveria bassiana infections in Delia antiqua due to associated microbiota.
results.author: Fangyuan Zhou
results.takeaway: Reintroducing microbiota from Delia antiqua larvae can increase their resistance to fungal infections, potentially improving pest biocontrol.

results.title: Versatile and Dynamic Symbioses Between Insects and Burkholderia Bacteria.
results.author: Martin Kaltenpoth
results.takeaway: Insects and Burkholderia bacteria have diverse symbioses, providing nutritional benefits, resistance to insecticides, and valuable insights into symbiotic interactions.

results.title: Sea Urchin Larvae (Strongylocentrotus purpuratus) Select and Maintain a Unique Microbiome Compared to Environmental Sources.
results.author: Karina Brocco French
results.takeaway: Sea urchin larvae select and maintain a unique microbiome compared to their diet and habitat, with filtered seawater being the main source of potential symbionts.

results.title: Symbiotic Microorganisms and Their Different Association Types in Aquatic and Semiaquatic Bugs
results.author: Yu Men
results.takeaway: Symbiotic microorganisms help true bugs adapt to diverse ecological habitats, providing benefits such as richer amino acid and lipid metabolism pathways and xenobiotics biodegradation pathways.

results.title: Symbiotic Life of Echinoderm Larvae
results.author: Tyler J. Carrier
results.takeaway: Echinoderm larvae associate with specific bacteria and exhibit specific responses to pathogenic bacteria, which may aid in maintaining the symbiotic community and avoiding dysbiosis.

results.title: Larvae of Deep-Sea Invertebrates Harbor Low-Diversity Bacterial Communities
results.author: Tyler J. Carrier
results.takeaway: Deep-sea invertebrate larvae associate with low-diversity bacterial communities, suggesting a weak reliance on a microbiome and differing symbiotic contributions from coastal marine invertebrates.

results.title: Microbiota acquisition and transmission in Drosophila flies
results.author: Robin Guilhot
results.takeaway: Drosophila flies acquire microbiota from diverse sources, enabling mutualistic interactions and local adaptation through microbiota-mediated adaptation.

results.title: Dynamics of Insect–Microbiome Interaction Influence Host and Microbial Symbiont
results.author: Ayushi Gupta
results.takeaway: Insect-microbe interactions influence both host and microbial symbiont life cycles and biology, potentially leading to sustainable and environmentally safe pest control strategies for crop plants.

results.title: Abiotic factors shape mosquito microbiomes that enhance host development
results.author: Nicola G Kriefall
