results.takeaway: Leaf detritus in larval habitats can shape mosquito microbiomes, affecting larval developmental success and potentially impacting the success of mosquito populations.

results.title: Phylosymbiosis: The Eco-Evolutionary Pattern of Insect–Symbiont Interactions
results.author: Man Qin
results.takeaway: Phylosymbiosis in insects occurs through a combination of stochastic and deterministic mechanisms, influenced by host phylogeny and microbial community dissimilarities.

results.title: Incidence and Diversity of Torix Rickettsia–Odonata Symbioses
results.author: Panupong Thongprem
results.takeaway: Torix Rickettsia is a common symbiosis in odonates, found in a wide range of species from both Europe and South America, with coinfection and vertical transmission likely.

results.title: Ecological context shapes microbial contributions to nutrition and development in Drosophila melanogaster
results.author: Elisabeth K. Riedel
results.takeaway: Ecological context influences microbial contributions to larval nutrition and development in Drosophila melanogaster, affecting larval development time and adult body weight.

results.title: Microplastic exposure across trophic levels: effects on the host–microbiota of freshwater organisms
results.author: J. Varg
results.takeaway: Exposure to microplastics and pesticides directly affects the microbiome of aquatic organisms, with indirect effects affecting higher trophic levels through diet.

results.title: Microbial ecology of sandflies—the correlation between nutrition, Phlebotomus papatasi sandfly development and microbiome
results.author: Slavica Vaselek
results.takeaway: Live and diverse microbiomes play a crucial role in the development and survival of sandflies, with higher mortality rates and longer larval development for larvae fed with depleted microbiome-depleted medium.

results.title: Dynamics of symbiotic bacterial community in whole life stage of Harmonia axyridis (Coleoptera: Coccinellidae)
results.author: Hui-Yong Xue
results.takeaway: The symbiotic bacterial composition of the ladybird beetle changes throughout its life cycle, with higher diversity in eggs and lower diversity in adults and pupae stages.

results.title: The origin and maintenance of microbial symbionts in Drosophila larvae
results.author: Robin Guilhot
results.takeaway: Drosophila larvae maintain stable associations with bacteria and yeasts, with both females and males transmitting these symbionts to the larvae, and some persist throughout the host life cycle and are transmitted to offspring.

results.title: Odonata: Who They Are and What They Have Done for Us Lately: Classification and Ecosystem Services of Dragonflies
results.author: M. May
results.takeaway: Dragonflies and damselflies provide aesthetic and spiritual benefits, may impact disease vectors and agricultural pests, and are important predators in aquatic ecosystems.

results.title: Odonata (dragonflies and damselflies) as a bridge between ecology and evolutionary genomics
results.author: S. Bybee
results.takeaway: Odonata (dragonflies and damselflies) provide a unique model for integrating evolutionary genomics with ecology, offering insights into insect evolution that are not easily gained from other animal groups.

results.title: Impact of Larval Food Source on the Stability of the Bactrocera dorsalis Microbiome
results.author: Vivek Kempraj
results.takeaway: Certain bacterial symbionts remain stable in female Bactrocera dorsalis flies across different larval diets, potentially aiding in developing novel management techniques against this pest.

results.title: Scientists' warning on the need for greater inclusion of dragonflies in global conservation
results.author: M. Samways
results.takeaway: Dragonflies play a crucial role in global conservation, facing threats such as habitat loss, climate change, and invasive alien species, and need greater inclusion in conservation policies and activities.

results.title: Episyrphus balteatus symbiont variation across developmental stages, living states, two sexes, and potential horizontal transmission from prey or environment
results.author: Xiao Chang
results.takeaway: Hoverflies and aphids share similar symbionts, with Proteobacteria and Firmicutes dominating their microbiomes, and potential horizontal transmission from prey or the environment.

results.title: Link of dragonflies and damselflies with the aquatic environment is differently expressed in adult and larval stages
results.author: E. Peeters
results.takeaway: Dragonfly larvae are better indicators of good water quality than damselfly larvae, while adult Odonata are weakly correlated with larval presence in a nearby water system.

results.title: A Review of the Density, Biomass, and Secondary Production of Odonates
results.author: A. Rivas-Torres
results.takeaway: Odonates contribute significantly to the exportation of matter and energy from aquatic to terrestrial ecosystems, with the highest biomass found in dragonflies of the Aeshnidae, Corduliidae, and Gomphidae families.

results.title: Biodiversity and ecology of dragonfly larvae in the plain zone of the Central Caucasus
results.author: Sergey Kozminov
results.takeaway: Dragonfly larvae's distribution and ecology in the Central Caucasus depend on factors such as aquatic ecosystems, natural food supply, and vegetation, suggesting their potential use as bioindicators for aquatic ecosystems and organic matter pollution.

results.title: Odonate predation as a factor influencing dytiscid beetle distribution and community structure.
results.author: D. Larson
results.takeaway: Odonate larvae predation contributes to the partitioning of dytiscid beetle distribution and community structure, with odonates dominating open and permanent sites while dytiscids prefer less stability and denser vegetation.

results.title: Dragon colors: the nature and function of Odonata (dragonfly and damselfly) coloration
results.author: C. M. Suárez‐Tovar
results.takeaway: Adult odonates exhibit a diverse range of colors, which serve as signals for mate choice, species recognition, and predator avoidance, as well as for physiological adaptations to abiotic conditions.

120.state: Insect-larva microbial and algal symbiosis is booming — larval versus adult microbiomes, Rickettsia in odonates, and algal and bacterial symbionts shaping larval development. The 1907 dragonfly-alga observation is an early instance of what is now a large field.

facets.q: Can a colour pattern induced by the larval environment be inherited in amphibians (transgenerational plasticity)?
How does rearing background colour affect pigment pattern formation in Salamandra?

results.title: The Midwife Toad Challenge After (Half) a Century
results.author: J. Arntzen
results.takeaway: Paul Kammerer's claim that environmentally induced character states are carried over to the next generation remains unconfirmed, but his experiment on fire salamander color pattern change has been confirmed.

results.title: Genetic and environmental drivers of colour and pattern in the Australian jacky dragon (Amphibolurus muricatus)
results.author: Rebecca S Raynal
results.takeaway: Heritable genetic factors and hatchling age are key drivers of coloration in the Australian jacky dragon, while thermal environment does not significantly contribute to variation in coloration.

results.title: The Genetic Basis of Adaptation following Plastic Changes in Coloration in a Novel Environment.
results.author: Ammon Corl
results.takeaway: Ancient plasticity in coloration facilitated initial survival in a lava environment, followed by genetic changes that modified the phenotype and aided adaptation to the new environment.

results.title:
Non-genetic inheritance of induced defense morphologies across multiple unexposed generations of
 Daphnia lumholtzi
results.author: Shannon N Snyder
results.takeaway: Non-genetic inheritance of predator-induced morphologies in Daphnia lumholtzi water fleas persists through multiple generations, highlighting the ecological significance of non-genetic inheritance in natural populations.

results.title: Family resemblance in color‐patch size is not affected by stress experience in a cichlid fish
results.author: Angelika Ziegelbecker
results.takeaway: The size of a yellow bar on a cichlid fish's body is strongly determined by genetic factors or shared maternal and brood environments, with limited environmental influences later in life.

results.title: Background matching through fast and reversible melanin-based pigmentation plasticity in tadpoles comes with morphological and antioxidant changes
results.author: H. C. Liedtke
results.takeaway: Tadpoles can rapidly and reversibly adjust their pigmentation to match environmental changes, leading to larger tail fins and improved redox status.

results.title: Fish and dragonfly nymph predators induce opposite shifts in color and morphology of tadpoles
results.author: Justin C. Touchon
results.takeaway: Tadpoles can shift both color and morphology in opposite, predator-specific directions in response to fish and dragonfly nymph predators, potentially resulting in genetic variation for developmental plasticity.

results.title: Predator-induced transgenerational plasticity in animals: a meta-analysis
results.author: Kirsty J Macleod
results.takeaway: Predator-induced transgenerational plasticity is strong in animals, but no clear evidence supports specific hypotheses about its strength, direction, or waning over ontogeny.

results.title: Plasticity across Generations
results.author: R. Bonduriansky
results.takeaway: Transgenerational plasticity can contribute to adaptive evolution, with both adaptive and non-adaptive forms, but caution is needed when inferring adaptive function.

results.title: MORPHOLOGY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF LARVAL COLOR VARIATION IN THE WHITE-LINED SPHINX MOTH, HYLES LINEATA
results.author: C. Francois
results.takeaway: The yellow and green coloration in larvae of the whitelined sphinx moth, Hyles lineata, is primarily controlled by one gene, with modifier genes interacting with it, and may provide an ecological advantage in changing environments.

results.title: Plastic background colour matching in the springbok mantis
results.author: N. Burke
results.takeaway: The springbok mantis exhibits strong developmental plasticity in coloration, with green backgrounds promoting all-green phenotypes and brown backgrounds producing mixed phenotypes, likely due to habitat diversity and sedentary lifestyle.

results.title: Adaptive colouration in amphibians.
results.author: Andreas Rudh
results.takeaway: Adaptive coloration in amphibians plays a role in thermoregulation, UV protection, predator avoidance, and sexual signaling, but many functions remain unexplored and few genes influencing pigmentation or patterning have been identified.

results.title: Transgenerational Plasticity in Human-Altered Environments
results.author: S. Donelan
results.takeaway: Human-induced rapid environmental change may negatively impact transgenerational plasticity, potentially leading to negative fitness outcomes for species.

results.title: Transgenerational Plasticity in Flower Color Induced by Caterpillars
results.author: M. Sobral
results.takeaway: Herbivory on wild radish plants leads to transgenerational effects on flower color, partly related to chromatin modifications.

results.title: Developmental Plasticity in Anurans: Meta-analysis Reveals Effects of Larval Environments on Size at Metamorphosis And Timing of Metamorphosis.
results.author: Molly A. Albecker
results.takeaway: Developmental plasticity in anurans is partly influenced by larval environments, with higher temperatures and lower water levels reducing mass at metamorphosis and less food and higher densities increasing the duration of the larval period.

results.title: Experimental Tests for Heritable Morphological Color Plasticity in Non-Native Brown Trout (Salmo trutta) Populations
results.author: Peter A. H. Westley
results.takeaway: Photypic plasticity, rather than heritable variation, is the most likely explanation for the variation in color intensity observed among non-native brown trout populations in nature.

results.title: Transgenerational Plasticity in the Context of Predator-Prey Interactions
results.author: J. Tariel
results.takeaway: Predator-induced transgenerational plasticity in metazoans allows prey to fine-tune their phenotype to the current risk of predation, with potential adaptive value and underlying mechanisms.

results.title: What coloration brings: Implications of background adaptation to oxidative stress in anurans
results.author: Tijana B. Radovanović
results.takeaway: Background color adaptation in amphibians can increase oxidative stress, but if the background color changes, defense systems decline and lipid peroxidation occurs.

results.title: Pigmentation plasticity enhances crypsis in larval newts: associated metabolic cost and background choice behaviour
results.author: Nuria Polo-Cavia
results.takeaway: Newt larvae exhibit considerable pigmentation plasticity, enhancing their concealment against predators regardless of perceived predation risk, but metabolic costs may constrain their camouflage in dim habitats.

results.title: Transgenerational plasticity research: Challenges and opportunities
results.author: C. McAndry
results.takeaway: Transgenerational plasticity research can advance our understanding of organismal evolution and climate change responses by clarifying terminology and integrating parallel processes.

results.title: The conspicuous postmetamorphic coloration of fire salamanders, but not their toxicity, is affected by larval background albedo.
results.author: Eugenia Sanchez
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Fire salamanders' black-and-yellow coloration is conspicuous to predators at night, but not their toxicity, as larval background albedo does not affect their alkaloid profile.

results.title: Factors determining the dorsal coloration pattern of aposematic salamanders
results.author: Benedetta Barzaghi
results.takeaway: Environmental conditions and food availability are more important than local adaptations in determining differences in aposematic coloration patterns of salamanders.

results.title: Pigmentation enhancement techniques during ornamental fish production
results.author: Cher Chien Lau
results.journal: Reviews in Fish Biology and Fisheries
results.takeaway: Feed additives and tank color and light are key factors in enhancing ornamental fish body pigmentation and patterns, offering valuable insights for boosting the breeding industry.

results.title: ADAPTIVE COLORATION AND GENE FLOW AS A CONSTRAINT TO LOCAL ADAPTATION IN THE STREAMSIDE SALAMANDER, AMBYSTOMA BARBOURI
results.author: Andrew Storfer
results.takeaway: Larvae of the streamside salamander, Ambystoma barbouri, tend to be lighter in permanent streams, less conspicuous to fish, and have higher survival, while those in ephemeral streams are darker and more conspicuous to predators.

results.title: Developmental costs of yellow colouration in fire salamanders and experiments to test the efficiency of yellow as a warning colouration
results.author: B. Caspers
results.takeaway: Yellow coloration in fire salamanders reduces predation risk, but its development is condition-dependent and potentially costly.

results.title: Fish skin pigmentation in aquaculture: the influence of rearing conditions and its neuroendocrine regulation.
results.author: P. Vissio
results.takeaway: Fish skin pigmentation in aquaculture is influenced by neuroendocrine regulation and rearing conditions, which impact its market value and welfare.

results.title: Does tank background colour influence the growth, survival, and carotenoid content in fishes? An illustration in filament barb, Dawkinsia filamentosa (Valenciennes, 1844)
results.author: N. Padhi
results.takeaway: Black background color is the best tank color for rearing and production of filament barb, with higher growth rate, survival rate, and carotenoid content compared to other tank colors.

results.title: Which pigment appears first in the corolla—patterned or background?
results.author: Eapsa Berry
results.takeaway: In 28 out of 35 studied species, pigment pattern appears in the corolla earlier than background color, with a tendency towards earlier appearance in the corolla.

results.title: Functional colour genes and signals of selection in colour‐polymorphic salamanders
results.author: J. Burgon
results.takeaway: European fire salamanders show functional associations between color morphs and genes, suggesting that current ecological and evolutionary hypotheses may need revision.

results.title: The Effect of Background Color on Skin Color Variation of Juvenile Plectropomus leopardus
results.author: Feibiao Song
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: A white background is more conducive to maintaining red skin color in juvenile Plectropomus leopardus, while black and red backgrounds cause darkening in response to other colors.

results.title: Editorial: Evo-Devo of Color Pattern Formation
results.author: R. Mallarino
results.takeaway: Advances in genomic, molecular, and imaging approaches reveal the mechanistic basis of color pattern formation in animal species, providing insights into evolutionary developmental biology and phenotypic diversity.

results.title: A methodological workflow for quantitative colouration and colour pattern comparison reveals taxonomic and habitat‐level differences in the polymorphic fire salamander (Salamandra salamandra)
results.author: L. Alarcón‐Ríos
results.takeaway: This study effectively detects differences in color patterns in the polymorphic fire salamander (Salamandra salamandra) at both taxonomic and habitat levels, revealing an increased prevalence of melanistic coloration and less homogeneous color pattern in urban settings.

results.title: Larval Pigmentation Reveals Environmental and Genetic Influences in Hybridizing Ambystoma Salamanders
results.author: Alfredo Ascanio
results.takeaway: Hybrid Ambystoma salamander larvae show intermediate UVR responses, with greater similarity to their maternal phenotype, suggesting potential performance reduction if intermediate pigmentation is suboptimal.

results.title: Blue, Black, and Stripes: Evolution and Development of Color Production and Pattern Formation in Lizards and Snakes
results.author: Takeo Kuriyama
results.takeaway: Lizards and snakes' color patterns are influenced by pigment cells and genetic factors during embryogenesis, improving our understanding of reptilian color pattern evolution.

results.title: Skin Colour in Salamanders Is Modulated by Both Epitranscriptomic Methylation and Gene Expression
results.author: Nicholas Strowbridge
results.takeaway: Multiple molecular mechanisms, including gene expression and RNA methylation, contribute to salamander color diversity, with a positive correlation and significant overlap in transcripts.

results.title: Effects of background color on pigmentation, morphological traits, and behavior in the European tree frog (Hyla arborea, Hylidae, Anura) tadpoles
results.author: Marko Mirc
results.takeaway: European tree frog tadpoles can rapidly change body pigmentation to achieve crypsis, with reversible color changes, and can actively choose an appropriate background for maximum crypsis.

results.title: Zebrafish Pigment Pattern Formation: Insights into the Development and Evolution of Adult Form.
results.author: Larissa B. Patterson
results.takeaway: Zebrafish pigment patterns are shaped by precise coordination of pigment cell lineage specification, differentiation, and cellular interactions, providing insights into pattern diversification in vertebrates.

results.title: EffEcts of background color and prEdation risk on color changE in firE salamandEr larvaE
results.author: O. Segev
results.takeaway: Fire salamander larvae rapidly change color to become less conspicuous against contrasting backgrounds, with predator presence further enhancing this response.

16.state: Whether an environmentally induced pattern can be inherited is today's transgenerational-plasticity debate: modern work shows non-genetic inheritance of induced morphologies (Daphnia) but stresses that Kammerer's specific claim is unconfirmed and that coloration is often strongly genetic. The core question is open and actively argued.

facets.q: Can environmental conditions alter the heritable germ line (transgenerational epigenetic inheritance)?
Is there evidence for soma-to-germline transmission of environmentally acquired traits?

results.title: Environmentally induced epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of disease
results.author: E. Nilsson
results.takeaway: Environmental factors, such as toxicants, abnormal nutrition, and stress, can promote epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of diseases, increasing disease susceptibility in subsequent generations.

results.title: Transgenerational Inheritance of Environmentally Induced Epigenetic Alterations during Mammalian Development
results.author: L. Legoff
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can induce parental alterations and affect offspring phenotypes through gametic epigenetic inheritance, influencing disease risk assessment.

results.title: Present and future challenges for the investigation of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance.
results.author: M. Fallet
results.takeaway: Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance (TEI) faces challenges in understanding molecular mechanisms and the downstream chain of events leading to persistent phenotypic changes.

results.title: Transgenerational inheritance: how impacts to the epigenetic and genetic information of parents affect offspring health.
results.author: M. J. Xavier
results.takeaway: Environmental factors, ageing, diet, and toxicant exposure can alter the epigenetic composition of parents, potentially affecting offspring's health and well-being.

results.title: Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: a critical perspective
results.author: A. Bird
results.takeaway: The evidence for environmentally induced epigenetic inheritance in mammals remains inconclusive, with genome sequencing strengthening the causal relationship between genotypes and organismal characteristics.

results.title: A paternal environmental legacy: Evidence for epigenetic inheritance through the male germ line
results.author: A. Soubry
results.journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology
results.takeaway: Paternal environmental factors can influence offspring's future health through epigenetic changes in the male germ line, potentially affecting DNA methylation, histone modification, and non-coding RNAs.

results.title: Inheritance of environment-induced phenotypic changes through epigenetic mechanisms
results.author: Yukiko Tando
results.takeaway: Parental environmental factors can cause epigenetic changes that alter the phenotypes of descendants through various mechanisms, including DNA methylation, histone modification, and small RNAs via metabolites.

results.title: Developmental origins of epigenetic transgenerational inheritance
results.author: M. Hanson
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can induce epigenetic alterations in germ cells during specific developmental windows, potentially affecting phenotypic variation and disease risk in various species.

results.title: Environmentally Induced Epigenetic Transgenerational Inheritance of Reproductive Disease
results.author: E. Nilsson
results.takeaway: Environmental factors, such as environmental toxins, can contribute to the transgenerational inheritance of reproductive disease susceptibility in various organisms, including humans.

results.title: Environmentally Induced Transgenerational Epigenetic Reprogramming of Primordial Germ Cells and the Subsequent Germ Line
results.author: M. Skinner
results.takeaway: Vinclozolin, an agricultural fungicide, alters transgenerational epigenetic reprogramming and function of the male germline, contributing to the epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of disease.

results.title: Transgenerational inheritance and its modulation by environmental cues
results.author: R. Verdikt

results.title: Environmental induced transgenerational inheritance impacts systems epigenetics in disease etiology
results.author: Daniel Beck
results.takeaway: Environmental toxicants promote transgenerational diseases like kidney, prostate, and testicular pathologies, with unique DNA methylation regions and genes influencing each exposure generational lineage.

results.title: Elusive inheritance: Transgenerational effects and epigenetic inheritance in human environmental disease
results.author: S. Martos
results.journal: Progress in biophysics and molecular biology
results.takeaway: Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance of environmental changes in mammals is rare, and its role in human environmental diseases remains elusive due to the complexity of the mammalian lifecycle and the difficulty of establishing a role for epigenetic inheritance.

results.title: Lamarck rises from his grave: parental environment‐induced epigenetic inheritance in model organisms and humans
results.author: Yan Wang
results.takeaway: Parental environment-induced epigenetic alterations can transmit across generations in various organisms, with sex-specific effects, and require further investigation for better understanding of gene-environment interactions.

results.title: Environmentally induced epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of phenotype and disease
results.author: C. Guerrero-Bosagna
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can alter germ line epigenomes, leading to epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of altered phenotypes and diseases.

results.title: Role of epigenetic transgenerational inheritance in generational toxicology
results.author: E. Nilsson
results.takeaway: Environmental toxicants can cause epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of disease, requiring new government policies to protect future generations.

results.title: Environmentally induced epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of sperm epimutations promote genetic mutations
results.author: M. K. Skinner
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can induce epigenetic inheritance that promotes genetic mutations, advancing our understanding of how the environment impacts disease and evolution.

results.title: Environmental epigenetic transgenerational inheritance and somatic epigenetic mitotic stability
results.author: M. Skinner
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can influence the epigenome, promoting stable phenotypes and playing a critical role in disease etiology and evolutionary biology.

results.title: Environmental signals and transgenerational epigenetics
results.author: M. Skinner
results.takeaway: Environmental factors and toxicants can promote epigenetic transgenerational inheritance of phenotypes and diseases, with limited impact on somatic cells and limited developmental periods.

results.title: Environmental Stress Drives Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance through miR-212/132 Expression Alterations
results.author: Yuancheng Luo
results.takeaway: Environmental stress in mice leads to transgenerational epigenetic inheritance through miR-212/132 expression alterations, potentially accelerating evolution in mammals.

results.title: Transgenerational epigenetics: Integrating soma to germline communication with gametic inheritance.
results.author: A. Sharma
results.takeaway: Parental gamete-borne epigenetic factors, particularly RNAs, influence offspring's phenotype, with potential implications in health, disease, and ageing.

results.title: Bubbling beyond the barrier: exosomal RNA as a vehicle for soma–germline communication
results.author: Daniel Phillips
results.takeaway: Somatic RNAs in extracellular vesicles mediate the communication of phenotypic information from the body to its gametes, reprogramming the offspring epigenome and phenotype.

results.title: Molecular insights into transgenerational inheritance of stress memory.
results.author: Qian Zhang
results.journal: Journal of genetics and genomics = Yi chuan xue bao
results.takeaway: Environmental stressors can influence future generations through germline-mediated epigenetic inheritance, with soma-to-germline signaling playing a crucial role in this process.

results.title: Environmental stresses induce transgenerationally inheritable survival advantages via germline-to-soma communication in Caenorhabditis elegans
results.author: Saya Kishimoto
results.takeaway: Environmental stresses in Caenorhabditis elegans lead to transgenerationally inheritable survival advantages through epigenetic alterations, providing offspring with survival advantages in dealing with environmental perturbations.

results.title: Soma-to-germline RNA communication
results.author: C. Conine
results.takeaway: Intercellular RNA trafficking from nurse cells or somatic tissues to developing gametes is crucial for epigenetic inheritance, challenging the distinction between germline and soma.

results.title: Bioinformatic analysis revealing association of exosomal mRNAs and proteins in epigenetic inheritance.
results.author: A. Sharma
results.takeaway: Exosomal mRNAs and proteins, like extracellular microRNAs, may mediate soma to germline information transfer in mammals, providing a "transgenerational systems biology" perspective.

results.title: Transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: focus on soma to germline information transfer.
results.author: A. Sharma
results.journal: Progress in biophysics and molecular biology
results.takeaway: Regulatory RNAs and hormones play a role in soma to germline information transfer in transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, with direct evidence in plants and circumstantial evidence in animals.

results.title: Roll over Weismann: extracellular vesicles in the transgenerational transmission of environmental effects.
results.author: Sally A. Eaton
results.takeaway: Extracellular vesicles shed by somatic cells may be a credible means for environmental experiences to cause transmissible epigenetic changes in the germline, leading to the inheritance of acquired traits.

results.title: Multi-omics data analysis supporting inheritance of acquired traits in humans
results.author: A. Sharma
results.takeaway: Exercise-induced DNA methylation changes in sperm support the biological plausibility of epigenetic inheritance in humans, representing transcriptional response to exercise in soma.

results.title: The third barrier to transgenerational inheritance in animals: somatic epigenetic resetting
results.author: Chee Kiang Ewe
results.takeaway: Somatic epigenetic resetting is a third barrier to transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in animals, limiting its ability to transmit acquired responses across generations.

results.title: Molecular insights into transgenerational non-genetic inheritance of acquired behaviours
results.author: J. Bohacek
results.takeaway: Non-genetic inheritance of acquired behaviors in mammals can be influenced by environmental factors, with potential mechanisms including epigenetic modifications and non-coding RNAs in male germ cells.

results.title: Introduction: epigenetic strategies in animals and plants.
results.author: P. Meyer
results.takeaway: Mobile regulatory RNAs and hormones play a role in transgenerational epigenetic inheritance in plants, while direct evidence for RNA-mediated information transfer in animals is lacking.

results.title: Microbiome‐Germline Interactions and Their Transgenerational Implications
results.author: Michael Elgart
results.takeaway: Gut bacteria interactions with the host's germline can potentially lead to the inheritance of acquired characteristics through soma-to-soma, soma-to-germline, and soma-germline-soma modes.

results.title: Still no evidence for transgenerational inheritance or absence of epigenetic reprogramming in the honey bee
results.author: Fernando G. Soley
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: In honey bees, DNA methylation patterns are stably transferred across generations, but the absence of epigenetic reprogramming suggests no widespread and important role for adaptive change in evolution.

results.title: An evolutionary process without variation and selection
results.author: L. Gabora
results.journal: Journal of the Royal Society Interface
results.takeaway: Self-other reorganization (SOR) may explain cumulative, adaptive change in domains like early life and cultural evolution, without the need for natural selection or variation.

results.title: The inheritance of acquired epigenetic variations.
results.author: E. Jablonka
results.takeaway: Acquired epigenetic variations can influence gene expression in the next generation, potentially affecting adaptive evolution and causing a type of "Lamarckian" inheritance.

results.title: Small RNA Shuffling between Murine Sperm and Their Cytoplasmic Droplets during Epididymal Maturation
results.author: Hetan Wang
results.takeaway: Sperm exchange small RNAs with cytoplasmic droplets rather than epididymosomes during epididymal maturation, suggesting caution in interpreting sperm gaining foreign small RNAs as a mechanism of epigenetic inheritance.

results.title: The Somatic Impact on Inheritance and the Germline Control of the Soma.
results.author: S. Ishikawa
results.takeaway: Recent studies challenge the traditional dogma of heredity and explore the control of heritable genome integrity in soma-germline circuits, impacting both somatic and reproductive aging.

results.title: Inheritance and maintenance of small RNA‐mediated epigenetic effects
results.author: Piergiuseppe Quarato
results.takeaway: Small RNAs play a significant role in transferring epigenetic information across generations, including traits acquired through the soma during an organism's life.

results.title: Stable Transmission of DNA Methylation Epimutations from Germlines to the Liver and Their Association with Fatty Liver Disease in Medaka
results.author: Sourav Chakraborty
results.takeaway: Environmental stressors like bisphenol A (BPA) can cause stable germline transmission of DNA methylation epimutations, leading to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease in medaka fish.

95.state: The institute's boldest thesis — that environment reaches the germ-plasm — is now the field of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: toxicants, diet and stress can alter the germline epigenome, though mammalian evidence remains debated and mechanisms unsettled. Przibram's framing anticipates the modern question.

facets.q: Does light wavelength influence pigment pattern formation in amphibians?
How do photic cues regulate melanophore pattern development?

results.title: Individual colour patches as multicomponent signals
results.author: G. Grether
results.takeaway: Multiple trait evolution theory can be applied to individual color patch components, allowing for a more comprehensive understanding of animal coloration.

results.title: Light-specific wavelengths differentially affect the exploration rate, opercular beat, skin color change, opsin transcripts, and the oxi-redox system of the longsnout seahorse Hippocampus reidi.
results.author: J. A. Souto-Neto
results.journal: Comparative biochemistry and physiology. Part A, Molecular & integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Yellow light increases melanin levels in juvenile seahorses, while blue and yellow wavelengths increase juvenile mortality rates, impacting their color change, growth, and survival rates.

results.title: Colouration matters in dull toads: ultraviolet adornment for ladies and agrochemicals fading effects
results.author: S. Tartu
results.takeaway: Spiny toads exhibit sexual dichromatism, with females reflecting more ultraviolet light than males, and agrochemical exposure can alter throat coloration, potentially impacting mate attractiveness.

results.title: Salamanders and other amphibians are aglow with biofluorescence
results.author: Jennifer Y. Lamb
results.takeaway: Biofluorescence is widespread in amphibians, with variations in patterning and primary wavelengths within the green light spectrum, potentially impacting their ecology and evolution.

results.title: Evolutionary analyses of visual opsin genes in frogs and toads: Diversity, duplication, and positive selection
results.author: Ryan K. Schott
results.takeaway: Anurans exhibit diverse visual systems with ecological factors and behavior driving changes in their visual systems, including visual opsin duplication in the African bullfrog.

results.title: Adaptive colouration in amphibians.
results.author: Andreas Rudh
results.takeaway: Adaptive coloration in amphibians plays a role in thermoregulation, UV protection, predator avoidance, and sexual signaling, but many functions remain unexplored and few genes influencing pigmentation or patterning have been identified.

results.title: A transcriptome analysis revealing the molecular mechanism of light intensity-driven color variation in the skin of Chinese giant salamander (Andrias davidianus)
results.author: Zhizhi Jing

results.title: Colour Confusion: Reviewing Ambiguities in the Identification and Classification of Chromatophore Deficiencies Among Amphibians
results.author: John Gould
results.takeaway: This review clarifies terminology and establishes standards for identifying and classifying color abnormalities in amphibians, addressing ambiguity and confusion in the literature.

results.title: The diversity of opsins in Lake Baikal amphipods (Amphipoda: Gammaridae)
results.author: P. Drozdova
results.takeaway: Lake Baikal amphipods have evolved a unique visual system, with middle and long wavelength-sensitive opsins, suggesting common triggers during periods of darkness during Lake Baikal's ice cover and melting.

results.title: Naturally occurring fluorescence in frogs
results.author: C. Taboada
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Fluorescence in tree frogs contributes to 18-29% of total emerging light in twilight and nocturnal scenarios, enhancing brightness and matching night vision in amphibians.

results.title: Developmental changes in the cone visual pigments of black bream Acanthopagrus butcheri.
results.author: J. Shand
results.takeaway: Black bream develop a sensitivity to longer wavelengths in their visual pigments, potentially adapting to a changing light environment in estuarine waters.

results.title: Anion sensitivity and spectral tuning of cone visual pigments in situ.
results.author: J. Kleinschmidt
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Long-wavelength-absorbing vertebrate visual pigments are anion sensitive, with Cl- and Br- anions redshifting their absorbance spectrum, and Lyotropic anions causing blue shifts.

results.title: THE EFFECT OF WAVELENGTH AND INTENSITY OF LIGHT ON THE PROPORTION OF PIGMENTS IN PORPHYRIDIUM CRUENTUM
results.author: M. Brody
results.takeaway: Controlling light intensity and wavelength can alter the concentrations of chlorophyll and phycoerythrin in Porphyridium cruentum, allowing for varying red and green pigment proportions.

results.title: Adaptive evolutionary paths from UV reception to sensing violet light by epistatic interactions
results.author: S. Yokoyama
results.takeaway: Epistatic interactions in short wavelength-sensitive pigments can enable animals to evolve from UV reception to violet light reception, improving visual resolution and subtle contrast detection.

results.title: Editorial: Evo-Devo of Color Pattern Formation
results.author: R. Mallarino
results.takeaway: Advances in genomic, molecular, and imaging approaches reveal the mechanistic basis of color pattern formation in animal species, providing insights into evolutionary developmental biology and phenotypic diversity.

results.title: Biofluorescent sexual dimorphism revealed in a southern Appalachian endemic salamander, Plethodon metcalfi
results.author: J. Cox
results.takeaway: Southern Gray-Cheeked Salamanders exhibit biofluorescent sexual dimorphism, potentially related to ventral modified granular glands used in plethodontid chemosensory communication.

results.title: Larval Pigmentation Reveals Environmental and Genetic Influences in Hybridizing Ambystoma Salamanders
results.author: Alfredo Ascanio
results.takeaway: Hybrid Ambystoma salamander larvae show intermediate UVR responses, with greater similarity to their maternal phenotype, suggesting potential performance reduction if intermediate pigmentation is suboptimal.

results.title: Genetic Basis of Spectral Tuning in the Violet-Sensitive Visual Pigment of African Clawed Frog, Xenopus laevis
results.author: Yusuke Takahashi
results.takeaway: The spectral tuning of African clawed frog violet-sensitive visual pigment is influenced by 13 critical amino acid sites, increasing the total number of critical sites in vertebrate SWS1 pigments.

results.title: Zebrafish Pigment Pattern Formation: Insights into the Development and Evolution of Adult Form.
results.author: Larissa B. Patterson
results.takeaway: Zebrafish pigment patterns are shaped by precise coordination of pigment cell lineage specification, differentiation, and cellular interactions, providing insights into pattern diversification in vertebrates.

results.title: Changes in the visual environment affect colour signal brightness and shoaling behaviour in a freshwater fish
results.author: J. Kelley
results.takeaway: Western rainbowfish exhibit increased brightness of red color patterns in environments rich in organic matter, potentially enhancing communication in altered visual environments.

results.title: Visual input regulates melanophore differentiation
results.author: K. Atkinson‐Leadbeater
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Reduced visual input in stage 40 larvae leads to rapid melanophore differentiation, with extra-pineal melatonin signaling promoting this process.

results.title: Genetics and evolution of pigment patterns in fish.
results.author: R. Kelsh
results.takeaway: Fish pigment patterns are shaped by local cues, chromatophore interactions, and cell-cell interactions, with potential for evolution through developmental genetics and theoretical modeling.

results.title: Interplay of Light, Melatonin, and Circadian Genes in Skin Pigmentation Regulation
results.author: G. Bertolesi
results.takeaway: Circadian cycle regulation, rather than proliferative capacity, alters melanization of melanophores, with melatonin playing a key role in this process.

results.title: Role of eyes and pineal gland in melanophore response in the green frog. Rana esculenta.
results.author: P. K. Saxena
results.takeaway: The green frog's melanophore response is influenced by the eyes and pineal gland, with the latter potentially playing a role in photic cues and melanin aggregating substance release.

results.title: In vitro analysis suggests that difference in cell movement during direct interaction can generate various pigment patterns in vivo
results.author: Hiroaki Yamanaka
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Different cell movements during direct interaction between melanophores and xanthophores in zebrafish can generate various pigment patterns, highlighting the importance of cell movements in pattern formation.

results.title: Rhabdomeric phototransduction initiated by the vertebrate photopigment melanopsin.
results.author: M. C. Isoldi
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Melanopsin in vertebrates initiates a similar light-activated signaling cascade to invertebrates, resulting in increased intracellular inositol triphosphate and melanosome dispersion.

results.title: Iridophores as a source of robustness in zebrafish stripes and variability in Danio patterns
results.author: A. Volkening
results.takeaway: Iridophores play a key role in zebrafish stripes and Danio patterns, enabling robust patterning and variability within the fish species.

results.title: How fish color their skin: A paradigm for development and evolution of adult patterns
results.author: C. Nüsslein-Volhard
results.takeaway: Fish color their skin through species-specific interactions among pigment cell types, influenced by direct cell contact and tissue environment cues.

results.title: Pigment Pattern Formation by Contact-Dependent Depolarization
results.author: Masafumi Inaba
results.takeaway: Contact-dependent depolarization of melanophores and xanthophores in zebrafish skin leads to migration, potentially explaining the stripe pattern in zebrafish.

results.title: Interactions with Iridophores and the Tissue Environment Required for Patterning Melanophores and Xanthophores during Zebrafish Adult Pigment Stripe Formation
results.author: Larissa B. Patterson
results.takeaway: Iridophores play a crucial role in organizing melanophores and xanthophores during the development of stripes and interstripes in zebrafish.

results.title: Pigment patterns of larval salamanders (Ambystomatidae, Salamandridae): the role of the lateral line sensory system and the evolution of pattern-forming mechanisms.
results.author: D. Parichy
results.takeaway: Larval salamanders' horizontal stripe patterns are influenced by the development of the trunk lateral line sensory system, with similar mechanisms being common and ancestral in both Ambystomatidae and Salamandridae families.

results.title: Agouti and BMP signaling drive a naturally occurring fate conversion of melanophores to leucophores in zebrafish
results.author: Delai Huang
results.takeaway: Local signals in zebrafish's fins drive the transformation of black pigment cells into white ones, resulting in white highlights and ornamentation.

results.title: Bar, stripe and spot development in sand-dwelling cichlids from Lake Malawi
results.author: Laura A. Hendrick
results.takeaway: Stripes in sand-dwelling cichlids develop through differentiation of melanophores along horizontal myosepta, while bars are supported by extensive migration of undifferentiated melanophores in the skin during metamorphosis.

results.title: Iridophore apoptosis mediates socially-regulated developmental color pattern plasticity in an anemonefish
results.author: Laurie J. Mitchell
results.takeaway: Social environment influences developmental color pattern plasticity in anemonefish, with iridophore apoptosis playing a key role in regulating color changes and potentially aiding in conflict avoidance during recruitment.

results.title: Homotypic cell competition regulates proliferation and tiling of zebrafish pigment cells during colour pattern formation
results.author: B. Walderich
results.takeaway: Homotypic cell competition plays a key role in regulating the proliferation and tiling of zebrafish pigment cells during color pattern formation.

results.title: The dynamic behavior of chromatophores marks the transition from bands to spots in leopard geckos
results.author: Asier Ullate-Agote
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Leopard geckos' skin coloration is influenced by melanophores-iridophores interactions, with melanophores autonomously forming black spots in adulthood, and a genetic mutation in the transcription factor plays a key role in skin pattern formation.

results.title: Formation of the adult pigment pattern in zebrafish requires leopard and obelix dependent cell interactions
results.author: Florian Maderspacher
results.takeaway: Leopard and obelix-dependent cell interactions drive the formation of the adult pigment pattern in zebrafish, with leopard regulating homotypic interaction within melanophores and xanthophores and obelix controlling boundary shape.

results.title: Testis development in the Japanese eel is affected by photic signals through melatonin secretion
results.author: Ji-Yeon Hyeon
results.takeaway: Japanese eels' testis development is influenced by photic signals through melatonin secretion, with new moon exposure leading to higher eye and plasma melatonin levels.

results.title: Effect of monochromatic light on melanophore arrangement in spotted snakehead fish Channa punctata (Bloch, 1793).
results.author: Bulbul Ali
results.takeaway: Monochromatic light significantly influences melanophore arrangement in spotted snakehead fish Channa punctata, with blue light being appropriate for overall species color and continuous black or white light causing severe damage.

15.state: How light wavelength acts on pigment-pattern formation is partly addressed — visual input regulates melanophore differentiation and specific wavelengths change melanin in juveniles — but a direct account of light shaping amphibian pattern is still open. The specific question is alive only at the edges.

facets.q: Can amphibians regenerate internal organs such as the lung?
What is the cellular basis of visceral organ regeneration in salamanders?

results.title: A comparative perspective on lung and gill regeneration
results.author: L. Cadiz
results.takeaway: Fish and amphibians' gill regeneration offers a unique opportunity to uncover common signaling mechanisms for lung regeneration in all vertebrates, potentially leading to new advances in tissue repair after lung disease.

results.title: The Xenopus respiratory system reveals common tetrapod mechanisms for growth, regeneration and healing
results.author: Shiri Kult Perry
results.takeaway: Xenopus tropicalis amphibians show regenerative capacity for lung tissue repair, using the same signaling pathways and stem cells as mammals during lung growth, regeneration, and healing.

results.title: Developmental switch from morphological replication to compensatory growth for salamander lung regeneration
results.author: Binxu Yin
results.takeaway: Salamanders repair injured lungs by promoting epithelial cell proliferation to strengthen remaining alveoli without rebuilding new ones, demonstrating distinct regenerative strategies during different stages of life.

results.title: Lung injury in axolotl salamanders induces an organ‐wide proliferation response
results.author: T. Jensen
results.takeaway: Lung injury in axolotl salamanders leads to organ-wide proliferation, resulting in recovery of lung mass and morphology by 56 days post-amputation.

results.title: Organ regeneration evolved in fish and amphibians in relation to metamorphosis: Speculations on a post-embryonic developmental process lost in amniotes after the water to land transition.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Annals of anatomy = Anatomischer Anzeiger : official organ of the Anatomische Gesellschaft
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration evolved in fish and amphibians due to metamorphosis, but was lost in amniotes after the water-to-land transition.

results.title: The cancer paradigms of mammalian regeneration: can mammals regenerate as amphibians?
results.author: R. Sarig
results.takeaway: Mammalian regeneration can be improved by inducing de-differentiation of mature cardiomyocytes, potentially avoiding malignant transformation and enhancing regeneration in non-regenerating organs like the heart.

results.title: Advances in signaling in vertebrate regeneration as a prelude to regenerative medicine.
results.author: Cristi L. Stoick-Cooper
results.takeaway: Understanding shared signaling pathways in vertebrate regeneration can accelerate the development of regenerative medicine therapies for organ and appendage regeneration in humans.

results.title: On the problem of regeneration of the lungs in caudate amphibia
results.author: B. P. Solopaev
results.journal: Bulletin of Experimental Biology and Medicine
results.takeaway: Adult crested newts can regenerate lungs, while larvae of Triturus cristatus and Amblystoma mexicanum show less success.

results.title: Limb and tail loss and regeneration in the Nauta Mushroomtongue Salamander (Bolitoglossa altamazonica) in the Madre de Dios Region, Peru
results.author: J. Oakley
results.takeaway: Nauta mushroomtongue salamanders (Bolitoglossa altamazonica) can regenerate limbs and tails, demonstrating unique regenerative capabilities in amphibians.

results.title: Reversibility of the differentiated state: regeneration in amphibians.
results.author: W. Straube
results.takeaway: Fish and amphibians can regenerate complex body structures and organs, demonstrating reversibility of the differentiated state.

results.title: Nerve-mediated FGF-signaling in the early phase of various organ regeneration.
results.author: Sakiya Yamamoto
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Nerve-mediated FGF-signaling plays a crucial role in the early phase of organ regeneration in amphibians, contributing to their high regeneration capacity.

results.title: Amphibian regeneration and mammalian cancer: Similarities and contrasts from an evolutionary biology perspective
results.author: B. Corradetti
results.takeaway: Adult mammals' susceptibility to cancer may be determined by the loss of advanced regenerative capability during evolution, potentially offering a new treatment route.

results.title: Regeneration in axolotls: a model to aim for!
results.author: Stéphane Roy
results.takeaway: Axolotls are unique model organisms for studying tissue regeneration and cancer resistance in vertebrates, offering potential insights for regenerative medicine in the 21st century.

results.title: Tail and Spinal Cord Regeneration in Urodelean Amphibians
results.author: Eleonora N Grigoryan
results.takeaway: Urodelean amphibians can regenerate their tail and spinal cord throughout their life, highlighting the importance of cell dedifferentiation and mutual molecular regulation in tissue regeneration.

results.title: Heart regeneration in adult Xenopus tropicalis after apical resection
results.author: Souqi Liao
results.takeaway: Adult frog hearts can regenerate perfectly and scar-free after injury, providing a powerful model for studying cardiac regeneration in adult mammals.

results.title: Genetic, Epigenetic, and Post-Transcriptional Basis of Divergent Tissue Regenerative Capacities Among Vertebrates
results.author: Sheamin Khyeam
results.takeaway: Divergent tissue regenerative capacities among vertebrates, such as fish and amphibians, are influenced by genetic, epigenetic, and post-transcriptional similarities.

results.title: Invited Letter. Organ regeneration occurs in vertebrates with aquatic-related life cycles including metamorphosis and was lost during land transition.
results.author: Lorenzo Alibardi
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration in amphibians and fish is influenced by their aquatic life cycle and evolutionary history, while in terrestrial vertebrates, wound healing and scarring are limited.

results.title: Lessons for cardiac regeneration from non-mammalian model organisms.
results.author: Y. Watanabe
results.takeaway: Non-mammalian species like amphibians and teleost fish can regenerate their hearts after severe damage, potentially reducing heart failure morbidity and mortality.

results.title: Induction of Vertebrate Regeneration by a Transient Sodium Current
results.author: A. Tseng
results.journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience
results.takeaway: A transient sodium current can restore regeneration in vertebrates, highlighting the importance of sodium transport regulation for organ repair.

results.title: Cancer in Amphibia, a rare phenomenon?
results.author: Esteban Torres-Dimas
results.takeaway: Amphibians show a relationship between regeneration and cancer resistance, with regenerating tissues showing a resistance to tumorigenesis.

results.title: Model systems for regeneration: salamanders
results.author: A. Joven
results.takeaway: Salamanders are key models for regeneration, demonstrating remarkable abilities to regrow tissues, organs, and even whole body parts, providing insights into cell, tissue, and organ regeneration mechanisms.

results.title: MAPK/ERK Pathway as a Central Regulator in Vertebrate Organ Regeneration
results.author: X. Wen
results.takeaway: The MAPK/ERK pathway plays a central role in vertebrate organ regeneration, underlying the differential regenerative capacities among species and offering potential applications in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

results.title: Heart regeneration in the salamander relies on macrophage-mediated control of fibroblast activation and the extracellular landscape
results.author: J. Godwin
results.takeaway: Macrophages play a crucial role in heart regeneration, and preventing fibrosis is a key therapeutic target for improving human cardiac repair outcomes.

results.title: Salamanders: The molecular basis of tissue regeneration and its relevance to human disease
results.author: C. M. Arenas Gómez
results.takeaway: Salamanders are a valuable model for studying tissue regeneration, which may lead to therapies for human diseases in the future.

results.title: Reading and editing the Pleurodeles waltl genome reveals novel features of tetrapod regeneration
results.author: Heng Wang
results.takeaway: The Pleurodeles waltl genome reveals novel features of tetrapod regeneration, including the co-expression of embryonic stem cell-specific miRNAs and Harbinger DNA transposons during limb regeneration.

results.title: The cellular and signaling dynamics of salamander limb regeneration.
results.author: Elad Bassat
results.takeaway: Salamanders can regenerate their limbs after limb amputation, involving multiple cell types and coordinated cell-cell communication and physical events.

results.title: Immunity in salamander regeneration: where are we standing and where are we headed?
results.author: L. Bolaños-Castro
results.journal: Developmental dynamics : an official publication of the American Association of Anatomists
results.takeaway: The immune system plays a critical role in salamander regeneration, but our understanding of its precise roles and functions remains limited.

results.title: Identification of the Adult Hematopoietic Liver as the Primary Reservoir for the Recruitment of Pro-regenerative Macrophages Required for Salamander Limb Regeneration
results.author: R. Debuque
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: The liver is the primary source of leukocytes recruited to regenerating limbs in adult salamanders, with splenic maturation being dispensable for macrophage education.

results.title: Positional Memory in Vertebrate Regeneration: A Century's Insights from the Salamander Limb.
results.author: L. Otsuki
results.journal: Cold Spring Harbor perspectives in biology
results.takeaway: Positional memory in vertebrate regeneration, as demonstrated in the salamander limb model, could lead to improved tissue repair and tissue engineering for therapeutic purposes.

results.title: Altered metabolic state impedes limb regeneration in salamanders
results.author: Zhengrong Peng
results.takeaway: Under extreme physiological stress, salamanders can exploit alternative strategies to secure blastema formation for limb regeneration, but limb outgrowth and patterning are significantly attenuated.

results.title: Putative epithelial–mesenchymal transitions during salamander limb regeneration: Current perspectives and future investigations
results.author: Ryan T. Kim
results.journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) plays a crucial role in salamander limb regeneration, regulating keratinocyte migration, epithelial cell invasion, and non-epithelial blastemal progenitor cell escape.

results.title: Salamanders aid research into mechanisms of non‐canonical organ regeneration for clinical applications
results.author: Binxu Yin
results.takeaway: Salamanders' remarkable regenerative capacities have expanded to include non-canonical organs, offering potential solutions to challenging clinical problems.

results.title: Salamander Insights Into Ageing and Rejuvenation
results.author: M. Yun
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Salamanders are valuable models for understanding the nature of organismal senescence and the interplay between regeneration and ageing, with their extraordinary longevity and indefinite regenerative potential.

results.title: Appendage Regeneration in Vertebrates: What Makes This Possible?
results.author: Valentina Daponte
results.takeaway: Understanding the similarities and differences in appendage regeneration in lizards, salamanders, and zebrafish can guide effective regenerative strategies in mammals.

results.title: Towards comparative analyses of salamander limb regeneration
results.author: Varun B. Dwaraka
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Comparative studies of salamander species are necessary to reveal commonalities and differences in limb regeneration mechanisms, providing insights into salamander evolution.

results.title: Research into the Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms of Regeneration in Salamanders: Then and Now
results.author: R. Debuque
results.takeaway: Salamanders' unique ability to regenerate tissues and organs offers a unique model for studying regenerative medicine, with modern molecular tools aiding in understanding regeneration mechanisms.

results.title: Salamander models for elucidating mechanisms of developmental biology, evolution, and regeneration: Part one
results.author: Catherine McCusker
results.takeaway: Salamanders provide valuable models for studying developmental biology, evolution, and regeneration, with diverse life cycles and habitats, potentially leading to regenerative therapies for humans.

results.title: The salamander blastema within the broader context of metazoan regeneration
results.author: Benjamin Tajer
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: The salamander limb blastema serves as a useful reference model for comparing regeneration processes across different animal species, aiding in our understanding of regeneration and aiding in the selection of regenerative model systems.

results.title: Senescent cells enhance newt limb regeneration by promoting muscle dedifferentiation
results.author: Hannah E. Walters
results.takeaway: Senescent cells can enhance newt limb regeneration by promoting muscle dedifferentiation, a key mechanism for regeneration of complex structures.

126.state: Internal-organ regeneration in amphibians is an active goal: salamanders and Xenopus repair lung tissue by epithelial proliferation, and shared fish/amphibian gill-lung signalling is mined for clues, with the capacity lost in amniotes. This intra-body lung-regeneration result is on the modern agenda.

facets.q: How determined or plastic is the tail regeneration blastema in reptiles?
What is the cellular composition and positional identity of the lizard tail regeneration blastema?

results.title: The regenerating tail of lizard transits through a tumour‐like stage represented by the regenerative blastema
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration involves a tumor-like stage called the regenerative blastema, which self-regulates to produce a new tail within 1 to 2 months, offering insights into cancer growth regulation.

results.title: Review. Regeneration of the tail in lizards appears regulated by a balanced expression of oncogenes and tumor suppressors.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Annals of anatomy = Anatomischer Anzeiger : official organ of the Anatomische Gesellschaft
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration is regulated by a balanced expression of oncogenes and tumor suppressors, with no malignant transformation observed during cell movements.

results.title: The regeneration blastema of lizards: an amniote model for the study of appendage replacement
results.author: E. Gilbert
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration involves a blastema-mediated process, resulting in a functional but non-identical replacement after tail loss, with diverse regenerative mechanisms among different species.

results.title: Ultrastructural analysis of early regenerating lizard tail suggests that a process of dedifferentiation is involved in the formation of the regenerative blastema
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Dedifferentiation from adult cells and stem cell multiplication contribute to the formation of regenerating lizard tail blastema after tail loss by autotomy.

results.title: A novel amniote model of epimorphic regeneration: the leopard gecko, Eublepharis macularius
results.author: K. McLean
results.takeaway: Leopard geckos exhibit a highly conserved pattern of epimorphic regeneration, with a latent period of scar-free healing and regeneration occurring through blastema-mediated cell proliferation.

results.title: Review: The Regenerating Tail Blastema of Lizards as a Model to Study Organ Regeneration and Tumor Growth Regulation in Amniotes
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration uses signaling pathways similar to tumor growth, providing a unique model for studying organ regeneration and tumor growth regulation in amniotes.

results.title: Microscopical observations on the regenerating tail of tsinling dwarf skink (Scincella tsinlingensis).
results.author: Chun Yang
results.takeaway: Tail regeneration in Scincella tsinlingensis lizards occurs through a seven-stage process, with faster growth in summer and autumn.

results.title: Regeneration in Reptiles Generally and the New Zealand Tuatara in Particular as a Model to Analyse Organ Regrowth in Amniotes: A Review
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Reptiles, particularly the New Zealand tuatara, can repair tail injuries through a combination of wound healing and continuous growth, providing valuable information for improving organ regeneration in endothermic amniotes.

results.title: Immunolocalization of tumor suppressors arhgap28 and retinoblastoma in the lizard Podarcis muralis suggests that they contribute to the regulated regeneration of the tail
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Arhgap28 and retinoblastoma proteins contribute to regulated tail regeneration in lizards, with their localization in various tissues suggesting their involvement in tissue differentiation.

results.title: Cartilage and Muscle Cell Fate and Origins during Lizard Tail Regeneration
results.author: R. Londono
results.takeaway: Differentiated cartilage and muscle cells contribute to regenerated muscle and cartilage tissues in mourning gecko lizards, suggesting dedifferentiation and/or transdifferentiation are partially responsible for their remarkable regenerative ability.

results.title: Blood vessel formation during tail regeneration in the leopard gecko (Eublepharis macularius): The blastema is not avascular
results.author: Samantha L. Payne
results.takeaway: Leopard gecko tail regeneration involves the formation of a hierarchical network of blood vessels, with a dynamic interplay between pro-angiogenic and anti-angiogenic proteins.

results.title: Histochemical, Biochemical and Cell Biological aspects of tail regeneration in lizard, an amniote model for studies on tissue regeneration.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizards show high regenerative power in tail regeneration, with stem cells playing a key role in the process, and future molecular studies could focus on specific gene activation in regeneration processes in amniotes and humans.

results.title: Lizard tail skeletal regeneration combines aspects of fracture healing and blastema-based regeneration
results.author: Thomas P. Lozito
results.takeaway: Lizard tail skeletal regeneration involves proximal cartilage tubes derived from vertebra periosteal cells and distal tubes derived from blastema cells, with differences in cell sources and signaling.

results.title: Gene expression in regenerating and scarring tails of lizard evidences three main key genes (wnt2b, egfl6, and arhgap28) activated during the regulated process of tail regeneration
results.author: M. Degan
results.takeaway: Three key genes (wnt2b, egfl6, and arhgap28) play a crucial role in regulating lizard tail regeneration after cauterization, affecting cell proliferation, movement, and immune response.

results.title: Spinal ganglia and peripheral nerves innervating the regenerating tail and muscles of lizards
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration is mainly sustained by the interaction of the apical ependyma with the wound epidermis, with regenerating nerves contributing to muscle regeneration and neurotrophic stimulation promoting cell proliferation.

results.title: Tail regeneration in Lepidosauria as an exception to the generalized lack of organ regeneration in amniotes.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Lepidosaurians, lizards, have evolved tail regeneration as an exception to the general lack of organ regeneration in amniotes, due to the loss of metamorphosis genes and an effective immune system.

results.title: Perspective: Appendage regeneration in amphibians and some reptiles derived from specific evolutionary histories.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Amphibian regeneration and lizard regeneration are derived from specific evolutionary histories, and regenerative medicine could potentially induce limb regeneration in humans by creating soft, hyaluronate-rich blastemas.

results.title: Transcriptome analysis of the regenerating tail vs. the scarring limb in lizard reveals pathways leading to successful vs. unsuccessful organ regeneration in amniotes
results.author: N. Vitulo
results.takeaway: Lizards successfully regenerate their tail but not their limbs, highlighting the importance of controlling cell proliferation and inflammation for successful organ regeneration.

results.title: De Novo Transcriptome Sequencing and Analysis of Differential Gene Expression among Various Stages of Tail Regeneration in Hemidactylus flaviviridis
results.author: Sonam Patel
results.takeaway: Stage-wise transcriptome analysis reveals molecular signaling pathways and cellular processes involved in lizard tail regeneration, potentially benefiting other amniotes.

results.title: Probe Sequencing Analysis of Regenerating Lizard Tails Indicates Crosstalk Among Osteoclasts, Epidermal Cells, and Fibroblasts
results.author: Darian J Gamble
results.takeaway: Crosstalk between osteoclasts and fibroblasts regulates blastema and epidermis formation during lizard tail regeneration.

results.title: Autoradiography and inmmunolabeling suggests that lizard blastema contains arginase-positive M2-like macrophages that may support tail regeneration.
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Annals of anatomy = Anatomischer Anzeiger : official organ of the Anatomische Gesellschaft
results.takeaway: M2-like macrophages in lizard tail blastema support tail regeneration, potentially making it an immuno-privileged organ where cell proliferation and growth occur without tumorigenic outgrowth.

results.title: Single-cell analysis of lizard blastema fibroblasts reveals phagocyte-dependent activation of Hedgehog-responsive chondrogenesis
results.author: Ariel C Vonk
results.takeaway: Lizard blastema fibroblasts activate Hedgehog-responsive chondrogenesis through phagocyte-induced gene activations, offering potential targets for regeneration in other species, including humans.

results.title: Immunoreactivity for Dab2 and Foxp3 suggests that immune‐suppressive cells are present in the regenerating tail blastema of lizard
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration occurs in immune-tolerant conditions, with immune-suppressive cells present in the regenerating blastema, supporting the hypothesis that organ regeneration in vertebrates can only occur in an immune-suppressed or immune-evasive environment.

results.title: Immunolocalization of cell proliferation and tumor markers in the regenerating tail of the lizard Podarcis muralis likely involved in cell proliferation control.
results.author: Lorenzo Alibardi

results.title: Tail regeneration reduction in lizards after repetitive amputation or cauterization reflects an increase of immune cells in blastemas
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: An increase in inflammation and immune reactions, rather than regeneration, is the main cause for organ regeneration failure in lizards after repetitive amputation or cauterization.

results.title: Immunolocalization indicates that both original and regenerated lizard tail tissues contain populations of long retaining cells, putative stem/progenitor cells
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Lizard tail regeneration is likely sustained by stem/progenitor cells in the stump after amputation, with the differentiation span of both white and red blood cells lasting longer than 4 weeks.

results.title: Temporal distribution of 5BrdU‐labelled cells suggests that most injured tissues contribute proliferating cells for the regeneration of the tail and limb in lizard
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Most injured tissues contribute proliferating cells for the regeneration of tail and limb in lizards, with a scarring outgrowth formed when stump tissues are damaged.

results.title: Epithelial–mesenchymal transition contrast in the amputated tail and limb of the northern house gecko, Hemidactylus flaviviridis
results.author: Pooja Raval
results.takeaway: The northern house gecko's amputated tail regenerates, while the severed limb fails to regrow, due to differences in epithelial-mesenchymal transition processes and epithelial cell characteristics.

results.title: SnoRNAs may accelerate protein synthesis for the rapid growth of the regenerating tail blastema in the lizard Podarcis muralis
results.author: M. Degan
results.takeaway: SnoRNAs may accelerate protein synthesis and rapid growth of the regenerating tail blastema in lizards, potentially playing a key role in regeneration.

8.state: The reptilian regeneration blastema is now characterized in detail: lizard-tail regrowth proceeds through a self-regulating, tumour-like blastema that balances oncogenes and tumour suppressors and draws on dedifferentiation and stem cells, yielding a functional but imperfect replacement. Przibram's potency-testing question is exactly what this field asks.

facets.q: Can reptiles regenerate jaw and craniofacial bone, and what mechanisms drive craniofacial regeneration?
Is jaw or craniofacial regeneration possible in vertebrates beyond the tail and limb?

results.title: Wnt Signaling and Its Contribution to Craniofacial Tissue Homeostasis
results.author: Xing Yin
results.takeaway: Wnt signaling plays a crucial role in maintaining and healing craniofacial tissues, with potential therapeutic applications in accelerating healing through activation of endogenous stem cells.

results.title: Regeneration of Bone, Cartilage, and Tooth Following Lower Jaw Amputation in Newts.
results.author: Kento Tsubosaki
results.takeaway: Adult newts can regenerate their lower jaws, restoring bone, cartilage, and teeth after substantial loss, potentially benefiting human jaw reconstruction strategies.

results.title: Exploring the Use of Animal Models in Craniofacial Regenerative Medicine: A Narrative Review
results.author: S. A. Mosaddad
results.takeaway: Animal models can effectively assess the efficacy and safety of new therapeutic interventions in craniofacial tissue engineering, benefiting both human and veterinary medicine.

results.title: Tissue regeneration in dentistry: Can salamanders provide insight?
results.author: Fadi Sader
results.takeaway: Studying animals like Axolotls may help stimulate regeneration in humans, offering insights into why some treatments are successful and others fail in regenerating specific tissues like bones.

results.title: EGR1 Promotes Craniofacial Bone Regeneration via Activation of ALPL⁺PDGFD⁺ Periosteal Stem Cells
results.author: Yang Li
results.takeaway: EGR1 plays a crucial role in activating ALPL+PDGFD+ periosteal stem cells, promoting craniofacial bone regeneration by regulating BMP signaling and activating these cells in response to injury.

results.title: Regenerative Models for the Integration and Regeneration of Head Skeletal Tissues
results.author: Warren A. Vieira
results.takeaway: Fish and amphibians show remarkable regenerative capabilities in response to jaw injury, offering potential biological targets for improved jaw reconstructive surgery outcomes.

results.title: Axolotl mandible regeneration occurs through mechanical gap closure and a shared regenerative program with the limb
results.author: J. Kramer
results.takeaway: Axolotls can regenerate their lower jaws in 90 days, sharing molecular phases with limb regeneration, bringing us closer to understanding similar lesions in human mandibles.

results.title: Tooth regeneration in animals. A systematic review
results.author: José Bartolomé-Lechuga
results.journal: Medicina Oral, Patología Oral y Cirugía Bucal
results.takeaway: Dental regeneration in animals has advanced, but complete tooth regeneration remains elusive, with key genes and cellular sources still needed for future clinical applications.

results.title: DPSC-Derived Extracellular Vesicles Promote Rat Jawbone Regeneration
results.author: A.E. Lee
results.takeaway: DPSC-derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) promote jawbone regeneration and may represent a safe cell-free therapeutic approach for craniofacial bone regeneration.

results.title: Craniofacial Bone Tissue Engineering: Current Approaches and Potential Therapy
results.author: A. Aghali
results.takeaway: Advances in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine show promising results in regenerating critical-size cranial defects, with potential therapies including Megakaryocytes and BMP-2.

results.title: microRNA function in Craniofacial Bone Formation, Regeneration and Repair
results.author: L. Hong
results.takeaway: MicroRNAs play a crucial role in regulating craniofacial bone formation, regeneration, and repair, offering potential for treating genetic anomalies and traumatic injuries.

results.title: Mechanoresponsive stem cells acquire neural crest fate in jaw regeneration
results.author: R. C. Ransom
results.takeaway: Mechanoresponsive stem cells in the skeleton activate neural crest fate in jaw regeneration, enabling robust tissue growth and stem-cell-based regeneration of adult skeletal tissue.

results.title: Craniofacial and Long Bone Development in the Context of Distraction Osteogenesis
results.author: Harsh N. Shah
results.takeaway: Distraction osteogenesis effectively regenerates bone, addressing various craniofacial and appendicular skeletal deficiencies, but the molecular mechanisms underlying bone regeneration remain elusive.

results.title: Endogenous Mechanisms of Craniomaxillofacial Repair: Toward Novel Regenerative Therapies
results.author: H. desJardins-Park
