results.takeaway: Co-operative Bmp and Fgf signaling can transform skin wound healing into limb formation in urodele amphibians, potentially contributing to the development of novel treatments for organ regeneration.

results.title: Establishment of neural stem cell culture from the central nervous system of the Iberian ribbed newt Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: Ryohei Seki-Omura
results.takeaway: The Iberian ribbed newt Pleurodeles waltl can produce neural stem cells, potentially revealing key genes and signaling pathways essential for spinal cord regeneration.

results.title: Cooperative inputs of Bmp and Fgf signaling induce tail regeneration in urodele amphibians.
results.author: Aki Makanae
results.takeaway: Cooperative inputs of Bmp and Fgf signaling induce tail regeneration in urodele amphibians, suggesting a common mechanism for organ regeneration in urodele amphibians.

results.title: An approach for elucidating dermal fibroblast dedifferentiation in amphibian limb regeneration
results.author: A. Satoh
results.takeaway: B2FF treatment induces multipotent dermal fibroblasts in amphibian limb regeneration, with PDE4B signaling playing a crucial role in this process.

results.title: Salamander models for elucidating mechanisms of developmental biology, evolution, and regeneration: Part two
results.author: Catherine McCusker
results.takeaway: This issue highlights the importance of consistent gene and transgenic nomenclature in salamander research, as well as the role of TGF-b and BMP signaling in limb regeneration and wound healing.

results.title: A quantitative analysis of 3D-cell distribution in regenerative muscle-skeletal system with synchrotron X-ray computed microtomography
results.author: M. Tesařová
results.takeaway: Synchrotron X-ray computed microtomography and chemical contrasting can accurately analyze 3D-cell distribution in developing muscle-skeletal systems, aiding in understanding anisotropic growth and skeletal shape.

results.title: Newts can normalize duplicated proximal–distal disorder during limb regeneration
results.author: Kazuki Koriyama
results.takeaway: Newt limbs can normalize duplicated positional disorder during limb regeneration by erasing a part of the fate in blastemas.

results.title: Developmental switch from morphological replication to compensatory growth for salamander lung regeneration
results.author: Binxu Yin
results.takeaway: Salamanders repair injured lungs by promoting epithelial cell proliferation to strengthen remaining alveoli without rebuilding new ones, demonstrating distinct regenerative strategies during different stages of life.

results.title: Development of an AI-Assisted Embryo Selection System Using Iberian Ribbed Newts for Embryo-Fetal Development Toxicity Testing.
results.author: Naofumi Saiki
results.takeaway: AI image analysis significantly enhances the accuracy and reproducibility of embryo selection in Iberian ribbed newt developmental toxicity testing, providing a reliable method for evaluating pharmaceuticals' developmental toxicity.

results.title: Photoperiod‐independent testicular development in the model newt Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: Mitsuki Kyakuno
results.takeaway: The model newt Pleurodeles waltl shows a suitable model for studying testicular development and spermatogenesis, with spermatogenesis proceeding irrespective of day length.

results.title: Invention sharing is the mother of developmental biology (part 4)
results.author: H. Ogino
results.takeaway: This special issue features four methods and protocols for studying developmental biology, highlighting the importance of sharing inventions for better research outcomes.

results.title: Iberian ribbed newts.
results.author: Andrew M. M. Matheson
results.takeaway: Iberian ribbed newts exhibit remarkable physiological and behavioral plasticity to adapt to both land and water environments, requiring adaptation to diverse environments.

results.title: Mutagenesis in Newts: Protocol for Iberian Ribbed Newts.
results.author: Toshinori Hayashi
results.takeaway: TALEN-mediated gene targeting in Iberian ribbed newts efficiently disrupts targeted genes in embryos, offering a unique model for regenerative biology research.

results.title: Standardized gene and genetic nomenclature for the newt Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: M. Yun
results.takeaway: Standardized gene and genetic nomenclature for the Iberian ribbed newt, Pleurodeles waltl, is recommended for the research community.

results.title: Biomechanical analysis of tendon regeneration capacity of Iberian ribbed newts following transection injury: Comparison to a mouse model
results.author: F. Sato
results.takeaway: Iberian ribbed newts show remarkable tendon regeneration capacity after injury, making them a suitable model for studying tendon regeneration in adult vertebrates.

results.title: Biomechanical properties of regenerated digital flexor tendon in immature newt following complete transection
results.author: Yu Masuda-Otsuka
results.takeaway: Immature Iberian ribbed newts can serve as experimental models for adult tendon regeneration research, with regenerated tendon strength improving and collagen structure becoming more organized after 12 weeks.

results.title: Gene manipulation for regenerative studies using the Iberian ribbed newt, Pleurodeles waltl.
results.author: Toshinori Hayashi
results.takeaway: The Iberian ribbed newt (Pleurodeles waltl) is a suitable model animal for regenerative studies, providing a unique experimental system for molecular genetics to clarify regeneration mechanisms and gene functions.

results.title: Cell Tracing by a Multicolor Reporter Transgenic Iberian Ribbed Newt Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: Shin-ichiro Hayashi
results.takeaway: The multicolor reporter Brainbow3.0 effectively traces cellular processes during organ regeneration in transgenic Iberian ribbed newts, providing valuable insights for various regeneration research fields.

results.title: Standardized gene and genetic nomenclature for the newt Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: M. Yun
results.takeaway: Adopting the axolotl nomenclature guidelines for Pleurodeles waltl will facilitate cross-species analysis, improve database navigation, and enhance gene annotation within the salamander research community.

results.author: F. J. Zamora‐Camacho
results.takeaway: The Iberian ribbed newt's antipredator defenses, including wart color and rib response time, are coordinated and interrelated, with faster newts showing less warts and slower rib response, suggesting a trade-off between both defenses.

results.title: Unique TCR loci architecture in newts: TRA and TRD separation in Pleurodeles waltl
results.author: M. Migalska
results.takeaway: The Iberian ribbed newt has unique TCR loci architecture, with the TRA and TRD loci residing on separate chromosomes, suggesting an ancestral feature and strengthening its position as a model species in amphibian immunology.

151.state: Pleurodeles waltl became a standard regeneration and developmental model — its genome and transcriptome are sequenced and it is used with modern molecular-genetic tools and in spaceflight studies. The husbandry work that helped establish it goes uncited.

facets.q: Does developmental rearing temperature set limb and tail proportions in mammals (Allen's rule)?
What is the developmental mechanism linking temperature to extremity length in endotherms?

results.title: Developmental Plasticity in Response to Temperature Aligns with Allen’s but not Bergmann’s Rule
results.author: Rachel E Bockrath

results.title: Temperature-dependent Developmental Plasticity and Its Effects on Allen’s and Bergmann’s Rules in Endotherms
results.author: Joshua K. R. Tabh
results.takeaway: Developmental plasticity drives Bergmann's and Allen's rules in endotherms, with non-linear contributions based on energy efficiency and thermal advantages at given ambient temperatures.

results.title: Allen's Rule Revisited: Temperature Influences Bone Elongation During a Critical Period of Postnatal Development
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.takeaway: Temperature significantly influences bone elongation during a critical period of early postnatal development, with the skeleton being most responsive to external stimuli during this period.

results.title: and its
results.author: Joshua K. R. Tabh
results.takeaway: Developmental plasticity drives James' and Allen's rules in endotherms, but their impact on species persistence depends on energy efficiency and thermal advantages at given temperatures.

results.title: A global test of Allen’s rule in rodents
results.author: B. H. Alhajeri
results.takeaway: Allen's rule is confirmed in rodents only for tails, with a positive relationship between tail length and cold temperatures, driven by adaptation to cold temperatures and habitat type.

results.title: Small mammals have big tails in the tropics
results.author: J. Alroy
results.takeaway: Small mammals have larger tails in the tropics, potentially related to increased arboreality and stability in tree branches.

results.title: Temperature regulates limb length in homeotherms by directly modulating cartilage growth
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Temperature indirectly influences limb length in mammals by affecting appendage temperature, rather than vascular nutrient delivery.

results.title: Limb proportions show developmental plasticity in response to embryo movement
results.author: A. Pollard
results.takeaway: Embryonic movement during prenatal development can alter limb proportions and regulate bone growth, potentially affecting post-hatching performance in animals.

results.title: What makes a long tail short? Testing Allen's rule in the toque macaques of Sri Lanka
results.author: M. Huffman
results.takeaway: Toque macaque tail length decreases with elevation and lower temperature, supporting Allen's rule and indicating adaptation to climate in this species.

results.title: Regionally specific and highly pleiotropic loci together mediate skeletal evolution in tropical and temperate house mice
results.author: Sylvia M. Durkin

results.title: The application of Bergmann's and Allen's rules to the poikilotherms
results.author: C. Ray
results.takeaway: Bergmann's and Allen's Rules apply to most warm-blooded animals, but their significance in heat conservation remains unclear.

results.title: Allometry to evaluate Allen's rule in climate warming.
results.author: Simone Santoro
results.takeaway: Allometry can help evaluate Allen's rule, which predicts a greater proportion of limbs and appendages relative to body size in warmer climates.

results.title: Challenging ecogeographical rules: Phenotypic variation in the Mountain Treeshrew (Tupaia montana) along tropical elevational gradients
results.author: A. Hinckley
results.takeaway: The Mountain Treeshrew's morphological variation is driven by factors other than temperature, suggesting local adaptation and a complex relationship with elevation in Borneo's tropical mountain system.

results.title: Biological Mechanisms for Allen's Rule: DNA Methylation as Mediator of the Association Between In Utero Exposure to Environmental Heat and Tibial Growth in Childhood
results.author: B. Straight
results.takeaway: DNA methylation changes mediate the association between in utero environmental heat exposure and childhood tibial growth in Kenyan Samburu pastoralist children.

results.title: An interspecific assessment of Bergmann’s rule in 22 mammalian families
results.author: Jostein Gohli
results.takeaway: Factors other than latitude and temperature are the major drivers of body mass evolution at the family level in mammals.

results.title: Allen's Rule in North American rabbits (Sylvilagus) and Hares (Lepus) is an Exception, Not a Rule
results.author: R. Stevenson
results.takeaway: Allen's rule has limited applicability in North American rabbits and hares, as ear and tail length in Lepus hares correlates positively with air temperature, but not in Sylvilagus rabbits.

results.title: Population history and ecology, in addition to climate, influence human stature and body proportions
results.author: Emma Pomeroy
results.takeaway: Population history and ecology, along with climate, influence human stature and body proportions, with population history explaining slightly more variation than environmental factors.

results.title: Altitudinal Variation of Limb Size of a High-Altitude Frog
results.author: Xueyan Liu
results.takeaway: Limb size of Rana kukunoris frogs declines with altitude, but this pattern varies geographically, with no significant variation in the southern region.

results.title: The validity of ecogeographical rules is context-dependent: testing for Bergmann's and Allen's rules by latitude and elevation in a widespread Andean duck
results.author: Natalia Gutiérrez‐Pinto
results.journal: Biological Journal of The Linnean Society
results.takeaway: The effect of temperature on morphology in Andean ducks varies depending on the environmental context, with Bergmann's rule being more valid across latitudes and Allen's rule being less valid along elevational gradients.

results.title: Allometry reveals trade-offs between Bergmann’s and Allen’s rules, and different avian adaptive strategies for thermoregulation
results.author: Arkadiusz Frӧhlich
results.takeaway: Birds' body size and shape are products of an evolutionary compromise, with larger birds having larger beak and tarsus lengths and smaller birds having smaller tarsus lengths.

results.title: Temperature, size and developmental plasticity in birds
results.author: B. Weeks
results.takeaway: Developmental plasticity may play a significant role in warming-driven size reductions in birds, with insulin-like growth factors potentially playing a key role.

results.title: Environmental Temperature Impact on Bone and Cartilage Growth
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.takeaway: Warm ambient temperature can lead to longer limbs in homeotherms, potentially aiding in thermal physiology and influencing human bone elongation disorders.

results.title: Sex and early-life conditions shape telomere dynamics in an ectotherm
results.author: Alexander Hansson
results.takeaway: Developmental temperature and sex influence early-life telomere dynamics in Lacerta agilis, with females showing longer telomeres and greater maintenance compared to males.

results.title: Bergmann's rule: Why does body size increase with latitude?
results.author: Kurt M. Ongman
results.takeaway: Burrowing owl body size increases with latitude, reflecting both acute and evolutionary responses to resource availability and thermoregulatory mechanisms, depending on the trait's plasticity or fixedness during adulthood.

results.title: ECTOTHERMS FOLLOW THE CONVERSE TO BERGMANN'S RULE
results.author: Timothy A Mousseau
results.takeaway: Ectotherms generally decrease in body size with decreasing mean temperature, but temperature-induced variation in cell size is not likely to explain observed patterns of body size variation along latitudinal and altitudinal clines in invertebrates.

results.title: Temperature Effects on Force and Actin–Myosin Interaction in Muscle: A Look Back on Some Experimental Findings
results.author: K. Ranatunga
results.takeaway: Warming active muscle significantly increases force, with a half-maximum at 10°C, and the sigmoidal temperature dependence of force is due to the endothermic nature of crossbridge force generation in different muscle types.

results.title: Osteological development of the vertebral column and of the fins in Pagellus erythrinus (L. 1758). Temperature effect on the developmental plasticity and morpho-anatomical abnormalities
results.author: D. Sfakianakis
results.takeaway: Temperature affects the developmental rate of Pagellus erythrinus' skeleton, with temperature-induced plasticity causing up to 10.9% differentiation in total length and 75% occurrence of caudal fin abnormalities.

results.title: Higher temperatures during development reduce body size in the zebra finch in the laboratory and in the wild
results.author: S. C. Andrew
results.takeaway: Higher temperatures during development reduce body size in zebra finches, potentially driving significant changes in morphology within and between populations.

results.title: Telomere elongation during early development is independent of environmental temperatures in Atlantic salmon
results.author: Darryl McLennan
results.takeaway: Atlantic salmon can restore telomere length during early development, potentially buffering harmful environmental effects experienced in early life.

results.title: Trade-Offs in Relative Limb Length among Peruvian Children: Extending the Thrifty Phenotype Hypothesis to Limb Proportions
results.author: Emma Pomeroy
results.takeaway: Peruvian children exposed to higher stress have shorter limbs and body segments, suggesting that functional traits (hand, foot, and head) may be partially protected at the expense of tibia and ulna under tough conditions.

results.title: Temperature, Growth Rate, and Body Size in Ectotherms: Fitting Pieces of a Life-History Puzzle1
results.author: Michael J. Angilletta
results.takeaway: Ectotherms grow slower but mature at larger body size in colder environments, highlighting the need for a multivariate theory incorporating thermal reaction norms and environmental variables.

results.title: Linking life-history theory and metabolic theory explains the offspring size-temperature relationship.
results.author: A. Pettersen
results.takeaway: Colder temperatures require mothers to produce larger, better provisioned offspring, as both metabolic and developmental rates increase with temperature, but developmental rate is more temperature sensitive.

results.title: Unilateral Heat Accelerates Bone Elongation and Lengthens Extremities of Growing Mice
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.journal: Journal of orthopaedic research : official publication of the Orthopaedic Research Society
results.takeaway: Unilateral heat exposure for 40 minutes per day for 14 days in growing mice significantly increases femoral and tibial lengths, potentially benefiting children with growth impediments.

results.title: Thermal tolerance patterns across latitude and elevation
results.author: J. Sunday
results.journal: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B
results.takeaway: Different species exhibit different thermal tolerance patterns across latitude and elevation, with differences in episodic thermal extremes largely explaining these patterns.

results.title: Shared extremes by ectotherms and endotherms: Body elongation in mustelids is associated with small size and reduced limbs
results.author: Chris J. Law
results.takeaway: Elongation of the body in mustelids is associated with reduced body size and forelimb length, suggesting a common pattern of trait covariance in body shape evolution across all vertebrates.

results.title: The genetic architecture underlying body-size traits plasticity over different temperatures and developmental stages in Caenorhabditis elegans
results.author: Muhammad I. Maulana
results.takeaway: Multiple loci affect body-size plasticity in Caenorhabditis elegans, with the majority clustering on Chromosome X, suggesting potential co-regulation of traits and trait plasticity under different environments.

results.title: Temperature Enhanced Extremity Lengthening is Growth Rate Dependent
results.author: J. Kerby
results.takeaway: Heat-induced limb lengthening is possible during both early and late postnatal growth phases, offering potential noninvasive treatments for limb length inequality in children.

77.state: Developmental temperature setting extremity proportions — Allen's rule — is actively studied: plasticity drives Allen's and Bergmann's rules in endotherms, temperature affects bone elongation in a critical early window, and rodent tails specifically lengthen with warmth. Przibram quantified the very coefficient now being mechanistically explained.

facets.q: Can a developmentally induced body proportion be inherited across generations in mammals?
Is there transgenerational inheritance of temperature-induced morphological changes?

results.title: Maternal high-fat diet effects on third-generation female body size via the paternal lineage.
results.author: G. A. Dunn
results.takeaway: Maternal high-fat diet exposure increases body size in third-generation female offspring, with this effect transmitted via the paternal lineage, potentially affecting disease predisposition.

results.title: Transgenerational developmental programming.
results.author: C. Aiken
results.takeaway: Developmental programming effects can persist in further generations, with suboptimal intrauterine development and maternal age increasing the likelihood of transmission.

results.title: Maternal high-fat diet promotes body length increases and insulin insensitivity in second-generation mice.
results.author: G. A. Dunn
results.takeaway: Maternal high-fat diet exposure promotes body length increases and reduced insulin sensitivity in offspring across multiple generations, with epigenetic inheritance supporting this effect.

results.title: INHERITANCE OF ENVIRONMENTAL VARIATION IN BODY SIZE: SUPERPARASITISM OF SEEDS AFFECTS PROGENY AND GRANDPROGENY BODY SIZE VIA A NONGENETIC MATERNAL EFFECT
results.author: C. Fox
results.takeaway: Environmental variation in body size is inherited by progeny in the seed beetle, Callosobruchus maculatus, through a nongenetic maternal effect.

results.title: Lamarck rises from his grave: parental environment‐induced epigenetic inheritance in model organisms and humans
results.author: Yan Wang
results.takeaway: Parental environment-induced epigenetic alterations can transmit across generations in various organisms, with sex-specific effects, and require further investigation for better understanding of gene-environment interactions.

results.title: Evolution of adult size depends on genetic variance in growth trajectories: a comment on analyses of evolutionary dynamics using integral projection models
results.author: Luis‐Miguel Chevin
results.takeaway: Integral projection models may underestimate evolutionary responses to environmental changes, as they do not fully capture the accumulation of phenotypic differences and their transmission across generations.

results.title: Non-genetic inheritance of induced defense morphologies across multiple unexposed generations of Daphnia lumholtzi
results.author: Shannon N Snyder
results.takeaway: Predator-induced changes in body shape in Daphnia lumholtzi can persist across several unexposed generations, highlighting the importance of non-genetic inheritance in shaping organismal form and function.

results.title: Epigenetic inheritance in the mouse.
results.author: I. Roemer
results.takeaway: Acquired epigenetic modifications in early embryos can be transmitted through the germline, affecting human health and raising questions about the evolutionary importance of epigenetic inheritance.

results.title: Analysis of response to 20 generations of selection for body composition in mice: fit to infinitesimal model assumptions
results.author: V. Martínez
results.takeaway: 20 generations of selective breeding in mice resulted in increased fatness and leanness, with genetic variance and heritability remaining similar across generations.

results.title: Age-dependent genetic architecture across ontogeny of body size in sticklebacks
results.author: Antoine Fraimout
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Different chromosomes contribute to heritability of body size in sticklebacks, suggesting that it responds to selection at different stages of development, potentially causing non-parallel evolution.

results.title: The heritability of body composition
results.author: A. Brener
results.takeaway: Heritable body composition traits are demonstrated in childhood, but environmental factors have a more significant impact during teenage years.

results.title: Ancestral dietary change alters the development of Drosophila larvae through MAPK signalling
results.author: Samuel G. Towarnicki
results.takeaway: Ancestral diet influences Drosophila larval development through changes in the MAPK signaling pathway, affecting growth rate between embryonic and pupal stages.

results.title: Size Inheritance
results.author: W. Castle
results.takeaway: The multiple factor hypothesis is inadequate for explaining body size inheritance in mammals, as many independent genes influence it, with some increasing it, others decreasing it, and some having no observable influence.

results.title: Universal DNA methylation age across mammalian tissues
results.author: A. T. Lu
results.takeaway: Aging can be accurately estimated using DNA methylation profiles across 185 mammalian species, suggesting it is evolutionary conserved and linked to developmental processes.

results.title: Molecular mechanisms of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance
results.author: Maximilian H. Fitz-James
results.takeaway: Epigenetic information, such as DNA methylation, histone modifications, and non-coding RNAs, can be inherited across generations in organisms, contributing to phenotypic variation and evolution.

results.title: Transgenerational Inheritance of Environmentally Induced Epigenetic Alterations during Mammalian Development
results.author: L. Legoff
results.takeaway: Environmental factors can induce parental alterations and affect offspring phenotypes through gametic epigenetic inheritance, influencing disease risk assessment.

results.title: Universal DNA Methylation Age Across Mammalian Tissues
results.author: A. Lu
results.takeaway: Aging is evolutionarily conserved and linked to developmental processes across all mammalian species, with universal pan-mammalian clocks accurately estimating age in any tissue.

results.title: Heritable changes in chromatin contacts associated with transgenerational susceptibility to diet-induced insulin dysregulation and obesity
results.author: Richard C Chang
results.takeaway: Prenatal exposure to tributyltin in mice leads to heritable changes in chromatin interactions, resulting in transgenerational susceptibility to diet-induced insulin dysregulation and obesity.

results.title: Endochondral ossification and the evolution of limb proportions
results.author: C. Rolian
results.takeaway: Mammals' diverse limb proportions are driven by differential limb bone growth, with chondrocyte population size evolving more readily and extreme hypertrophy being rarer.

results.title: Size-Factors and Size-Inheritance.
results.author: F. B. Sumner
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Castle's conclusion that genetic factors affecting size in rabbits are general and exclusive may not hold true for humans and other mammals, as size variations in different parts of the body may be independently inherited.

results.title: Review: Phenotypic and molecular evidence of inter- and trans-generational effects of heat stress in livestock mammals and humans.
results.author: J. Laporta
results.journal: Animal : an international journal of animal bioscience
results.takeaway: Maternal exposure to extreme heat can negatively impact future generations in various species, including humans, swine, sheep, goats, and cattle, through epigenetic modifications.

results.title: Transgenerational effects of temperature fluctuations in Arabidopsis thaliana
results.author: Ying Deng
results.takeaway: Heat stress timing significantly affects plant phenotypes in offspring, with genetic variation and correlations to climatic variability suggesting that transgenerational effects can evolve.

results.title: Natural genetic variation in a multigenerational phenotype in C. elegans
results.author: Lise Frézal
results.takeaway: C. elegans wild populations may harbor natural genetic variation in epigenetic inheritance phenomena, such as heat-sensitive mortal germline phenotypes, which can be suppressed by temperature alternations at each generation.

results.title: Thermal legacies: transgenerational effects of temperature on growth in a vertebrate.
results.author: S. Salinas
results.takeaway: Sheepshead minnows can adaptively program their offspring for maximal growth at present temperature, potentially altering extinction risk in the face of climate change.

results.title: Thermal conditioning of quail embryos has transgenerational and reversible long-term effects
results.author: A. Vitorino Carvalho
results.journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
results.takeaway: Thermal conditioning of quail embryos has beneficial effects on post-hatch heat tolerance, but may cause transgenerational but reversible growth defects.

results.title: Thermal transgenerational plasticity in natural populations of Daphnia.
results.author: M. Walsh
results.takeaway: Temperatures in parents and grandparents significantly influence the maturation age of grand-offspring in Daphnia zooplankton, potentially allowing for rapid responses to global climatic change.

results.title: Intergenerational effects of ocean temperature variation: Early life benefits are short-lived in threespine stickleback
results.author: H. C. Spence-Jones
results.takeaway: Parental exposure to natural temperature variation in threespine sticklebacks may offset early-life growth effects, but these effects are short-lived and may not persist under more challenging environmental conditions.

results.title: Transgenerational plasticity in marine sticklebacks: maternal effects mediate impacts of a warming ocean
results.author: L. Shama
results.takeaway: Maternal effects on offspring body size in marine sticklebacks can buffer short-term negative effects of ocean warming, contributing to their evolutionary potential and persistence under climate change.

results.title: Transgenerational plasticity research: Challenges and opportunities
results.author: C. McAndry
results.takeaway: Transgenerational plasticity research can advance our understanding of organismal evolution and climate change responses by clarifying terminology and integrating parallel processes.

results.title: No evidence for thermal transgenerational plasticity in metabolism when minimizing the potential for confounding effects
results.author: Ø. N. Kielland
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: The current evidence for thermal transgenerational plasticity in metabolism in Daphnia pulex is weak, with no evidence of adaptive changes in offspring fitness when temperatures match parent conditions.

results.title: Transgenerational effects on body size and survival in Brook charr (Salvelinus fontinalis)
results.author: C. Houle
results.takeaway: Offspring from "warm" breeders have higher survival rates than those from "cold" breeders, with a selection treatment linked to lower Fulton's condition index, which positively correlates with survival in lakes.

results.title: Temperature-size responses during ontogeny are independent of progenitors’ thermal environments
results.author: Gerard Martínez‐De León
results.takeaway: Temperature-size responses in Collembola species are generally weak, with egg and juvenile development being especially responsive at higher temperatures and slightly affected by transgenerational plasticity.

results.title: Epigenetic responses to temperature and climate.
results.author: B. McCaw
results.takeaway: Epigenetic modifications, such as DNA methylation and histone/chromatin modification, play a crucial role in regulating phenotypic variation and adaptation to environmental temperature changes, with temperature- and species-specific effects.

results.title: Epigenetic stress memory: A new approach to study cold and heat stress responses in plants
results.author: M. Ramakrishnan
results.takeaway: Epigenetic changes in plants play a key role in cold and heat stress responses, with potential for inherited stress memories and improved plant development.

results.title: Grandparental effects in marine sticklebacks: transgenerational plasticity across multiple generations
results.author: L. Shama
results.takeaway: Grandparental exposure to elevated temperatures negatively impacts hatching success in marine sticklebacks, with effects persisting across multiple generations and playing a role in mediating climate change impacts.

results.title: Transgenerational transmission of environmental effects in livestock in the age of global warming
results.author: Moran Gershoni
results.takeaway: Global warming and climate change can cause long-term epigenetic changes in livestock, potentially affecting their performance and welfare regardless of their immediate environment.

results.title: Separating Paternal and Maternal Contributions to Thermal Transgenerational Plasticity
results.author: Sarah L. Chang
results.takeaway: Thermal transgenerational plasticity (TGP) in sheepshead minnows is contributed by both parents, with offspring growth rates being highest when all three temperatures match, suggesting an epigenetic basis.

results.title: Transgenerational effects of heat shock on gene regulation and fitness-related traits in natural Drosophila populations
results.author: Ewan Harney
results.takeaway: Heat shock in Drosophila populations can lead to transgenerational inheritance of potentially beneficial phenotypes and gene expression, with effects persisting up to four generations in great-great-grandoffspring.

results.title: Multigenerational temperature effects on paternal epigenetic inheritance in European sea bass
results.author: N. Sánchez-Baizán
results.takeaway: Temperature affects epigenetic modifications in European sea bass, potentially influencing species adaptation and conservation in the face of global warming.

results.title: Perception of Temperature Even in the Absence of Actual Change is Sufficient to Drive Transgenerational Epigenetic Inheritance
results.author: Guy Teichman
results.takeaway: Temperature perception alone in Caenorhabditis elegans can drive transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, influencing gene expression across generations.

76.state: Whether a developmentally induced proportion is inherited is the transgenerational-plasticity question: maternal high-fat-diet effects on body size persist for three generations and induced body-size changes are inherited in seed beetles, but mammalian mechanisms remain debated. This direct Lamarckian test prefigures it.

facets.q: Does rearing temperature alter tail and extremity length in rodents during development?
How does ambient temperature during growth affect body proportions in mammals?

results.title: Allen's Rule Revisited: Temperature Influences Bone Elongation During a Critical Period of Postnatal Development
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.takeaway: Temperature significantly influences bone elongation during a critical period of early postnatal development, with the skeleton being most responsive to external stimuli during this period.

results.title: Effect of warm rearing on the development of thermolytic effectors in rats
results.author: A. Demicka
results.takeaway: Warm rearing stimulates hypertrophy of thermolytic organs in rats, particularly in females, which is crucial for pregnancy and caring for young.

results.title: An experimental study of somatic modifications and their reappearance in the offspring
results.author: F. B. Sumner
results.takeaway: Warm-room-raised mice have longer tails, feet, and ears compared to cold-room-raised mice, with the ear showing less pronounced temperature response.

results.title: The Reappearance in the Offspring of Artificially Produced Parental Modifications
results.author: F. B. Sumner
results.takeaway: Temperature differences during growth can cause permanent changes in offspring's body parts, such as tail length and hair quantity, or are they merely transitory?

results.title: Lability of tail length of the white-footed mouse, Peromyscus leucopus noveboracensis.
results.author: R. Thorington
results.takeaway: White-footed mice raised at 25° C have significantly longer tails at maturity than those raised at 15° C, suggesting a direct effect of temperature on tail growth.

results.title: Behaviorally mediated, warm adaptation: a physiological strategy when mice behaviorally thermoregulate.
results.author: C. Gordon
results.takeaway: Mice housed in a thermocline for long periods experience behaviorally mediated warm adaptation, leading to smaller internal organs and longer tails compared to mice housed in isothermal conditions.

results.title: Effect of Two Extreme Temperatures on Growth and Tail-length of Mice
results.author: M. A. E. Ashoub
results.takeaway: Mice exposed to extreme temperatures show differences in growth and tail-length, with cold environments affecting their development.

results.title: Exercise mitigates the stunting effect of cold temperature on limb elongation in mice by increasing solute delivery to the growth plate
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.takeaway: Chronic exercise and warm temperatures promote bone lengthening by increasing solute delivery to the growth plate in mice, regardless of temperature.

results.title: The effect of rearing infant rats at three environmental temperatures on the structure of some of their organs.
results.author: Z. Vacek
results.takeaway: Prolonged exposure to high and low environmental temperatures in infant rats leads to various biochemical, morphological, and endocrine changes, with some changes influenced by the animal's age.

results.title: The effects of changes in the environmental temperature on the growth of tail bones in the mouse.
results.author: F. Al-Hilli
results.takeaway: Mouse tail growth after weaning is influenced by environmental temperatures, with hotter temperatures promoting faster growth rates and longer tails.

results.title: Genotype and environment in tail length in mice.
results.author: S. A. Barnett
results.journal: Quarterly journal of experimental physiology and cognate medical sciences
results.takeaway: Mice tail length is not influenced by cold, and its variation is higher in the reducedtail strain A/Tb than in other inbred strains.

results.title: Body weights, tail lengths, body temperatures, food intakes, & some slaughter data for four strains of mice reared at three different environmental temperatures
results.author: M. L. Bigham
results.journal: New Zealand Journal of Agricultural Research
results.takeaway: Body weight is more important for heat regulation in cold environments (7°C) and tail length is important for heat regulation in hot environments (30°C), with long-tail strains showing a smaller rise in body temperature.

results.title: Thermal environment affects morphological and behavioral development of Rattus norvegicus.
results.author: Jill A. Villarreal
results.takeaway: Norway rats develop body morphology and thermal preferences based on their thermal environment, with cool-housed rats preferring warmer temperatures and warm-housed rats preferring cooler temperatures.

results.title: Heterosis and adaptability in the heat tolerance of mice.
results.author: G. Harrison
results.takeaway: Mice with longer tails and less fur show adaptive responses to high temperatures, suggesting heterosis in their overall heat tolerance.

results.title: A global test of Allen’s rule in rodents
results.author: B. H. Alhajeri
results.takeaway: Allen's rule is confirmed in rodents only for tails, with a positive relationship between tail length and cold temperatures, driven by adaptation to cold temperatures and habitat type.

results.title: Temperature regulates limb length in homeotherms by directly modulating cartilage growth
results.author: Maria A Serrat
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Temperature indirectly influences limb length in mammals by affecting appendage temperature, rather than vascular nutrient delivery.

results.title: Teratogenic Effects of Mild Heat Stress during Mouse Embryogenesis: Effect of Trp53
results.author: D. Boreham
results.takeaway: Mild heat stress during mouse embryogenesis disrupts a tail length-regulating process, with the teratogenic outcome depending on gestation time and the ability of Trp53 to regulate tail length.

results.title: Appendage Sizes in Three Rodent Species are Affected Indirectly by Climate Factors via Their Effects on Body Size.
results.author: Ke Li
results.takeaway: Climate factors indirectly affect appendage sizes in three rodent species through their effects on body size, with temperature-appendage-length patterns driven by species-specific temperature sensitivities and appendage-specific ontogenetic rates.

results.title: Selection for body weight in the mouse at three temperatures and the correlated response in tail length.
results.author: R. L. Baker
results.takeaway: Mice selected for increased body weight under low nutrition planes are more adaptable to both high and low temperatures, while those selected for decreased body weight under high nutrition planes show less fatty tissue.

results.title: Effects of cold on the skeletal growth of albino rats.
results.author: Marjorie M. C. Lee
results.takeaway: Cold exposure in albino rats leads to smaller body measurements, slower growth rates, shorter tail and feet, and shorter but thicker bones, possibly due to reduced blood flow to the skeletal tissue.

results.title: Small increases in ambient temperature reduce offspring body mass in an equatorial mammal
results.author: M. Khera
results.takeaway: Higher ambient temperatures lead to lighter pups in equatorial banded mongooses, with direct physiological constraints rather than opportunity costs of behavioral thermoregulation explaining the negative impact of high temperatures on pup growth.

results.title: Effects of ambient temperatures between 5 and 35 °C on energy balance, body mass and body composition in mice
results.author: Zhijun Zhao
results.takeaway: Colder temperatures increase energy expenditure in mice, but these increases are offset by increased food intake, so the impact on obesity levels may be small.

results.title: Effects of ambient temperature on the growth performance, fat deposition, and intestinal morphology of geese from 28 to 49 days of age
results.author: Z.L. Liu
results.takeaway: High ambient temperature decreases growth performance and fat deposition in geese from 28 to 49 days of age, with the upper critical temperature being 25.19°C.

results.title: Geographic variation in body size: the effects of ambient temperature and precipitation
results.author: Y. Yom-Tov
results.takeaway: Rainfall positively influences body size in several species of mammals, while low temperatures may inhibit photosynthesis, with a significant proportion of variability in body size explained by rain.

results.title: Growing Up in a Changing Climate: How Temperature Affects the Development of Morphological, Behavioral and Physiological Traits of a Marsupial Mammal
results.author: Clare Stawski
results.takeaway: Temperature during development influences behavioral and physiological traits in yellow-footed antechinus, a marsupial mammal with a limited lifespan and a single breeding event per year.

results.title: Effects of ambient temperature on growth performance, blood parameter, and fat deposition of geese from 14 to 28 days of age
results.author: Z.L. Liu
results.takeaway: The upper critical temperature for geese from 14 to 28 days of age is 26.17°C, and high ambient temperature can lead to growth depression.

results.title: Why are organisms usually bigger in colder environments? Making sense of a life history puzzle.
results.author: David Atkinson
results.takeaway: Adult organisms tend to be larger in colder environments, possibly due to adaptive responses to temperature limitations or unavoidable consequences of cell size and temperature relationships.

results.title: Ambient air temperature exposure and foetal size and growth in three European birth cohorts.
results.author: E. Essers
results.takeaway: Ambient air temperature exposure during pregnancy is associated with changes in foetal head circumference throughout gestation, with susceptibility periods for cold exposure in the first trimester.

results.title: Mammalian body size is determined by interactions between climate, urbanization, and ecological traits
results.author: M. Hantak
results.takeaway: Urbanization and temperature influence mammalian body size, often leading to larger individuals, with life history and ecological factors playing a critical role in mediating these effects.

results.title: Evaluation of the effects of environmental temperature and nutrition on growth and development
results.author: M. Dauncey
results.takeaway: Environmental temperature and energy intake independently influence growth and development in young mammals, with skeletal growth being faster in warm environments and higher energy intake.

results.title: On the Interplay among Ambient Temperature, Basal Metabolic Rate, and Body Mass
results.author: D. Naya
results.takeaway: Ambient temperature's impact on basal metabolic rate decreases with body mass, suggesting its importance in metabolic evolution in endotherms is size-dependent.

results.title: Symposium on Growth: Environment and Growth
results.author: C. Winchester
results.takeaway: Ambient temperature influences food intake, water consumption, and energy conversion, with stress factors such as high humidity, wind, and direct sunlight affecting growth.

results.title: Climate‐Driven Body Size Changes in Birds and Mammals Reveal Environmental Tolerance Limits
results.author: Matthew J Watson
results.takeaway: Climate change leads to smaller body size changes in birds and mammals, balancing thermoregulation and osmotic balance, with agricultural land use increasing these changes.

results.title: Artificially raised roost temperatures lead to larger body sizes in wild bats.
results.author: Carolin Mundinger
results.takeaway: Artificially raising roost temperatures in wild bats leads to larger body sizes due to metabolic savings in warmer conditions, potentially impacting population persistence.

results.title: Bergmann’s Rule and Body Size in Mammals
results.author: R. Freckleton
results.takeaway: Bergmann's rule reveals that body size increases in cold areas, while smaller mammals are found in warmer areas, with mountainous areas influencing local and broad-scale patterns.

results.title: Limb proportions show developmental plasticity in response to embryo movement
results.author: A. Pollard
results.takeaway: Embryonic movement during prenatal development can alter limb proportions and regulate bone growth, potentially affecting post-hatching performance in animals.

results.title: Optimal energy allocation trade-off driven by size-dependent physiological and demographic responses to warming.
results.author: Viktor Thunell
results.takeaway: Warming reduces optimal energy allocation to growth in size-structured organisms like pike, leading to reduced body size and a truncated population size structure.

results.title: Bergmann's rule and the geography of mammal body size in the Western Hemisphere
results.author: M. Rodríguez
results.takeaway: Mammal body size varies with temperature in cold areas, but not in warmer areas, with mountainous areas influencing local and broad-scale patterns.

results.title: Developmental variation in ecogeographic body proportions.
results.author: Libby W Cowgill
results.takeaway: Immature body proportions, such as bi-iliac breadth and intralimb indices, are strongly influenced by ecogeographic principles, while other factors like nutrition and growth constraints also play a role.

results.title: Prolonged growth during the food-restricted dry season in a small African mammal
results.author: R. Rimbach
results.takeaway: African striped mice can maintain a prolonged growth period during the long dry season, with increased food availability and lower ambient temperature promoting growth.

152.state: Rearing temperature altering tail and extremity length is textbook developmental plasticity and still studied: warm-reared rodents grow longer tails, feet and ears, with permanent effects traced to temperature-sensitive bone elongation. This heat experiment is an early demonstration.

facets.q: Do environment and sex jointly determine continuous morphological traits such as tail length?
How do sex and developmental temperature interact to shape body proportions?

results.title: Morphological Variation in the Striped Field Mouse Across Three Countries
results.author: L. Balčiauskas
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: The striped field mouse exhibits ecogeographic rules, with larger body size in colder climates and smaller appendage sizes in warmer climates, highlighting its adaptability to environmental changes.

results.title: SEXUAL SELECTION IN THE MONOGAMOUS BARN SWALLOW (HIRUNDO RUSTICA). I. DETERMINANTS OF TAIL ORNAMENT SIZE
results.author: A. Møller
results.takeaway: Tail length in the monogamous barn swallow Hirundo rustica is 20% greater in males than in females, correlates with wing length, and has a 0.59 heritability, suggesting secondary sexual traits may show significant heritability.

results.title: Phenotypic Analysis of Growth and Morphological Traits in Miniature Breeds of Japanese Indigenous Chickens
results.author: Ryoko Ono
results.takeaway: Age-dependent changes in growth and morphological traits in Japanese indigenous chicken breeds reveal the influence of sex and environment on their characteristics.

results.title: Scaling of Morphological Characters across Trait Type, Sex, and Environment
results.author: K. Voje
results.takeaway: Negative allometry is the expected scaling relationship of morphological traits within species, with genitals showing negative allometry in insects and spiders, and terrestrial organisms may have different scaling patterns than aquatic species.

results.title: Morphological Correlates of Locomotion in the Aquatic and the Terrestrial Phases of Pleurodeles waltl Newts from Southwestern Iberia
results.author: F. J. Zamora‐Camacho
results.takeaway: Morphological differences in Pleurodeles waltl newts do not impact locomotor performance in either aquatic or terrestrial stages, suggesting no trade-off between the two modes in these animals.

results.title: Differences in growth, morphometric traits, and male sexual maturity among Pacific white shrimp, Penaeus vannamei, from different commercial hatcheries
results.author: S. Chow
results.takeaway: Genetic differences exist in morphometric traits and male sexual maturity among Pacific white shrimp hatchery lots, but environmental factors largely influence these traits.

results.title: Allometry, sexual dimorphism, and sexual trait elaboration in the birds of paradise.
results.author: Thomas MacGillavry
results.journal: Evolution; international journal of organic evolution
results.takeaway: Birds-of-paradise exhibit extreme diversity in tail lengths, with body size following Rensch's rule in polygynous species, but no evidence of RR in tail length, suggesting similar evolutionary allometries between males and females.

results.title: MORPHOLOGICAL DIMORPHISM VARIES ACROSS THE ENDEMIC XANTUS’ HUMMINGBIRD (HYLOCHARIS XANTUSII) GENETIC POPULATIONS IN THE BAJA CALIFORNIA PENINSULA
results.author: Cristina González-Rubio Sanvicente
results.takeaway: Morphological differences in Xantus' hummingbirds are influenced by environmental factors and oases distribution, with altitude and oasis location playing a key role in determining wing, wing chord, and bill width traits.

results.title: No evidence of sexual dimorphism in the tails of the swallowtail butterflies Papilio machaon gorganus and P. m. britannicus
results.author: Lydia K. Koutrouditsou
results.takeaway: No evidence of sexual dimorphism in swallowtail butterfly tails was found, suggesting that tail morphology is primarily driven by predator avoidance factors rather than aerodynamics.

results.title: AN ANALYSIS OF CONTINENT‐WIDE PATTERNS OF SEXUAL SELECTION IN A PASSERINE BIRD
results.author: A. P. Møller
results.takeaway: Sexual selection patterns in barn swallows are consistent across large geographical scales but vary among populations, with geographical patterns predicting current phenotypic variation.

results.title: The influence of natural selection and sexual selection on the tails of sea-snakes (Laticauda colubrina)
results.author: R. Shine
results.journal: Biological Journal of The Linnean Society
results.takeaway: Relative tail length in male sea kraits influences their fitness through natural and sexual selection, with longer tails enhancing survival, growth, and locomotor ability, while shorter tails improve swimming speed and mating success.

results.title: Sexual Dimorphism in the Chinese Endemic Species Hynobius maoershanensis (Urodela: Hynobiidae)
results.author: Huiqun Chen
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: Sexual dimorphism in Hynobius maoershanensis is present in shape, but not size, with males having wider heads and females having wider axilla-groin spaces, potentially improving mating success and reproductive output.

results.title: The Genetic Basis of Elongated Tails in Birds: Evidence for Sexual Selection as a Driver of Convergent Evolution.
results.author: Hui Wang
results.takeaway: Sexual selection plays a major role in the evolution of elongated tails in birds, with genes related to feather development, reproductive functions, and immunity showing convergence replacements and rapid evolution.

results.title: HIR UNDO RUSTICA). I. DETERMINANTS OF TAIL ORNAMENT SIZE
results.author: A. Møller
results.takeaway: Tail length in barn swallows is 20% greater in males than in females, correlated with wing length, and is highly repeatable, with heritability of 0.59.

results.title: HOW TO COMPENSATE FOR COSTLY SEXUALLY SELECTED TAILS: THE ORIGIN OF SEXUALLY DIMORPHIC WINGS IN LONG‐TAILED BIRDS
results.author: A. Balmford
results.takeaway: Long-tailed birds with expensive tails tend to have longer wings, suggesting that sexual dimorphism may have evolved to reduce the costs of elaborate ornaments.

results.title: The role of ecological variation in driving divergence of sexual and non-sexual traits in the red-backed fairy-wren (Malurus melanocephalus)
results.author: Daniel T. Baldassarre
results.takeaway: Ecological variation plays a significant role in driving divergence of sexual and non-sexual traits in the red-backed fairy-wren, with geographic distance affecting plumage color and environmental variation affecting tail length.

results.title: SEXUAL SELECTION IN THE BARN SWALLOW HIRUNDO RUSTICA. III. FEMALE TAIL ORNAMENTS.
results.author: A. M. Ller
results.takeaway: Female barn swallow tail length accurately reflects reproductive potential and is under directional selection, potentially leading to an evolutionary response due to heritability of the trait.

results.title: The Evolution of Rodent Tail Morphology
results.author: C. Sheard
results.takeaway: Rodent tail morphology is influenced by temperature, locomotory mode, and environmental factors, with local ecological context playing a significant role in shaping its shape.

results.title: Testosterone production, sexually dimorphic morphology, and digit ratio in the dark-eyed junco
results.author: K. Cain
results.takeaway: Individual variation in developmental hormone exposure, as reflected by digit 2 to digit 4, is correlated with adult hormone production ability and sexually dimorphic morphology in the dark-eyed junco.

results.title: Sexual dimorphism of tail length in lacertid lizards: test of a morphological constraint hypothesis
results.author: L. J. Barbadillo
results.takeaway: Male copulatory organs do not significantly impact the evolution of dimorphism in tail length in lacertid lizards.

results.title: Effect of temperature on sexual size dimorphism during the developmental period in the broad-horned flour beetle.
results.author: Kentarou Matsumura
results.takeaway: The temperature-size rule also influences the size of sexual traits in the broad-horned flour beetle, with male weapon size and body size trading off at lower temperatures.

results.title: Annual temperature, body size, and sexual size dimorphism in the evolution of Pyrgomorphidae
results.author: Raúl Cueva del Castillo
results.takeaway: Higher temperatures positively correlate with sexual size dimorphism in Pyrgomorphidae grasshoppers, with species adapted to higher temperatures favoring larger females.

results.title: Environmental effects on sexual size dimorphism of a seed-feeding beetle
results.author: R. Stillwell
results.takeaway: Rearing temperature significantly influences sexual size dimorphism in seed beetles, with females being larger than males at all temperatures and the dimorphism being smallest at the lowest temperature.

results.title: Oxygen and temperature affect cell sizes differently among tissues and between sexes of Drosophila melanogaster.
results.author: M. Czarnoleski
results.takeaway: Temperature and oxygen levels affect Drosophila melanogaster's body size and cell sizes differently, suggesting that ectotherms adjust their life-history traits and cellular composition to prevent severe hypoxia.

results.title: Temperature‐dependent scaling of fitness traits with body size in hydra
results.author: J. Tökölyi
results.takeaway: Larger body sizes in hydra are more beneficial for reproduction in cold environments, explaining the temperature-size rule in ectotherm species.

results.title: Equal temperature–size responses of the sexes are widespread within arthropod species
results.author: A. Hirst
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: Equal temperature-body size responses are found in both sexes across 85 diverse arthropod species, suggesting equal fitness costs and benefits for both sexes.

results.title: Thermal variability induces sex-specific morphometric changes in zebrafish (Danio rerio).
results.author: A. Grigg
results.takeaway: Thermal variability during different life stages in zebrafish leads to sex-specific morphometric changes, with males benefiting from lifelong fluctuating temperature exposure.

results.title: Sex-specific transgenerational plasticity: Developmental temperatures of mothers and fathers have different effects on sons and daughters.
results.author: F. Seebacher
results.takeaway: Warmer mothers and fathers raise their guppies at 28°C, leading to reduced mass and length in their offspring, while warmer fathers produce shorter sons and greater sustained swimming speed in their daughters.

results.title: WITHIN‐ AND BETWEEN‐GENERATION EFFECTS OF TEMPERATURE ON THE MORPHOLOGY AND PHYSIOLOGY OF DROSOPHILA MELANOGASTER
results.author: W. Crill
results.takeaway: Sex and developmental temperature significantly influence Drosophila melanogaster traits, with parental temperatures having small effects on a few traits.

results.title: Effects of global warming on sex ratios in fishes.
results.author: B. Geffroy
results.takeaway: Global warming may lead to male bias in fish sex ratios, with transgenerational plasticity potentially mitigating these effects in some populations.

results.title: Experimental evolution reveals trade-offs between sexual selection and heat tolerance in Drosophila prolongata.
results.author: A. D. De Nardo
results.journal: Evolution; international journal of organic evolution
results.takeaway: Male Drosophila prolongata with even or slightly male-biased sex ratios show larger body size and greater thermal sensitivity, but experience more decline in sperm competitiveness under developmental or adult heat stress.

results.title: No degradation of temperature-mediated phenotypic plasticity in Drosophila melanogaster after more than 275 generations of artificial selection on body-size.
results.author: Yvonne L. Ofodile
results.takeaway: Artificial selection on body size in Drosophila melanogaster for over 275 generations had minimal effects on developmental plasticity, but did affect sex-specific slopes of the relationship between size and rearing temperature.

results.title: The influence of climate and population structure on East Asian skeletal morphology.
results.author: Elizabeth O. Cho
results.takeaway: Climate influences East Asian skeletal morphology, with larger body size associated with colder climates, but the same traits may not be consistently associated with climate for both sexes.

results.title: Gender and Timing during Ontogeny Matter: Effects of a Temporary High Temperature on Survival, Body Size and Colouration in Harmonia axyridis
results.author: M. Knapp
results.takeaway: Elevated temperature during early developmental stages reduces survival but does not affect adult phenotype, while exposure during later stages affects body size, coloration, and sexual dimorphism in harlequin ladybirds.

results.title: Effects of temperature on the ontogeny of sexual size dimorphism in the fall armyworm Spodoptera frugiperda
results.author: Wei Liu
results.takeaway: Temperature significantly influences the ontogeny of sexual size dimorphism in the fall armyworm Spodoptera frugiperda, with males taking longer to develop and females growing faster at 28°C.

results.title: Ecophysiological determinants of sexual size dimorphism: integrating growth trajectories, environmental conditions, and metabolic rates
results.author: Marie-Claire Chelini
results.takeaway: Female crab spider Mecaphesa celer exhibits extreme sexual size dimorphism due to differences in growth trajectories and environmental factors, with females being more sensitive to environmental factors than males.

results.title: Why get big in the cold? Towards a solution to a life-history puzzle
results.author: Isabell Karl
results.takeaway: Copper butterflies Lycaena tityrus grow larger at lower temperatures due to increased food intake and better efficiency in converting food into body matter, potentially explaining the temperature-size rule.

results.title: Developmental temperature, body size and male mating success in fruit flies, Drosophila melanogaster (Diptera: Drosophilidae)
results.author: S. Pavković‐Lučić
results.takeaway: Male body size in fruit flies is not correlated with mating success when influenced by temperature variability, suggesting that temperature-induced body size is not a sexually selected trait.

results.title: Body growth and shape regulation by neuropeptides and other neuroactive peptides in insects.
results.author: Andrés Garelli

results.title: Sexual dimorphism in body composition across human populations: Associations with climate and proxies for short‐ and long‐term energy supply
results.author: J. Wells
results.takeaway: Sexual dimorphism in body composition increases with decreasing temperature, with females investing more in adiposity and males more in lean mass, and is influenced by short-term energy availability.

75.state: How environment and sex jointly tune continuous traits is active in eco-evo-devo: ecogeographic rules, temperature effects on sexual size dimorphism, and allometric scaling of appendages. Reading tail length as a conditional sex character fits this framing.

facets.q: Does autologous ovarian tissue transplantation restore endocrine function and fertility?
What is the clinical success of ovarian tissue autotransplantation for fertility preservation?

results.title: Current Success and Efficiency of Autologous Ovarian Transplantation: A Meta-Analysis
results.author: F. Pacheco
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation of ovarian tissue before autotransplantation shows promising success rates, making it a viable option for fertility preservation.

results.title: Evaluation of ovarian tissue transplantation: results from three clinical centers.
results.author: M. Shapira
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue autotransplantation (AT) is highly effective in restoring fertility in sterilized patients, with prior chemotherapy exposure not being a contraindication, and repeated transplantation may be feasible in selected cases.

results.title: Comparison of Orthotopic and Heterotopic Autologous Ovarian Tissue Transplantation (OTT) Outcomes.
results.author: K. Oktay
results.takeaway: Orthotopic ovarian tissue transplantation is preferred for those intending to conceive, while heterotopic transplantation is less invasive for those primarily seeking endocrine function restoration.

results.title: Fresh and cryopreserved ovarian tissue transplantation for preserving reproductive and endocrine function: a systematic review and individual patient data meta-analysis
results.author: H. Khattak
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation and transplantation can effectively restore reproductive and hormonal functions in women, potentially preserving endocrine function as well.

results.title: Ovarian function and reproductive outcome after ovarian tissue transplantation: a systematic review
results.author: Sepideh Sheshpari
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue transplantation effectively restores hormonal function, endocrine balance, and fertility outcomes in patients with predisposing conditions and cancer treatments.

results.title: Restoration of ovarian endocrine function with encapsulated immune-isolated human ovarian xenograft in ovariectomized mice
results.author: Margaret A Brunette
results.takeaway: Encapsulated human ovarian tissue in an immune-isolating hydrogel-based capsule effectively restores ovarian endocrine function in ovariectomized mice, demonstrating integration with host physiology.

results.title: Reproductive and Endocrine Outcomes in a Cohort of Danish Women following Auto-Transplantation of Frozen/Thawed Ovarian Tissue from a Single Center
results.author: L. Colmorn
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is a safe and promising method for restoring fertility, with initial recovery of endocrine function and ongoing function in 7/14 women two years after transplantation.

results.title: Ovarian tissue transplantation: 10 years of experience at the Bologna University
results.author: R. Fabbri
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue transplantation (OTT) effectively restores ovarian function and fertility in women with premature ovarian insufficiency, with no alterations in menstrual cycle or hormonal levels.

results.title: Utility and Outcomes of Ovarian Tissue Cryopreservation and Transplantation for Gynecologic Cancers
results.author: M. Erden
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation and autologous transplantation are feasible options for preserving ovarian function in women with gynecologic cancers, but ovarian tissue cryopreservation is underutilized.

results.title: Evaluation of safety, feasibility and efficacy of intra-ovarian transplantation of autologous adipose derived mesenchymal stromal cells in idiopathic premature ovarian failure patients: non-randomized clinical trial, phase I, first in human
results.author: M. Mashayekhi
results.takeaway: Intra-ovarian transplantation of autologous adipose-derived stromal cells is safe and feasible, potentially improving ovarian function and menstruation in premature ovarian failure patients.

results.title: Influence of Autologous In Vitro Activation of Ovaries by Stem Cells and Growth Factors on Endocrine and Reproductive Function of Patients with Ovarian Insufficiency-A Clinical Trial Study
results.author: Suada Tinjić
results.takeaway: Autologous ovarian activation with stem cells and growth factors can potentially improve reproductive and endocrine function in patients with ovarian insufficiency, potentially delaying menopause and improving quality of life.

results.title: Recent advances in transplant medicine: Fertility restoration and redefining parentage in uterine and ovarian transplantation
results.author: Nimmala. Vaishnavi Sai
results.takeaway: Uterine and ovarian transplantation procedures show promising futures in treating infertility in women, with the type of transplantation mainly determining the genetic origin and reproductive success of the offspring.

results.title: P-455 Laparoscopic robot-assisted autologous transplantation of cryopreserved ovarian tissue: report of an operative technique and reproductive outcomes in a case series
results.author: M. Fastrez
results.takeaway: Laparoscopic robot-assisted autologous transplantation of ovarian tissue is feasible and safe, with comparable reproductive outcomes to published data.

results.title: New advances in ovarian autotransplantation to restore fertility in cancer patients
results.author: M. Salama
results.takeaway: Human ovary autotransplantation shows promise for fertility preservation in young women undergoing cancer treatments, offering unique advantages like immediate initiation of therapy and restoring endocrine and reproductive functions.

results.title: Fertility preservation before sterilizing treatment: cryopreservation of both ovaries to restore endocrine and reproductive functions.
results.author: Catherine Poirot
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation of both ovaries before sterilizing treatment can potentially restore both endocrine and reproductive functions in cancer survivors, potentially improving their quality of life.

results.title: Autologous Ovarian Tissue Transplantation
Robot-assisted orthotopic and heterotopic ovarian tissue transplantation techniques: surgical advances since our first success in 2000.
results.author: K. Oktay
results.takeaway: Robot-assisted orthotopic and heterotopic ovarian tissue transplantation techniques using Alloderm have successfully restored ovarian function and fertility in cancer patients, offering a promising fertility preservation option.

results.title: Autologous Transplantation of Human Ovarian Tissue
results.author: E. Taylan
results.takeaway: Autologous transplantation of frozen-thawed human ovarian tissue offers a practical option for fertility preservation in girls of prepubertal age and women undergoing potentially gonadotoxic therapies for oncological or nononcological diseases.

results.title: A Systematic Study on Reproductive Endocrine Function Recovery From Subcutaneous Ovarian Autotransplantation in Mice After 2 Weeks.
results.author: Hai-feng Cao
results.takeaway: Ovarian endocrine and reproductive function are restored within 2 weeks after subcutaneous ovarian autotransplantation in mice, making the 14th day a landmark for studying heterotopic ovarian transplantation in mice.

results.title: Assessment of long term endocrine function after transplantation of frozen-thawed human ovarian tissue to the heterotopic site: 10 year longitudinal follow-up study
results.author: S. S. Kim
results.journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
results.takeaway: Long-term endocrine function can be established with heterotopic transplantation of cryobanked human ovarian tissue, benefiting young cancer survivors with premature ovarian failure.

results.title: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation: Low usage rates and high live‐birth rate after transplantation
results.author: E. J. Hoekman
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation is an effective method to restore fertility in young women at risk of premature ovarian insufficiency after gonadotoxic treatment, but more knowledge is needed to offer it selectively.

results.title: Efficacy of ovarian tissue cryopreservation for fertility preservation: lessons learned from 545 cases
results.author: P. Jadoul
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation (OTC) has a 30% pregnancy rate after autotransplantation, but its real efficacy remains unclear due to limited follow-up and limited data.

results.title: History, Evolution and Current State of Ovarian Tissue Auto-Transplantation with Cryopreserved Tissue: A Successful Translational Research Journey from 1999 to 2020
results.author: L. Marin
results.takeaway: Ovarian transplantation with cryopreserved tissue has evolved to a point where live birth rates exceed 35% and is no longer considered experimental.

results.title: Two successful pregnancies following autotransplantation of frozen/thawed ovarian tissue.
results.author: C. Andersen
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation of ovarian tissue and autotransplantation can preserve fertility in women with malignant diseases, leading to two successful pregnancies.

results.title: 86 successful births and 9 ongoing pregnancies worldwide in women transplanted with frozen-thawed ovarian tissue: focus on birth and perinatal outcome in 40 of these children
results.author: A. K. Jensen
results.journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation of ovarian tissue is an established fertility preservation method, with 86 successful births and 9 ongoing pregnancies worldwide, and a mean gestation age of 39 weeks and mean birth weight of 3168 g.

results.title: Safety and efficacy of ovarian tissue autotransplantation: A systematic literature review.
results.author: E. Giovannopoulou
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue autotransplantation is a safe procedure with acceptable pregnancy rates, but more data is needed for confirmation.

results.title: Cryopreservation and autotransplantation of human ovarian tissue prior to cytotoxic therapy--a technique in its infancy but already successful in fertility preservation.
results.author: M. von Wolff
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation and transplantation of ovarian tissue prior to cytotoxic therapy is a promising and successful method for fertility preservation in selected cancer patients.

results.title: Strategies to safely use cryopreserved ovarian tissue to restore fertility after cancer: a systematic review.
results.author: L. Eijkenboom
results.takeaway: Experimental strategies are being developed to safely use cryopreserved ovarian tissue to restore fertility after cancer, but require further research and ethical considerations.

results.title: Autologous Ovarian Tissue Transplantation: Preoperative Assessment and Preparation of the Patient
results.author: V. Turan
results.takeaway: Preoperative evaluation and preparation of patients for ovarian tissue transplantation is crucial to improve success rates and ensure safety during the procedure.

results.title: Autotransplantation of cryopreserved ovarian tissue – effective method of fertility preservation in cancer patients
results.author: Z. Gamzatova
results.takeaway: Autotransplantation of cryopreserved ovarian tissue is a promising and effective method for fertility preservation in cancer patients, restoring both fertility and endocrine function without delay in cancer treatment.

results.title: Perinatal outcomes of pregnancies following autologous cryopreserved ovarian tissue transplantation: a systematic review with pooled analysis
results.author: M. Erden
results.journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
results.takeaway: Autologous cryopreserved ovarian tissue transplantation does not increase perinatal complications compared to the general pregnant population, except for preeclampsia, which may be due to chemotherapy exposure, underlying medical conditions, and common use of assisted reproductive techniques.

results.title: Recent advances in the field of ovarian tissue cryopreservation and opportunities for research
results.author: C. Ladanyi
results.journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
results.takeaway: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation and auto-transplantation techniques show potential for restoring fertility in post-pubertal women, with potential for becoming a standard of care for women facing sterility due to ovarian damage.

results.title: Ovarian cryopreservation with rapamycin improves fertility restoration in a murine orthotopic transplantation model
results.author: Jules Bindels
results.takeaway: Cryopreservation with rapamycin improves fertility restoration in mice, potentially resolving follicular loss after grafting and improving chances of pregnancy.

35.state: Ovarian-tissue autotransplantation is established clinical practice for fertility preservation: cryopreservation followed by orthotopic grafting restores hormonal function and fertility. The early proof that an autografted ovary keeps working anticipates a routine procedure.

facets.q: Can gastropod snails regenerate eyes and sensory tentacles?
What is the cellular mechanism of eye and tentacle regeneration in pulmonate snails?

results.title: Role of the Age Factor in Eye Regeneration in the Gastropod Achatina fulica
results.author: O. S. Tartakovskaya
results.journal: Biology Bulletin of the Russian Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: In the snail Achatina fulica, eye regeneration decreases with age, but the period of regeneration increases, with regenerated eyes remaining smaller than normal eyes even after six months.

results.title: Implanted cerebral ganglia produce supernumerary eyes and tentacles in host snails
results.author: S. Moffett
results.takeaway: Implanted cerebral ganglia in host snails can produce supernumerary eyes and tentacles, connecting with the host nervous system and periphery.

results.title: A genetically tractable non-vertebrate system to study complete camera-type eye regeneration
results.author: A. Accorsi
results.takeaway: Apple snail Pomacea canaliculata can fully regenerate camera-type eyes, offering a genetically tractable model for studying complex sensory organ regeneration.

results.title: [Age factor in eye regeneration of the gastropod mollusk Achatina fulica].
results.author: O. S. Tartakovskaia
results.takeaway: The ability to regenerate the eye in the snail Achatina fulica increases with age, but the number of regenerated tentacles decreases and the regeneration period increases.

results.title: Restoration of morphological and functional integrity in the regenerating eye of the giant African land snail Achatina fulica
results.author: M. Bobkova
results.takeaway: The regenerating eye of the giant African land snail Achatina fulica is essentially the same as the original eye, with the exception of a smaller size due to reduced retinal cells.

results.title: Studies on eye regeneration in a snail, Helix aspersa.
results.author: R. M. Eakin
results.takeaway: Nerve fibers influence the regeneration of the eye in Helix aspersa snails, with a pigmented interstitial cell playing a prominent role in the tentacle regeneration process.

results.title: A New Protocol of Computer-Assisted Image Analysis Highlights the Presence of Hemocytes in the Regenerating Cephalic Tentacles of Adult Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: G. Bergamini
results.takeaway: A new computer-assisted image analysis protocol can help quantify hemocytes in regenerating tissues, aiding in understanding the role of immune-related cells in sensory organ regeneration.

results.title: Morphology and odor sensitivity of regenerated snail tentacles.
results.author: R. Chase
results.takeaway: Regenerated snail tentacles show accurate size regulation and sensory cell regeneration, with odor sensitivity recovering to equivalent levels as intact tentacles after 10 weeks post-lesion.

results.title: The Golden Apple Snail Pomacea canaliculata: From Zygotes to Stable Mutant Lines.
results.author: Asmita Gattamraju
results.takeaway: Golden apple snails (Pomacea canaliculata) are a genetically tractable model organism for studying sensory organ regeneration and other aspects of molluscan biology.
