results.title: Complete Regeneration of a Camera‐type Eye in the Research Organism Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: A. Accorsi
results.takeaway: Pomacea canaliculata freshwater snails can regenerate their camera-type eyes after complete amputation, offering potential for genome editing and gene function perturbation technologies.

results.title: Clodronate Liposome-Mediated Phagocytic Hemocyte Depletion Affects the Regeneration of the Cephalic Tentacle of the Invasive Snail, Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: G. Bergamini
results.takeaway: Clodronate liposomes temporarily deplete phagocytic hemocytes in freshwater snails, halting tentacle regeneration onset and altering gene expressions related to oxygen transport, immunity, clotting, and inflammation.

results.title: Biological activities of gastropods secretions: snail and slug slime
results.author: Muhammad Rashad
results.takeaway: Snail and slug slime has diverse biological activities, including defense mechanisms, potential therapeutic applications, and exploration of its components may lead to new bioactive molecules.

results.title: Neural regeneration in gastropod molluscs.
results.author: S. Moffett
results.takeaway: Snails exhibit neural regeneration after injuries, demonstrating adaptability and potential for future applications in promoting neural regeneration in mammals.

results.title: Regeneration of the visual system in gastropods (Mollusca)
results.author: O. Tuchina
results.takeaway: Gastropods show extensive visual projections across the brain, potentially revealing the neuronal basis of their behavioral reactions.

results.title: Eye regeneration in the mystery snail.
results.author: M. Bever
results.takeaway: Mystery snails can regenerate their eyes completely after amputation, demonstrating a similar process to that of amphibian limbs.

results.title: Structure and regeneration of the eyes of strombid gastropods
results.author: H. P. Hughes
results.takeaway: Strombid gastropods' eyes regenerate into fully formed, miniature organs within 5-16 days after amputation, with two types of retinal cells and microvilli developing quickly.

results.title: Electroretinographic and ultrastructural study of the regenerated eye of the snail Cryptomphallus aspersa.
results.author: D. Flores
results.takeaway: Regenerated eyes of snails show similar electroretinographic responses to control eyes, but repetitive stimulation leads to fatigue, with fatigue being influenced by light intensity.

results.title: Ocular tentacle regeneration and associated malformations in Stramonita haemastoma (Gastropoda: Muricidae)
results.author: Tahani El Ayari
results.takeaway: Tentacular malformations in Stramonita haemastoma marine gastropods are often deficient, indicating the capacity for regeneration and potential as a model for regeneration studies.

results.title: Ommatophore (Eyestalk) Magic: Lobatus costatus.
results.author: I. Schwab
results.takeaway: The conch (Lobatus costatus) has a unique eye system with modified tentacles that support olfactory and visual organs, and can regenerate eyes after injury or loss.

results.title: Metabolism of the snailCryptozona ligulata during regeneration of optic tentacles
results.author: O. V. Subramanyam
results.takeaway: SnailCryptozona ligulata regenerates optic tentacles by increasing carbohydrates and glycogen, with a complete regeneration process taking 18-21 days.

results.title: Pomacea canaliculata: development and regeneration of complex eyes.
results.author: A. Accorsi
results.takeaway: The freshwater snail Pomacea canaliculata can regenerate complex camera-type eyes in a short time (1 month), making it a suitable model system for studying development and regeneration.

results.title: Serotoninergic reinnervation of regenerating tentacular sensory organs in a pulmonate snail, Cryptomphalus aspersa
results.author: V. Flores
results.takeaway: Serotonin (5-HT) plays a role in the regeneration process of tentacular sensory organs in the pulmonate snail Cryptomphalus aspersa, suggesting reciprocal developmental interactions with regenerating tentacular tissues.

results.title: The transcription factor Zic4 promotes tentacle formation and prevents epithelial transdifferentiation in Hydra
results.author: M. Vogg
results.takeaway: Zic4 plays a key role in tentacle formation and maintenance in Hydra, preventing epithelial transdifferentiation during regeneration.

results.title: Regeneration in Stentor coeruleus
results.author: W. Marshall
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Single cells can regenerate missing structures, resembling animal development and regeneration processes, and may have evolved by exploiting pre-existing subcellular developmental strategies from unicellular ancestors.

results.title: Effects of tentacle amputation and regeneration on the morphology and activity of the olfactory center of the terrestrial slug Limax valentianus
results.author: R. Matsuo
results.takeaway: Tentacle amputation and regeneration in terrestrial slugs Limax valentianus cause degeneration and re-innervation of olfactory nerves, affecting the basal state of local field potential oscillation.

results.title: Distinct stem-like cell populations facilitate functional regeneration of the Cladonema medusa tentacle
results.author: S. Fujita
results.takeaway: The regeneration of tentacles in Cladonema medusa jellyfish relies on both resident homeostatic stem cells and repair-specific proliferative cells, enabling efficient appendage regeneration and providing insight into blastema formation across animal evolution.

results.title: Mesoglea Extracellular Matrix Reorganization during Regenerative Process in Anemonia viridis (Forskål, 1775)
results.author: M. Parisi
results.takeaway: The mesoglea, an extracellular matrix in anemones, plays a key role in wound healing and tissue regeneration by modifying consistency and stiffness, acting as a scaffold for reconstruction of missing tissues.

123.state: Gastropod eye and tentacle regeneration is an emerging model: apple snails (Pomacea) fully regrow camera-type eyes and are genetically tractable, and implanted ganglia can induce supernumerary eyes. The pond-snail result is an early entry in invertebrate eye-regeneration research.

facets.q: What is the comparative regenerative capacity across gastropod molluscs?
Which body parts can gastropods regenerate and how does this vary phylogenetically?

results.title: Regeneration in the dorids exemplified by Onchidoris muricata (Gastropoda, Nudibranchia)
results.author: E. Nikitenko
results.takeaway: Dorid molluscs, specifically Onchidoris muricata, show varying regenerative abilities in chemosensory organs and dorsal body outgrowths, likely linked to their unique subepidermal calcite spicule complex.

results.title: Neural regeneration in gastropod molluscs.
results.author: S. Moffett
results.takeaway: Snails exhibit neural regeneration after injuries, demonstrating adaptability and potential for future applications in promoting neural regeneration in mammals.

results.title: Molluscs on acid: gastropod shell repair and strength in acidifying oceans
results.author: D. Coleman
results.takeaway: Ocean acidification significantly reduces the survival and protection of less resistant mollusc species, potentially increasing predation pressure and competition within intertidal communities.

results.title: Among-individual differences in risk-taking behavior are associated with shell repair rate but not growth in a marine gastropod
results.author: T. Cornwell
results.takeaway: Bolder individuals in the marine gastropod Buccinum undatum repair shells more slowly during the second stage of repair, but growth is not correlated with either boldness or repair rates.

results.title: Relative cost of producing skeletal organic matrix versus calcification: Evidence from marine gastropods
results.author: A. R. Palmer
results.takeaway: Producing skeletal organic matrix is more metabolically demanding than calcification, potentially explaining the evolutionary loss of high organic matrix skeletal microstructures in major invertebrate groups.

results.title: Deciphering regeneration through non-model animals: A century of experiments on cephalopod mollusks and an outlook at the future
results.author: Fabio de Sio
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Cephalopod mollusks offer unique insights into regeneration mechanisms and their potential as valuable models for understanding complex cellular and molecular mechanisms in biology and medicine.

results.title: Molecular Determinants of Cephalopod Muscles and Their Implication in Muscle Regeneration
results.author: L. Zullo
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Cephalopod muscle types and structures may link to their high regenerative potential, potentially identifying conserved molecular pathways for regeneration in both invertebrate and vertebrate species.

results.title: A comparative analysis of immune memory in bivalve and gastropod Mollusks: From mechanistic insights to practical applications
results.author: Hongyu Li
results.takeaway: Oysters exhibit "trained immunity" and can be used to combat pathogens like Ostreid herpesvirus 1 in aquaculture, with potential applications in disease management.

results.title: A congruent topology for deep gastropod relationships
results.author: T. J. Cunha
results.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
results.takeaway: This study resolves deep relationships among five major gastropod lineages, revealing that vetigastropods and patellogastropods are sister taxa, and neritimorphs are the sister group to caenogastropods and heterobranchs.

results.title: Comparative regenerative mechanisms across different mammalian tissues
results.author: S. Iismaa
results.takeaway: Recent insights into molecular mechanisms of regeneration in various mammalian tissues show potential for future regenerative therapies, despite constraints like immune responses, extracellular matrix composition, age, injury type, and physiological adaptations.

results.title: What Determines the Regenerative Capacity in Animals
results.author: Andong Zhao
results.takeaway: Regenerative capacity varies greatly across animals, with factors such as stem cell properties, dedifferentiation and transdifferentiation potentials, gene expression, epigenetic regulators, and immune responses playing a role.

results.title: Cephalopod Tissue Regeneration: Consolidating Over a Century of Knowledge
results.author: Pamela Imperadore
results.takeaway: Cephalopod mollusks can regenerate several structures, but many aspects of tissue regeneration remain largely unexplored, potentially leading to important discoveries and approaches in regenerative medicine.

results.title: Culture of the gastropod Pomacea dolioides (Reeve, 1856): effects of calcium on growth, survival and shell regeneration
results.author: Rafaela Fernanda Batista Ferreira
results.takeaway: Calcium dissolved in water significantly influences the growth, survival, and shell regeneration of Pomacea dolioides gastropods, with 80 mg/L being the ideal concentration for optimal growth and fattening.

results.title: Towards a phylogeny of gastropod molluscs: an analysis using morphological characters
results.author: W. Ponder
results.journal: Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society
results.takeaway: Gastropods are a monophyletic clade, dividing into two primary groups: Eogastropoda and Prosobranchs.

results.title: A Comparative Analysis of Arm Regeneration in Astropecten indicus and Ophiocnemis marmorata
results.author: Asbar Banu Bazeer
results.journal: ILMU KELAUTAN: Indonesian Journal of Marine Sciences
results.takeaway: Ophiocnemis marmorata echinoderm regenerates arms rapidly and without scarring, making it a promising model for anti-fibrotic research and stem cell studies.

results.title: Body-size evolution in gastropods across the Plio-Pleistocene extinction in the western Atlantic
results.author: Brendan M. Anderson
results.takeaway: Gastropod body-size evolution during the Plio-Pleistocene extinction in the western Atlantic is more complex than in bivalves, with ecology and functional traits playing a significant role.

results.title: High-value compounds from the molluscs of marine and estuarine ecosystems as prospective functional food ingredients: An overview
results.author: K. Chakraborty
results.takeaway: High-value compounds and functional food ingredients from marine and estuarine molluscs can enhance immunity and reduce the risk of various ailments.

results.title: The adult shell matrix protein repertoire of the marine snail Crepidula is dominated by conserved genes that are also expressed in larvae
results.author: Rebecca N. Lopez-Anido
results.takeaway: The shell matrix proteins (SMPs) in marine snail Crepidula are mainly conserved genes expressed in larvae, suggesting modular expression patterns could facilitate shell morphological diversification.

results.title: Deciphering mollusc shell production: the roles of genetic mechanisms through to ecology, aquaculture and biomimetics
results.author: M. Clark
results.takeaway: Advances in genomic technologies and epigenetics enable molluscs to adapt shell shape, composition, and organic content to local conditions, potentially enhancing their resilience to climate change impacts.

results.title: Co-Option and De Novo Gene Evolution Underlie Molluscan Shell Diversity
results.author: F. Aguilera
results.takeaway: Molluscan shell diversity is driven by lineage-specific secretomes, which include rapidly evolving proteins and the recruitment and loss of ancient and recently evolved genes in the mantle regulatory network.

results.title: Extreme autotomy and whole-body regeneration in photosynthetic sea slugs.
results.author: Sayaka Mitoh
results.takeaway: Sacoglossan sea slugs can perform extreme autotomy, shedding their main body and heart, and regenerating a new body, potentially facilitating survival after autotomy and regeneration.

results.title: Neoblasts and the evolution of whole-body regeneration.
results.author: Andrew R. Gehrke
results.takeaway: Neoblasts play a conserved role in whole-body regeneration in flatworms and acoels, but their extrinsic inputs during regeneration vary, making the distinction between homology and convergence unclear.

results.title: Regeneration of the visual system in gastropods (Mollusca)
results.author: O. Tuchina
results.takeaway: Gastropods show extensive visual projections across the brain, potentially revealing the neuronal basis of their behavioral reactions.

results.title: The sea spider Pycnogonum litorale overturns the paradigm of the absence of axial regeneration in molting animals
results.author: Georg Brenneis
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: The sea spider Pycnogonum litorale can regrow almost complete segments and the terminal body region after transverse amputation, challenging the assumption of axial regeneration loss in molting animals.

results.title: Evolution and development of gastropod larval shell morphology: experimental evidence for mechanical defense and repair
results.author: C. Hickman
results.takeaway: Gastropod larval shells are fine-tuned for survival in plankton, with rapid repair and features that resist mechanical damage playing a significant role in their ability to thrive in the environment.

results.title: A phylogenomic backbone for gastropod molluscs.
results.author: J. Uribe
results.takeaway: This study supports the recognition of five subclasses in Gastropoda: Patellogastropoda, Vetigastropoda, Neritimorpha, Caenogastropoda, and Heterobranchia, with Patellogastropoda as the sister lineage to all other gastropod

results.title: Phylogenetic distribution of regeneration and asexual reproduction in Annelida: regeneration is ancestral and fission evolves in regenerative clades
results.author: E. Zattara
results.takeaway: Regeneration is ancestral in Annelida and regenerative ability is required for the evolution of fission, suggesting a strong relationship between the two processes.

results.title: Temperate Intertidal Ecosystems are Functionally Richer but More Vulnerable to Loss Than Tropical Ecosystems
results.author: Matilda Murley
results.takeaway: Temperate intertidal ecosystems have greater functional richness but are more vulnerable to loss than tropical ecosystems, as they are supported by fewer or single species.

results.title: Regeneration and regengrow in multicellular animals derive from the presence of processes of organ metamorphosis and continuous growth in their life cycles
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.takeaway: Regeneration is a basal ancestral property of aquatic animals, while terrestrial animals lack regeneration genes, relying on wound healing and scarring for repair.

results.title: Whole-Body Regeneration in Sponges: Diversity, Fine Mechanisms, and Future Prospects
results.author: A. Ereskovsky
results.takeaway: Sponges exhibit a wide variety of whole-body regeneration processes, making them promising models for studying the origin and early evolution of regeneration processes.

results.title: Towards comparative analyses of salamander limb regeneration
results.author: Varun B. Dwaraka
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Comparative studies of salamander species are necessary to reveal commonalities and differences in limb regeneration mechanisms, providing insights into salamander evolution.

results.title: Molecular phylogenetics of Caenogastropoda (Gastropoda: Mollusca).
results.author: D. Colgan
results.takeaway: This study supports the monophyly of Caenogastropoda and Hypsogastropoda, with some lineages still poorly resolved or unresolved due to rapid radiation after the Permian-Triassic extinction.

results.title: Do Not Lose Your Head over the Unequal Regeneration Capacity in Prolecithophoran Flatworms
results.author: Alexandra L. Grosbusch
results.takeaway: Prolecithophoran flatworms have diverse regeneration capacities, suggesting that head regeneration capacity is not an apomorphy of Adiaphanida.

results.title: A genome-based phylogeny for Mollusca is concordant with fossils and morphology.
results.author: Zeyuan Chen
results.takeaway: A genome-based phylogeny for Mollusca confirms a morphology-based phylogeny and shows widespread genomic variation, explaining both historical challenges and evolutionary success.

99.state: Comparative mollusc regeneration is active: varying capacities across dorids and other gastropods, neural regeneration after injury, and ocean-acidification effects on shell repair. Mapping who-regenerates-what across molluscs is the modern comparative program.

facets.q: Can freshwater snails regenerate amputated body parts and sensory organs?
What is the regenerative capacity of pulmonate freshwater gastropods?

results.title: A New Protocol of Computer-Assisted Image Analysis Highlights the Presence of Hemocytes in the Regenerating Cephalic Tentacles of Adult Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: G. Bergamini
results.takeaway: A new computer-assisted image analysis protocol can help quantify hemocytes in regenerating tissues, aiding in understanding the role of immune-related cells in sensory organ regeneration.

results.title: The Golden Apple Snail Pomacea canaliculata: From Zygotes to Stable Mutant Lines.
results.author: Asmita Gattamraju
results.takeaway: Golden apple snails (Pomacea canaliculata) are a genetically tractable model organism for studying sensory organ regeneration and other aspects of molluscan biology.

results.title: Clodronate Liposome-Mediated Phagocytic Hemocyte Depletion Affects the Regeneration of the Cephalic Tentacle of the Invasive Snail, Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: G. Bergamini
results.takeaway: Clodronate liposomes temporarily deplete phagocytic hemocytes in freshwater snails, halting tentacle regeneration onset and altering gene expressions related to oxygen transport, immunity, clotting, and inflammation.

results.title: A genetically tractable non-vertebrate system to study complete camera-type eye regeneration
results.author: A. Accorsi
results.takeaway: Apple snail Pomacea canaliculata can fully regenerate camera-type eyes, offering a genetically tractable model for studying complex sensory organ regeneration.

results.title: Eye regeneration in the mystery snail.
results.author: M. Bever
results.takeaway: Mystery snails can regenerate their eyes completely after amputation, demonstrating a similar process to that of amphibian limbs.

results.title: Restoration of morphological and functional integrity in the regenerating eye of the giant African land snail Achatina fulica
results.author: M. Bobkova
results.takeaway: The regenerating eye of the giant African land snail Achatina fulica is essentially the same as the original eye, with the exception of a smaller size due to reduced retinal cells.

results.title: Model systems for regeneration: Hydra
results.author: M. Vogg
results.takeaway: Freshwater polyps serve as a powerful model system for studying regeneration, with recent results questioning the classical self-enhancement and long-range inhibition model and highlighting the need for integrative explanations incorporating biochemical and mechanical signaling.

results.title: Pomacea canaliculata: development and regeneration of complex eyes.
results.author: A. Accorsi
results.takeaway: The freshwater snail Pomacea canaliculata can regenerate complex camera-type eyes in a short time (1 month), making it a suitable model system for studying development and regeneration.

results.title: Crustacean leg regeneration restores complex microanatomy and cell diversity
results.author: Alba Almazán
results.takeaway: Crustacean leg regeneration accurately replicates complex microanatomy and restores sensory function, with regenerated and uninjured legs having indistinguible cell-type composition and transcriptional profiles.

results.title: From two segments and beyond: Investigating the onset of regeneration in Syllis malaquini
results.author: Vanessa Spieß
results.takeaway: Syllis malaquini can successfully regenerate the whole body, with two to three segments needed and varying capacity depending on gut region.

results.title: ﻿Morphological analysis of regenerated antennae in the isopod Porcellioscaber (Isopoda, Crustacea), with emphasis on the main sensory structures
results.author: P. Zidar
results.takeaway: The regenerated antennae of the isopod Porcellioscaber retain their sensory functionality, as only the number of common sensory structures is reduced during regeneration.

results.title: Systemic Cell Cycle Re‐entry Following Amputation in Axolotl: Consequence and Mechanism
results.author: Jessica L. Whited
results.takeaway: Axolotl salamanders show a two-step model for limb regeneration, with systemic activation before local refinement at the injury site.

results.title: Electrical responses to amputation of the eye in the mystery snail.
results.author: M. Bever
results.takeaway: Ionic current may control eye regeneration in the mystery snail, with wound healing processes occurring within 60 hours post-amputation.

results.title: Potential Involvement of Snail Members in Neuronal Survival and Astrocytic Migration during the Gecko Spinal Cord Regeneration
results.author: Tingting Shen
results.takeaway: Snail1 and Snail3 proteins promote neuronal survival and astrocytic migration during gecko spinal cord regeneration, potentially offering clues for CNS repair in mammals.

results.title: Positional Memory in Vertebrate Regeneration: A Century's Insights from the Salamander Limb.
results.author: L. Otsuki
results.journal: Cold Spring Harbor perspectives in biology
results.takeaway: Positional memory in vertebrate regeneration, as demonstrated in the salamander limb model, could lead to improved tissue repair and tissue engineering for therapeutic purposes.

results.title: Extreme autotomy and whole-body regeneration in photosynthetic sea slugs.
results.author: Sayaka Mitoh
results.takeaway: Sacoglossan sea slugs can perform extreme autotomy, shedding their main body and heart, and regenerating a new body, potentially facilitating survival after autotomy and regeneration.

results.title: Effect of temperature and body size on anterior and posterior regeneration in Hermodice carunculata (Polychaeta, Amphinomidae)
results.author: Andrea Toso
results.takeaway: Hermodice carunculata can efficiently regenerate anterior body parts in 12-20 weeks at 22°C, with larger worms surviving 100% mortality during blastema formation.

results.title: Utilisation of Snails for Wound Healing: A Review
results.author: Diana Fadhilah
results.journal: Journal of Tropical Biodiversity and Biotechnology
results.takeaway: Snail species and their secretions, particularly mucus, show potential in wound healing, with compounds like mucopolysaccharides, polyphenols, peptides, and glycosaminoglycans contributing to the healing process.

results.title: Neural regeneration in gastropod molluscs.
results.author: S. Moffett
results.takeaway: Snails exhibit neural regeneration after injuries, demonstrating adaptability and potential for future applications in promoting neural regeneration in mammals.

results.title: Morphology and odor sensitivity of regenerated snail tentacles.
results.author: R. Chase
results.takeaway: Regenerated snail tentacles show accurate size regulation and sensory cell regeneration, with odor sensitivity recovering to equivalent levels as intact tentacles after 10 weeks post-lesion.

results.title: A survey of miRNAs involved in biomineralization and shell repair in the freshwater gastropod Lymnaea stagnalis
results.author: Nicolas Cerveau
results.takeaway: The freshwater snail Lymnaea stagnalis has 370 unique microRNAs, with 10 differentially regulated in shell regeneration, and most are novel.

results.title: Spontaneous Regeneration of the Central Nervous System in Gastropods
results.author: R. Matsuo
results.takeaway: Land slugs show remarkable brain regenerative ability, achieving full functional recovery after injury without exogenous interventions, suggesting enhanced neurogenesis plays a role in this process.

results.title: Gut microbiota in parasite-transmitting gastropods
results.author: Peipei Li
results.takeaway: Gut microbiomes in parasite-transmitting gastropods play a crucial role in controlling snail populations and snail-borne diseases, with potential applications in controlling snail-borne diseases.

results.title: Culture of the gastropod Pomacea dolioides (Reeve, 1856): effects of calcium on growth, survival and shell regeneration
results.author: Rafaela Fernanda Batista Ferreira
results.takeaway: Calcium dissolved in water significantly influences the growth, survival, and shell regeneration of Pomacea dolioides gastropods, with 80 mg/L being the ideal concentration for optimal growth and fattening.

results.title: Invertebrate herbivory during the regeneration phase: experiments with a freshwater angiosperm
results.author: A. Elger
results.takeaway: Invertebrate herbivory during regeneration of freshwater macrophytes can be a significant factor in plant community structure, with regenerating plants potentially avoiding it by modulating intrinsic characteristics or emerging under favorable environmental conditions.

results.title: Renal Accumulation and Hemocyte-Mediated Internalization After Acute Exposure to Injected Polyethylene Terephthalate Nanoplastics (PET-NPs) in the Freshwater Gastropod Pomacea canaliculata
results.author: Anita Ferri

results.title: Coprophagy : a supplementary food source for two freshwater gastropods ?
results.author: H. Brendelberger
results.takeaway: Coprophagy is a suitable strategy for freshwater snails to further degrade and utilize refractory material, potentially improving their growth and survival rates.

results.title: Metabolic equilibrium and reproductive resilience: Freshwater gastropods under nanoplastics exposure.
results.author: Ting Wang
results.takeaway: Nanoplastics significantly impact freshwater gastropods, causing stress responses and reproductive disturbances, but cellular energy allocation remains stable.

results.title: Observations on habitat and reproduction in the pulmonate, basommatophoran gastropod Latia neritoides Gray 1850 — the only bioluminescent freshwater mollusc in the world: With 6 figures in the text
results.author: S. Moore

results.title: Global diversity of gastropods (Gastropoda; Mollusca) in freshwater
results.author: E. Strong
results.takeaway: Freshwater gastropods face significant threats from habitat loss, degradation, and introduced fishes, with their status being unknown and their extinction rate likely underestimated.

results.title: Gastropod growth and survival as bioindicators of stress associated with high nutrients in the intertidal of a shallow temperate estuary
results.author: I. Marsden
results.takeaway: Gastropod growth and survival can serve as bioindicators of stress associated with high nutrients in temperate estuaries, helping monitor recovery from eutrophication.

results.title: The power of regeneration and the stem-cell kingdom: freshwater planarians (Platyhelminthes).
results.author: E. Saló
results.journal: BioEssays : news and reviews in molecular, cellular and developmental biology
results.takeaway: Freshwater planarians show remarkable regeneration and developmental plasticity, with their unique morphological plasticity and regenerative capability derived from a stable population of totipotent stem cells called "neoblasts".

results.title: Regeneration in the dorids exemplified by Onchidoris muricata (Gastropoda, Nudibranchia)
results.author: E. Nikitenko
results.takeaway: Dorid molluscs, specifically Onchidoris muricata, show varying regenerative abilities in chemosensory organs and dorsal body outgrowths, likely linked to their unique subepidermal calcite spicule complex.

results.title: Cross-species metabolomic analysis identifies uridine as a potent regeneration promoting factor
results.author: Zunpeng Liu
results.takeaway: Uridine, a pyrimidine nucleoside, can rejuvenate aged human stem cells and promote tissue regeneration across various species, offering new avenues for metabolic intervention in tissue repair.

results.title: Surveys that prioritize site number over time per site will result in better gastropod status assessments: a case study on the rediscovery of Big Black Rocksnail
results.author: Calvin R. Rezac
results.takeaway: Prioritizing sampling as many sites as possible under favorable conditions in freshwater gastropod surveys can lead to better conservation outcomes.

results.title: Chapter 10 – Mollusca: Gastropoda
results.author: K. Brown
results.takeaway: Gastropods are the most diverse class of molluscs, with over 50,000 species in marine and freshwater Caenogastropoda and 20,000 in terrestrial Pulmonata subclasses.

results.title: Phylogenetic relationships and evolution of pulmonate gastropods (Mollusca): new insights from increased taxon sampling.
results.author: B. Dayrat
results.takeaway: Pulmonate gastropods show monophyly of Systellommatophora and Ellobiidae, with deep nodes still uncertain and uncertainties in early fossil records impacting our understanding of their macro-evolutionary history.

results.title: Current extinction rate in European freshwater gastropods greatly exceeds that of the late Cretaceous mass extinction
results.author: T. Neubauer
results.takeaway: European freshwater gastropod extinction rates are three orders of magnitude higher than those during the Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction, indicating a severe impact on freshwater biota for millions of years to come.

results.title: Contrasting reproductive biology of two hydrothermal gastropods from the Mid-Atlantic Ridge: implications for resilience of vent communities
results.author: J. Marticorena
results.takeaway: Two hydrothermal gastropod species from the Mid-Atlantic Ridge have contrasting reproductive strategies, impacting their resilience to natural and anthropogenic disturbances.

46.state: Freshwater snails are now a tractable regeneration model: apple and mystery snails fully regrow eyes, and hemocyte-depletion experiments show phagocytes are required for tentacle regeneration. This early snail survey anticipates it.

facets.q: Can the polychaete Ophryotrocha regenerate its anterior end including the head?
Is Ophryotrocha a model for annelid anterior regeneration and ageing?

results.title: Regeneration in the gonochoristic polychaete Ophryotrocha notoglandulata
results.author: H. Pfannenstiel
results.takeaway: The gonochoristic polychaete Ophryotrocha notoglandulata can regenerate both anterior and posterior segments, with regeneration capacity being identical in both male and female forms, and the prostomium not necessary for caudal regeneration.

results.title: Morphological and molecular features of early regeneration in the marine annelid Ophryotrocha xiamen
results.author: Ruanni Chen
results.takeaway: The marine annelid Ophryotrocha xiamen shows early regeneration, with wound healing achieved within one day and blastema formation one day later, and 243 genes identified in metabolism and signal transduction.

results.title: A NOTE ON AN ABNORMAL
results.author: Leonnates Jousseaumei
results.takeaway: Polychaetes can regenerate lost or damaged parts, with new segments and heads emerging at the anterior end, after injury, and this power is extremely valuable for their survival.

results.title: A Comprehensive Ultrastructure and Transcriptome Analyses of Early Regeneration in Ophryotrocha Xiamen Sp. Nov. (Annelida: Dorvilleidae)
results.author: Ruanni Chen
results.takeaway: Ophryotrocha Xiamen Sp. Nov. is a suitable model species for studying early regeneration mechanisms in annelids, with similar features to other invertebrates.

results.title: Aspects of Regeneration in Annelids
results.author: L. Hyman
results.takeaway: Annelids, all gradations in ability to regenerate completely occur, with anterior regeneration being most interesting, as it allows for more easily followed regenerative changes and quantitative measures.

results.title: Cellular and molecular profiles of anterior nervous system regeneration in Diopatra claparedii Grube, 1878 (Annelida, Polychaeta)
results.author: Mohd Ulul Ilmie Ahmad Nazri
results.takeaway: The polychaete Diopatra claparedii Grube, 1878 shows cellular and molecular changes during early nervous system regeneration, providing insights into regeneration within Annelida and understanding neurodegenerative diseases.

results.title: Simultaneous anterior and posterior regeneration and other growth phenomena in Maldanid polychaetes
results.author: G. Moment

results.title: The complete mitochondrial genome of a marine polychaete, Ophryotrocha xiamenensis (Annelida: Dorvilleidae)
results.author: Yiping Feng
results.takeaway: The complete mitochondrial genome of Ophryotrocha xiamenensis marine polychaete reveals its regeneration and evolution, placing it within the 'labronica' clade within the genus.

results.title: Experiments on anterior regeneration in Eurythoe complanata ("Polychaeta", Amphinomidae): reconfiguration of the nervous system and its function for regeneration
results.author: Monika C. M. Müller
results.takeaway: In Eurythoe complanata, nervous system regeneration occurs in two phases, with extirpation of one ganglion slowing regeneration and extirpation of more than one ganglion completely preventing it.

results.title: A Revised Spiralian Homeobox Gene Classification Incorporating New Polychaete Transcriptomes Reveals a Diverse TALE Class and a Divergent Hox Gene
results.author: Thomas B Barton-Owen
results.takeaway: This study reveals a diverse TALE class and divergent Hox gene in Spirobranchus lamarcki's operculum regeneration, improving our understanding of homeobox gene evolution and regenerative mechanisms.

results.title: Anterior regeneration in the spionid polychaetes Dipolydora quadrilobata and Pygospio elegans
results.author: S. Lindsay
results.takeaway: Both Dipolydora quadrilobata and Pygospio elegans can regenerate anterior tissue, with worms regrowing normal but smaller heads and palps after 16 days, regardless of tissue loss.

results.title: Do Not Lose Your Head over the Unequal Regeneration Capacity in Prolecithophoran Flatworms
results.author: Alexandra L. Grosbusch
results.takeaway: Prolecithophoran flatworms have diverse regeneration capacities, suggesting that head regeneration capacity is not an apomorphy of Adiaphanida.

results.title: Cell proliferation dynamics in regeneration of the operculum head appendage in the annelid Pomatoceros lamarckii.
results.author: Réka Szabó
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: The operculum head appendage in Pomatoceros lamarckii regenerates rapidly and consistently through a combination of early morphallactic stage and widespread proliferative activity within the filament.

results.title: Developmental onset of planarian whole-body regeneration depends on axis reset
results.author: C. Booth

results.title: Morphology Characterization of Anterior Regeneration in Diopatra claparedii
results.author: Nurul Nadirah Razali
results.takeaway: This study presents a reproducible protocol for inducing and analyzing anterior regeneration in Diopatra claparedii, providing a foundation for future investigations in annelid regeneration.

results.title: Effect of temperature and body size on anterior and posterior regeneration in Hermodice carunculata (Polychaeta, Amphinomidae)
results.author: Andrea Toso
results.takeaway: Hermodice carunculata can efficiently regenerate anterior body parts in 12-20 weeks at 22°C, with larger worms surviving 100% mortality during blastema formation.

results.title: Regeneration of the flatworm Prosthiostomum siphunculus (Polycladida, Platyhelminthes)
results.author: T. Schadt
results.takeaway: The flatworm Prosthiostomum siphunculus can regenerate submarginal eyes, cerebral eyes, pharynx, intestine, and sucker, with partial brain intact, but no regeneration of missing organs without the brain.

results.title: Caudal regeneration in the absence of a brain in two species of sedentary polychaetes.
results.author: S. D. Hill
results.journal: Journal of embryology and experimental morphology
results.takeaway: Two species of sedentary polychaetes, Branchiomma nigromaculata and Chaetopterus variopedatus, can undergo caudal regeneration without a brain, suggesting that ventral ganglia play a role in posterior regeneration.

results.title: About Regeneration of Alien Polychaete Polydora websteri (Annelida: Spionidae)
results.author: E. Lisitskaya
results.takeaway: The alien polychaete Polydora websteri can regenerate both anterior and posterior body parts, with a direct relationship between water temperature and regeneration success.

results.title: Anterior regeneration in the polychaete Marenzelleria viridis (Annelida: Spionidae)
results.author: T. A. Whitford
results.takeaway: The polychaete Marenzelleria viridis can regenerate its anterior end within 14 days, making it a valuable model organism for studying regeneration mechanisms.

results.title: Developmental and molecular biology of annelid regeneration: a comparative review of recent studies.
results.author: B. Özpolat
results.takeaway: Recent studies reveal conserved and variable features in annelid regeneration, offering insights into the developmental basis and evolution of this process.

results.title: Regeneration in the Segmented Annelid Capitella teleta
results.author: E. Seaver
results.takeaway: The segmented annelid Capitella teleta exhibits robust posterior regeneration, making it a valuable model for studying adult regeneration and its molecular and cellular control.

results.title: It Cuts Both Ways: An Annelid Model System for the Study of Regeneration in the Laboratory and in the Classroom
results.author: Veronica G. Martinez Acosta
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: An annelid model system can effectively study regeneration, providing insights for regenerative medicine and providing a primer for biology for researchers and educators.

results.title: Telomere maintenance during anterior regeneration and aging in the freshwater annelid Aeolosoma viride
results.author: Chi-Fan Chen
results.takeaway: Aging affects Aeolosoma viride regeneration, but timely regulation of telomerase functions is crucial for regeneration in this freshwater annelid.

results.title: Distinct patterns of gene expression during regeneration and asexual reproduction in the annelid Pristina leidyi
results.author: Irene del Olmo
results.journal: Journal of Experimental Zoology. Part B, Molecular and Developmental Evolution
results.takeaway: The molecular mechanisms underlying anterior regeneration and asexual reproduction in annelids are conserved across metazoans, with 291 genes upregulated during regeneration and 130 genes upregulated during asexual reproduction.

results.title: Comparative Regeneration Dynamics of Platynereis dumerilii and Pygospio elegans (Annelida): Morphological and Cellular Events.
results.author: Elena L Novikova
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Platynereis dumerilii fails to regenerate head structures early in the restoration process, while Pygospio elegans can regenerate both head and tail after amputation.

results.title: Comparative transcriptomics in Syllidae (Annelida) indicates that posterior regeneration and regular growth are comparable, while anterior regeneration is a distinct process
results.author: R. P. Ribeiro
results.takeaway: Syllids show high posterior regeneration abilities, but anterior regeneration varies across species, with gene expression changes in both processes.

results.title: The people behind the papers - Alicia Boyd and Elaine Seaver.
results.takeaway: Capitella teleta, an annelid worm, gains regenerative ability with age, suggesting metamorphosis serves as a switch in annelid regenerative potential.

results.title: Morphological investigations of posttraumatic regeneration in Timarete cf. punctata (Annelida: Cirratulidae)
results.author: Michael Weidhase
results.takeaway: Posttraumatic regeneration in Timarete cf. punctata reveals complex musculature and nervous system redevelopment patterns, with anterior regeneration showing similarities to larval development.

results.title: Evolutionary Aspects of Annelid Regeneration
results.author: V. G. Martinez-Acosta
results.takeaway: Annelids vary in their ability to regenerate body segments, with tail regeneration being nearly universal and head regeneration depending on the number of segments lost.

results.title: Distribution of segment regeneration ability in the Annelida.
results.author: Alexandra E. Bely
results.takeaway: The ability to regenerate posteriorly is nearly universal in annelids, while the ability to regenerate anteriorly is common but less widespread, with a dozen groups missing this ability.

results.title: Nervous system regeneration in Typosyllis antoni (Annelida: Syllidae)
results.author: Michael Weidhase
results.journal: Zoologischer Anzeiger – A Journal of Comparative Zoology
results.takeaway: Posttraumatic regeneration in Typosyllis antoni reveals that both anterior and posterior nervous systems regenerate, with neurites forming loops and reorganizing in a terminal commissure.

results.title: Annelids as models of germ cell and gonad regeneration
results.author: B. Özpolat
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Annelids can regenerate germ cells, challenging the belief in a germline-soma barrier, and offering potential insights into the origins of germ cell regeneration in sexually reproducing organisms.

47.state: Ophryotrocha is now a model for annelid regeneration and ageing: recent transcriptomic and ultrastructural work dissects anterior and posterior regeneration in Ophryotrocha species. Its 1905 debut as a regeneration animal goes uncited.

facets.q: How is anterior versus posterior identity (Wnt/beta-catenin axial polarity) controlled in annelid and leech regeneration?
Do leeches and annelids regenerate head and tail ends with different capacity?

results.title: Wnt/β-catenin signaling promotes posterior axial regeneration in non-regenerative tissue of the annelid Capitella teleta
results.author: Lauren F. Kunselman
results.takeaway: Wnt/-catenin signaling activates latent regenerative potential in non-regenerating tissue, but additional cell signaling pathways are needed for complete regeneration.

results.title: Wnt/β-catenin signalling is required for pole-specific chromatin remodeling during planarian regeneration
results.author: Eudald Pascual-Carreras
results.takeaway: Wnt/-catenin signaling plays a crucial role in specifying tissue identity during planarian regeneration, with homeobox transcription factors and chromatin-remodeling proteins as direct targets.

results.title: Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway regulates the regeneration of the annelid Lumbriculus variegatus.
results.author: Aldo Arturo Tellez-Garcia
results.takeaway: The canonical Wnt pathway plays a crucial role in annelid regeneration, with its activity being finely regulated to ensure smooth transitions between different cellular processes.

results.title: activin-2 is required for regeneration of polarity on the planarian anterior-posterior axis
results.author: Jennifer K. Cloutier
results.takeaway: Activin-2 plays a crucial role in determining the outcome of anterior-posterior axis-patterning events during regeneration in planarians.

results.title: Wnt, Ptk7, and FGFRL expression gradients control trunk positional identity in planarian regeneration
results.author: R. Lander
results.takeaway: Planarian regeneration relies on the restoration of regional identity through the interpretation and re-establishment of axis-wide transcriptional gradients of signaling molecules.

results.title: Β-catenin-dependent control of positional information along the AP body axis in planarians involves a teashirt family member.
results.author: H. Reuter
results.takeaway: The teashirt family member, tsh, plays a crucial role in controlling tissue polarity in planarians by regulating cell identity during homeostasis and regeneration.

results.title: Wnt signaling and the polarity of the primary body axis.
results.author: Christian P. Petersen
results.takeaway: Wnt signaling through beta-catenin controls posterior identity during body plan formation in most bilaterally symmetric animals, with a pre-bilaterian role in axial patterning.

results.title: Effects of GSK3β inhibition in the regeneration of Syllis malaquini (Syllidae, Annelida)
results.author: R. P. Ribeiro
results.takeaway: GSK3 inhibition in the annelid Syllis malaquini affects blastema differentiation and resegmentation, suggesting a potential link to gene regulatory networks in regeneration.

results.title: A wound-induced Wnt expression program controls planarian regeneration polarity
results.author: Christian P. Petersen
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Wnt signaling plays a crucial role in resetting the normal polarized features of the body axis during regeneration in planarians.

results.title: Planarian Hedgehog/Patched establishes anterior–posterior polarity by regulating Wnt signaling
results.author: S. Yazawa
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Hedgehog signaling in planarians regulates anterior-posterior polarity by regulating Wnt gene transcription, affecting head or tail regeneration based on the body's preexisting nervous system.

results.title: Smed-βcatenin-1 Is Required for Anteroposterior Blastema Polarity in Planarian Regeneration
results.author: Christian P. Petersen
results.takeaway: Smed-betacatenin-1 is a key gene in planarian regeneration, regulating the regeneration of heads at anterior-facing wounds and tails at posterior-facing wounds.

results.title: Transcriptomic analysis of three annelid species: looking for markers of positional information
results.author: Sofia Platova
results.takeaway: Spatial gene expression in annelid species reveals conserved molecular systems involved in posterior growth and positional identity, with species-specific modifications affecting regeneration capacity.

results.title: Establishment of the Axial Polarity and Cell Fate in Metazoa via Canonical Wnt Signaling: New Insights from Sponges and Annelids
results.author: V. Kozin
results.takeaway: The Wnt/-catenin pathway plays a crucial role in establishing the body plan in multicellular animals, with potential applications in various processes and diverse taxa.

results.title: A Wnt11 and Dishevelled signaling pathway acts prior to injury to control wound polarization for the onset of planarian regeneration
results.author: David I. Gittin
results.takeaway: A Wnt11 and Dishevelled signaling pathway acts prior to injury to control wound polarization and muscle orientation in planarian regeneration, using pre-existing tissue information to determine the outcome of wound-induced signals.

results.title: The planarian dorsal–ventral boundary regulates anterior–posterior axis growth and patterning
results.author: Chloe L Maybrun
results.takeaway: The planarian dorsal-ventral boundary (DVB) regulates anterior-posterior axis growth and patterning, enabling coordination of orthogonal positional information during regeneration.

results.title: Canonical Wnt signaling Is Involved in Anterior Regeneration of the Annelid Aeolosoma viride
results.author: Cheng-Yi Chen
results.takeaway: The canonical Wnt signaling pathway plays a role in regulating blastemal cellular responses during annelid regeneration, with dynamic activation and inhibition occurring at different stages.

results.title: Localization of planarian β-CATENIN-1 reveals multiple roles during anterior-posterior regeneration and organogenesis
results.author: M. Sureda-Gomez
results.takeaway: Planarian -catenin-1 has an anteroposterior gradient and plays multiple roles in anterior regeneration and organogenesis, including germline specification.

results.title: A PAK family kinase and the Hippo/Yorkie pathway modulate WNT signaling to functionally integrate body axes during regeneration
results.author: Viraj Doddihal
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: The H/YKI pathway and PAK family kinase help integrate body axes during regeneration in planarians, promoting coordinated growth and patterning during regeneration.

results.title: The Role of Early Bioelectric Signals in the Regeneration of Planarian Anterior/Posterior Polarity.
results.author: Fallon Durant
results.takeaway: Early bioelectric signals within 3 hours of injury are crucial for proper anterior-posterior polarity formation in planarian regeneration, regulating gene expression and anatomy over the following 10 days.

results.title: Evolutionary Aspects of Annelid Regeneration
results.author: V. G. Martinez-Acosta
results.takeaway: Annelids vary in their ability to regenerate body segments, with tail regeneration being nearly universal and head regeneration depending on the number of segments lost.

results.title: Regeneration in Annelids
results.author: M. Zoran
results.takeaway: Annelids can regenerate tail segments nearly universally, but the ability to regenerate head segments varies among species, suggesting regeneration ability may be an ancient trait lost during evolution.

results.title: Comparative Aspects of Annelid Regeneration: Towards Understanding the Mechanisms of Regeneration
results.author: R. P. Kostyuchenko
results.takeaway: Annelids exhibit diverse regeneration abilities, with some showing extensive regeneration and others showing limited tissue regeneration, highlighting the need to understand the cellular and molecular basis of regeneration in annelids.

results.title: Aspects of Regeneration in Annelids
results.author: L. Hyman
results.takeaway: Annelids, all gradations in ability to regenerate completely occur, with anterior regeneration being most interesting, as it allows for more easily followed regenerative changes and quantitative measures.

results.title: Comparative Regeneration Dynamics of Platynereis dumerilii and Pygospio elegans (Annelida): Morphological and Cellular Events.
results.author: Elena L Novikova
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Platynereis dumerilii fails to regenerate head structures early in the restoration process, while Pygospio elegans can regenerate both head and tail after amputation.

results.title: Secondary‐tail formation during stolonization in the Japanese green syllid, Megasyllis nipponica
results.author: Daisuke S. Sato
results.takeaway: Secondary-tail formation in Megasyllis nipponica during stolonization shows unique features, suggesting adaptive adaptation for rapid posterior end recovery.

results.title: What Role Do Annelid Neoblasts Play? A Comparison of the Regeneration Patterns in a Neoblast-Bearing and a Neoblast-Lacking Enchytraeid Oligochaete
results.author: M. Myohara
results.takeaway: Annelid neoblasts are more essential for efficient asexual reproduction than for regeneration of missing body parts, as they do not show antero-posterior gradient of regeneration ability along the body axis.

results.title: Distribution of segment regeneration ability in the Annelida.
results.author: Alexandra E. Bely
results.takeaway: The ability to regenerate posteriorly is nearly universal in annelids, while the ability to regenerate anteriorly is common but less widespread, with a dozen groups missing this ability.

results.title: Variations in cell plasticity and proliferation underlie distinct modes of regeneration along the antero-posterior axis in the annelid Platynereis.
results.author: Loïc Bideau
results.takeaway: Different degrees of cell plasticity contribute to the distinct modes of regeneration in the annelid Platynereis, with proliferative gut cells playing a role but not stem cells.

results.title: It Cuts Both Ways: An Annelid Model System for the Study of Regeneration in the Laboratory and in the Classroom
results.author: Veronica G. Martinez Acosta
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: An annelid model system can effectively study regeneration, providing insights for regenerative medicine and providing a primer for biology for researchers and educators.

results.title: Latent regeneration abilities persist following recent evolutionary loss in asexual annelids
results.author: Alexandra E. Bely
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Latent regeneration abilities can persist in asexual annelids, even after recent evolutionary loss, if the wound site contains elements characteristic of actively growing tissues.

results.title: From two segments and beyond: Investigating the onset of regeneration in Syllis malaquini
results.author: Vanessa Spieß
results.takeaway: Syllis malaquini can successfully regenerate the whole body, with two to three segments needed and varying capacity depending on gut region.

results.title: Regeneration in the Segmented Annelid Capitella teleta
results.author: E. Seaver
results.takeaway: The segmented annelid Capitella teleta exhibits robust posterior regeneration, making it a valuable model for studying adult regeneration and its molecular and cellular control.

results.title: Effect of temperature and body size on anterior and posterior regeneration in Hermodice carunculata (Polychaeta, Amphinomidae)
results.author: Andrea Toso
results.takeaway: Hermodice carunculata can efficiently regenerate anterior body parts in 12-20 weeks at 22°C, with larger worms surviving 100% mortality during blastema formation.

results.title: Distinct patterns of gene expression during regeneration and asexual reproduction in the annelid Pristina leidyi
results.author: Irene del Olmo
results.journal: Journal of Experimental Zoology. Part B, Molecular and Developmental Evolution
results.takeaway: The molecular mechanisms underlying anterior regeneration and asexual reproduction in annelids are conserved across metazoans, with 291 genes upregulated during regeneration and 130 genes upregulated during asexual reproduction.

results.title: Regeneration in Annelids: Cell Sources, Tissue Remodeling, and Differential Gene Expression
results.author: D. D. Nikanorova
results.takeaway: Annelids can regenerate various body parts and structures, with diverse cellular sources and mechanisms, potentially shedding light on the evolutionarily conserved programs for maintaining regeneration ability.

results.title: Large scale changes in the transcriptome of Eisenia fetida during regeneration
results.author: Aksheev Bhambri
results.takeaway: Novel ncRNAs play a critical role in Earthworm regeneration, with 315 novel transcripts identified during injury, suggesting novel ncRNAs play a critical role in Earthworm regeneration.

results.title: Comparative transcriptomics in Syllidae (Annelida) indicates that posterior regeneration and regular growth are comparable, while anterior regeneration is a distinct process
results.author: R. P. Ribeiro
results.takeaway: Syllids show high posterior regeneration abilities, but anterior regeneration varies across species, with gene expression changes in both processes.

results.title: The people behind the papers - Alicia Boyd and Elaine Seaver.
results.takeaway: Capitella teleta, an annelid worm, gains regenerative ability with age, suggesting metamorphosis serves as a switch in annelid regenerative potential.

118.state: Anterior-versus-posterior identity in annelid regeneration is mechanistically mapped: Wnt/beta-catenin, with Activin and homeobox genes, sets posterior identity and re-establishes regional identity, paralleling planarians. The leech's two-ended regeneration is an early polarity observation now explained.

facets.q: Can environmental conditions such as the gastropod shell induce body asymmetry in hermit crabs (developmental plasticity)?
How does shell occupation shape abdominal morphology in hermit crabs?

results.title: Environmentally induced subsymmetry in the hermit crab, Calcinus dapsiles Morgan (Decapoda, Diogenidae).
results.author: G. J. Morgan
results.takeaway: The hermit crab Calcinus dapsiles Morgan exhibits more symmetrical body shape and uropods than usual when living in a non-coiled shelter, suggesting environmental factors may influence their symmetry.

results.title: Genes for asymmetry easily overruled
results.author: A. Harvey
results.takeaway: Hermit crab Clibanarius vittatus's asymmetry can be easily overridden by environmental factors, potentially aiding in the evolution of symmetrical king crabs and coconut crabs from hermit crab ancestors.

results.title: Asymmetrical chela growth patterns and correlation of chela shape to inhabited gastropod shells in Pagurus bernhardus (Linnaeus, 1758) (Decapoda, Anomura, Paguridae)
results.author: Yannic C. Ege
results.takeaway: Pagurus bernhardus hermit crabs exhibit sexually dimorphic growth patterns, with males showing larger right chelae and females showing smaller left chelae, suggesting sexual selection for display and agonistic behavior.

results.title: Asymmetrical morphology and growth of the hermit crab Pagurus filholi (Decapoda, Anomura, Paguridae) reared in non-dextral shell conditions
results.author: M. Imafuku
results.takeaway: Hermit crab Pagurus filholi shows asymmetrical morphology when reared in non-dextral shell conditions, with the right uropod becoming extended and the right major cheliped becoming enlarged.

results.title: CENTRAL AND PERIPHERAL ORIGINS OF ASYMMETRY IN THE ABDOMINAL MOTOR SYSTEM OF THE HERMIT CRAB *
results.author: W. Chapple
results.journal: Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs' abdominal nervous system shows asymmetries in different directions, highlighting the complexity of laterality and the adaptation of their body to their environment.

results.title: Hermit crabs perceive the extent of their virtual bodies
results.author: Kohei Sonoda
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs can modify walking behavior based on the shape of their shell, indicating an awareness of environmental obstacles and a flexible body image.

results.title: The Behavioral Ecology of Hermit Crabs
results.author: B. Hazlett
results.journal: Annual Review of Ecology, Evolution, and Systematics
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs' strong association with their adopted shelters has greatly influenced their biology, with most species adapting to inhabiting gastropod shells and exhibiting behavioral changes as they become benthic, shell-seeking crabs.

results.title: Choosing a bigger opponent: the path to victory and greater gains for the small contestant
results.author: Luis M. Burciaga
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs choose opponents larger than themselves for contests over gastropod shells, with fighting success higher when they choose opponents with better shell species or fit.

results.title: The molding hypothesis: linking shell use with hermit crab growth, morphology, and shell-species selection
results.author: A. Turra
results.takeaway: Hermit crab growth and morphology are influenced by shell use, with males reaching larger sizes and intermolt periods than females, and shell-species selection influenced by past experience and shell type.

results.title: Adaptive divergence in response to shared environmental pressures: examining gastropod shell exploitation by intertidal hermit crabs.
results.author: G. Alcaraz
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs adapt to snail shell shape based on the intertidal zone they inhabit, with shell shape similarities and contrasts depending on the species and pressures faced.

results.title: THE EFFECTS OF SHELL SIZE AND SHAPE ON GROWTH AND FORM IN THE HERMIT CRAB PAGURUS LONGICARPUS
results.author: N. Blackstone
results.takeaway: Shell shape and size significantly influence the growth and form of Pagurus longicarpus hermit crabs, potentially explaining geographic variation in their appearance.

results.title: Pattern and Plasticity in Tropical Hermit Crab Growth and Reproduction
results.author: M. Bertness
results.takeaway: Tropical hermit crab species trade-off growth and reproduction based on their supply of shells, allowing them to adapt to resource limitations and competition.

results.title: Early-life environment shapes claw bilateral asymmetry in the European lobster (Homarus gammarus)
results.author: Lorenzo Latini
results.takeaway: Exposure to substrate alone promotes claw asymmetry in juvenile European lobsters, with the 6th developmental stage being the critical period for claw differentiation.

results.title: Performance Implications of Divergent Shell Size Preferences and Exoskeleton Mass of Two Closely Related Hermit Crabs
results.author: Chloe B. MacLean
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs with larger shells have better protection but reduced motility and energetic cost, while those with smaller shells have greater mobility but increased vulnerability to stressors.

results.title: Hermit crabs, shells, and sentience
results.author: R. W. Elwood
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs exhibit complex perceptual and cognitive abilities in locating, selecting, and changing shells, suggesting potential sentience and awareness.

results.title: Mechanism of a plastic phenotypic response: predator‐induced shell thickening in the intertidal gastropod Littorina obtusata
results.author: J. Brookes
results.takeaway: Predation-induced shell thickening in intertidal snail Littorina obtusata is an active physiological response, involving energetic and developmental costs, potentially enhancing its evolutionary potential.

results.title: The colour of combat: How subtleties of resource value alter the effects of fighting ability in hermit crab contests
results.author: Thomas M. Chatfield
results.takeaway: Hermit crab contests over empty shells are more intense and aggressive when both shells are of high contrast, influenced by resource value and potential predation risk.

results.title: The physical basis of mollusk shell chiral coiling
results.author: R. Chirat
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: The development of chiral shells in mollusks is influenced by the interaction between the soft body and hard shell, with mechanical forces balanced by body twists.

results.title: The plastic homes of hermit crabs in the Anthropocene.
results.author: Zuzanna A. Jagiello
results.journal: The Science of the total environment
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs are increasingly using artificial shells made from plastic, mainly caps, due to sexual signaling, lightness, odor cues, and camouflage in polluted environments.

results.title: Asymmetric effects of contaminant exposure during asymmetric contests in the hermit crab Pagurus bernhardus
results.author: S. J. White
results.takeaway: Copper exposure in hermit crabs reduces the number of fights initiated and the likelihood of evicting defenders, but has no effect on defenders' chances of resisting eviction.

results.title: Strawberry hermit crab (Coenobita perlatus, H. Milne Edwards, 1837) gastropod shell utilization pattern according to the type and size
results.author: Y. Jeremy
results.takeaway: Strawberry hermit crabs (Coenobita perlatus) consistently select shells based on their architecture and size, affecting their fitness, but often overlook slight differences in size due to availability issues.

results.title: Shell occupation by the hermit crab Dardanus insignis (Decapoda, Diogenidae) from the north Coast of São Paulo state, Brazil.
results.author: I. F. Frameschi
results.journal: Brazilian journal of biology = Revista brasleira de biologia
results.takeaway: The hermit crab Dardanus insignis primarily occupies adequate shells, with Olivancillaria urceus being the most commonly used shell, and shell availability influencing occupation patterns for smaller individuals.

results.title: Social conquest of land: Sea-to-land changes in shell architecture and body morphology, with consequences for social evolution.
results.author: M. E. Laidre
results.takeaway: Changes in shell architecture and body morphology in land hermit crabs have led to increased social dependence and easier eviction, potentially impacting social evolution on land.

results.title: Associations between Crab Morphology and Gastropod Shell Species in the Hermit Crab Pagurus Longicarpus
results.author: T. Wilber
results.takeaway: Crab morphology and shell species occupancy change ontogenetically, suggesting a relationship between shell species and cheliped shape in Pagurus longicarpus.

results.title: The Behavioral Mechanisms Underlying Shell Selection By Hermit Crabs1)
results.author: E. S. Reese
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs select shells based on their properties, primarily using visual orientation and tactile and proprioceptor-based sensory input.

results.title: Comparative analysis of shell occupation by two southern populations of the hermit crab Loxopagurus loxochelis (Decapoda, Diogenidae)
results.author: Luciane Ayres-Peres
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs' shell choice is influenced by factors such as morphometry, shell availability, environmental conditions, and competition, leading to a diverse shell occupancy pattern in southern Brazil.

results.title: Morphometric traits as drivers for module structures and species specialization: a study about the hermit crab–gastropod networks from three different regions on the Brazilian coast
results.author: A. D. Silva
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs with different sizes need different types of shells, influencing their modularity and species specialization in their interaction with gastropod shells.

results.title: Role of the abdomen in the regulation of shell position in the hermit crabPagurus pollicarus
results.author: W. Chapple
results.takeaway: The abdomen plays a key role in maintaining shell position in Pagurus pollicarus hermit crabs, with its role being one-tenth of total lift and influenced by the fourth and fifth pereiopods.

results.title: Shell use by sympatric hermit crab species in a seasonally open coastal lagoon in Northeastern Brazil
results.author: V. Vale
results.takeaway: Hermit crab species in a Brazilian coastal lagoon predominantly use Neritina virginea shells, with shell weight being the most important determinant of shell occupancy, highlighting the need for conservation of these species.

results.title: Study on Shell Utilization Pattern of Two Sympatric Hermit Crab Species on the Rocky Intertidal Region of Veraval, Gujarat, India
results.author: K. Patel
results.journal: Thalassas: An International Journal of Marine Sciences
results.takeaway: Shell occupation patterns of C. rhabdodactylus and C. ransoni hermit crabs are highly influenced by the diversity, morphology, and availability of gastropod shells in the study area.

results.title: Shell occupation by the South Atlantic endemic hermit crab Loxopagurus loxochelis (Moreira, 1901) (Anomura: Diogenidae)
results.author: I. F. Frameschi
results.takeaway: The South Atlantic endemic hermit crab Loxopagurus loxochelis primarily occupies Olivancillaria urceus shells in Brazil's southwestern coast, suggesting stable environments for population perpetuation and new individuals.

results.title: Postural control of shell position by the abdomen of the hermit crab, Pagurus pollicarus. III. Analysis of movements and calculations of forces exerted by the muscles
results.author: W. Chapple
results.takeaway: Hermit crabs maintain shell position by bending their abdomens, with the columella in the crook of its bent abdomen, and the muscles exerting forces and bending moments consistent with behavioral measurements.

104.state: Shell-induced body asymmetry in hermit crabs is confirmed developmental plasticity: crabs reared in symmetrical or non-dextral shells develop more symmetrical or reversed abdomens, and the asymmetry is readily overridden by environment. The de-shelling result is an early demonstration, occasionally re-cited.

facets.q: Can crustaceans regenerate specialized mouthpart appendages such as the maxilliped?
What governs serial homology and identity in regenerated crustacean appendages?

results.title: Autotomy and Regeneration of Appendages in Crustaceans: A Review
results.author: Lei Liu
results.journal: Journal of Ocean University of China
results.takeaway: Autotomy in crustaceans is a self-protection mechanism that can affect foraging efficiency, growth, and reproduction, with chelicerae removal reducing cannibalism in commercial production.

results.title: Functional Study of the BMP Signaling Pathway in Appendage Regeneration of Exopalaemon carinicauda
results.author: Chaofan Xing
results.takeaway: The BMP signaling pathway plays a critical role in the early stages of appendage regeneration in Exopalaemon carinicauda, affecting wound healing and blastema regeneration time.

results.title: Crustacean appendage evolution associated with changes in Hox gene expression
results.author: M. Averof
results.takeaway: Changes in Hox gene expression in crustaceans correlate with the modification of anterior thoracic limbs into feeding appendages, suggesting homeotic changes play a role in adaptive evolutionary change.

results.title: CRISPR/Cas9 Mutagenesis Reveals Versatile Roles of Hox Genes in Crustacean Limb Specification and Evolution.
results.author: Arnaud Martin
results.takeaway: Hox genes play diverse roles in crustacean limb specification and evolution, with abdominal-A and Abdominal-B essential for proper posterior patterning and Ubx affecting gill development.

results.title: Probing the evolution of appendage specialization by Hox gene misexpression in an emerging model crustacean
results.author: A. Pavlopoulos
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Hox gene misexpression in crustaceans can lead to gradual evolutionary transitions from thoracic legs to maxillipeds, revealing the role of Hox genes in morphological and functional transformations.

results.title: Novel regulation of the homeotic gene Scr associated with a crustacean leg-to-maxilliped appendage transformation.
results.author: Arkhat Abzhanov
results.takeaway: The homeotic gene Scr plays a novel role in the transformation of crustacean leg-to-maxilliped appendages, suggesting a novel regulatory mechanism for this process in this group of arthropods.

results.title: Knockdown of Parhyale Ultrabithorax recapitulates evolutionary changes in crustacean appendage morphology
results.author: Danielle M. Liubicich
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Reducing Ultrabithorax expression in Parhyale hawaiensis crustaceans leads to additional maxillipeds, supporting the idea that changes in Ubx expression contribute to crustacean appendage diversity.

results.title: Morphological and Molecular Changes during Limb Regeneration of the Exopalaemon carinicauda
results.author: Chaofan Xing
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: Limb regeneration in the Exopalaemon carinicauda crustacean occurs rapidly and involves a complex network of cells and genes, contributing to our understanding of regeneration mechanisms in crustaceans.

results.title: Wound healing and regeneration in the crab Paratelphusa hydrodromous.
results.author: R. G. Adiyodi
results.takeaway: Crab Paratelphusa hydrodromous can amputate appendages and regenerate them, with regeneration linked to molting and reproduction, governed by the same controlling mechanisms.

results.title: Naturally occurring abnormalities (Bruchdreifachbildungen) in the chelae of three species of Crustacea (Decapoda) and a possible explanation.
results.author: P. M. Shelton
results.journal: Journal of embryology and experimental morphology
results.takeaway: Crustacean appendage abnormalities result from failure of wounds to heal, resulting in a range of structures similar to those observed in insect legs.

results.title: Embryonic expression patterns of the Hox genes of the crayfish Procambarus clarkii (Crustacea, Decapoda)
results.author: A. Abzhanov
results.takeaway: Trunk Hox genes in crayfish Procambarus clarkii play an essential role in the development of the complex decapod bodyplan, with more complex expression patterns compared to woodlouse Porcellio scaber.

results.title: Appendage Regeneration in Vertebrates: What Makes This Possible?
results.author: Valentina Daponte
results.takeaway: Understanding the similarities and differences in appendage regeneration in lizards, salamanders, and zebrafish can guide effective regenerative strategies in mammals.

results.title: Crustacean Limb Morphogenesis during Normal Development and Regeneration
results.author: A. Pavlopoulos
results.takeaway: Crustacean models with improved genomic and transcriptomic resources reveal the molecular and cellular basis of embryonic limb development and postembryonic limb regeneration, shedding light on appendage specialization mechanisms across arthropods and bilaterians.

results.title: Crustaceans.
results.author: Annalisa M. VanHook
results.takeaway: Crustaceans exhibit diverse limb adaptations for various functions, with mantis shrimps having highly specialized raptorial appendages capable of striking prey with incredible force.

results.title: Regenerated claws of the virile crayfish Faxonius virilis (Hagen, 1870) (Decapoda: Astacidea: Cambaridae) generate weaker pinching forces compared to original claws
results.author: Z. Graham
results.takeaway: Regenerated claws in virile crayfish Faxonius virilis generate weaker pinching forces (36% and 40% weaker in males and females) and have less muscle mass compared to original claws, potentially causing dishonest claws in crayfish.

results.title: Parhyale hawaiensis, Crustacea
results.author: John Rallis
results.journal: Handbook of Marine Model Organisms in Experimental Biology
results.takeaway: Parhyale hawaiensis has emerged as a valuable model organism for comparative developmental, genetic, and genomic research, contributing to understanding molecular mechanisms in body patterning and appendage diversification during arthropod development and evolution.

results.title: Unexpected UBX expression in the maxilliped of the mystacocarid crustacean Derocheilocharis remanei—evidence for a different way of making a maxilliped?
results.author: M. Fritsch
results.takeaway: The mystacocarid crustacean Derocheilocaris remanei shows unexpected UBX/ABD-A expression in the developing maxilliped, suggesting a shift in Hox gene function in arthropods.

results.title: Crustacean leg regeneration restores complex microanatomy and cell diversity
results.author: Alba Almazán
results.takeaway: Crustacean leg regeneration accurately replicates complex microanatomy and restores sensory function, with regenerated and uninjured legs having indistinguible cell-type composition and transcriptional profiles.

results.title: Burgess Shale fossils illustrate the origin of the mandibulate body plan
results.author: Cédric Aria
results.takeaway: Burgess Shale fossils reveal the origin of the mandibulate body plan, suggesting distinct ontogenetic niches among stem arthropods during the Cambrian period.

results.title: Deep evolutionary origin of limb and fin regeneration
results.author: Sylvain Darnet
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: A common origin of paired appendage regeneration exists among Osteichthyes, with a shared genetic program involving limb and fin regeneration in various species.

results.title: The Daphnia carapace and other novel structures evolved via the cryptic persistence of serial homologs.
results.author: Heather S. Bruce
results.takeaway: The Daphnia carapace evolved from a shared, ancestral tissue that persists in a cryptic state in other arthropod lineages, suggesting cryptic persistence of unrecognized serial homologs as a general solution for the origin of novel structures.

results.title: Homeotic transformation in a terrestrial isopod: insights into the appendage identity in crustaceans
results.author: Naoto Inui
results.takeaway: Homeotic transformation of a pleopod in the terrestrial isopod Porcellio scaber may cause a malformed appendage with three-segmented pereopod-like structures.

results.title: Genetic and developmental bases of serial homology in vertebrate limb evolution.
results.author: Ilya Ruvinsky
results.takeaway: This study proposes models for the evolution of paired appendages in vertebrates, emphasizing the importance of considering interactions among multiple genes from different gene families for understanding complex developmental systems.

results.title: A chromatin code for limb segment identity in axolotl limb regeneration.
results.author: Akane Kawaguchi
results.takeaway: Axolotl limb connective tissue cells use segment-specific levels of histone H3K27me3 as an intrinsic segment information code, supporting an ancient, conserved regeneration program.
