results.title: Deconstructing the long‐standing a priori assumption that serial homology generally involves ancestral similarity followed by anatomical divergence
results.author: Natalia Siomava
results.takeaway: Serial homology may not always involve ancestral similarity and may display trends towards less similarity or more similarity in evolution, challenging the assumption that serial homology always involves anatomical divergence.

results.title: The hierarchical basis of serial homology and evolutionary novelty
results.author: J. DiFrisco
results.takeaway: Serial homology can be applied to repeated, individualized body parts, allowing a more fine-grained mapping of diverse trait-types and explaining why everything is not homologous with everything else in evolution.

results.title: Keeping at arm’s length during regeneration
results.author: Valerie A. Tornini
results.takeaway: Adult amphibians and fish can regenerate lost appendages, demonstrating remarkable developmental patterning, by maintaining positional identity and determining tissue completion.

results.title: Identification of Hox genes and their expression profiles during embryonic development of the emerging model organism, Macrobrachium olfersii.
results.author: M. Jaramillo
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part B, Molecular and developmental evolution
results.takeaway: Hox genes play a role in the development and evolution of body segments and appendages in crustaceans, with some isoforms previously unreported.

results.title: Evolution of Serial Patterns in the Vertebrate Pharyngeal Apparatus and Paired Appendages via Assimilation of Dissimilar Units
results.author: Tetsuto Miyashita
results.takeaway: Serial similarity in vertebrate pharyngeal apparatus and paired appendages is a derived state, acquired through the secondarily assimilation of distinctly patterned structures.

results.title: Genetic reprogramming of positional memory in a regenerating appendage
results.author: Ying-Ting Wang
results.takeaway: Positional information in regenerating tissues is malleable, with zebrafish tailfins retaining memory of their new size when disrupted during a 48-hour window of regeneration.

results.title: The Papilla Stage as a Critical Molecular Transition: Antp and Sex-Regulatory Network Orchestrate Cheliped Regeneration in Eriocheir sinensis
results.author: Benzhen Li
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI

results.title: Transitions in functional morphology from “large branchiopods” to Cladocera: Video and confocal microscopic studies of Cyclestheria hislopi (Cyclestherida) and Sida crystallina (Cladocera: Ctenopoda)
results.author: Zandra M. S. Sigvardt
results.takeaway: Cladocerans show a unique morphology and function, with a specialized swimming and feeding system that separates the anterior locomotory system and posterior feeding system, potentially explaining their omnipresence in various habitats.

results.title: Formation of body appendages during caudal regeneration in Platynereis dumerilii: adaptation of conserved molecular toolsets
results.author: Jan Grimmel
results.takeaway: Platynereis dumerilii's caudal regeneration shows conserved roles of genes involved in appendage formation, but the underlying mechanisms may not be identical across different clades.

results.title: Common themes in tetrapod appendage regeneration: a cellular perspective
results.author: Bess M. Miller
results.takeaway: Understanding commonalities between regenerative modes across diverse species can help integrate past observations and modern understanding of complex tissue regeneration.

results.title: Cellular diversity of the regenerating caudal fin
results.author: Yiran Hou
results.takeaway: Zebrafish regenerate their caudal fins after amputation, involving epithelial, mesenchymal, and hematopoietic cells, with molecular signatures aiding in cell type identification.

45.state: Serial homology and identity of regenerated crustacean appendages is active evo-devo: Hox genes (Ubx, abd-A, Abd-B) specify and transform thoracic legs into feeding maxillipeds, and BMP signalling drives early appendage regeneration. The maxilliped-regeneration result fits directly.

facets.q: Does the total amount of melanin change during physiological colour change in fish, beyond pigment redistribution?
How is melanin synthesis regulated during background colour adaptation in fishes?

results.title: Background matching through fast and reversible melanin-based pigmentation plasticity in tadpoles comes with morphological and antioxidant changes
results.author: H. C. Liedtke
results.takeaway: Tadpoles can rapidly and reversibly adjust their pigmentation to match environmental changes, leading to larger tail fins and improved redox status.

results.title: A new insight into the pigmentation of the three-spined stickleback exposed to oxidative stress: day and night study
results.author: E. Sokołowska
results.takeaway: Fish skin pigmentation can be used to assess their welfare and detect environmental toxic agents, but only in dorsal skin during the day.

results.title: Fish pigmentation and coloration: Molecular mechanisms and aquaculture perspectives
results.author: Mingkun Luo
results.takeaway: This review highlights the importance of understanding cellular and molecular mechanisms in fish coloration, which can be used to enhance body color in aquaculture through single-sex control and molecular marker-assisted breeding.

results.title: Pigmentation and colour changing mechanism in fishes
results.author: Mintumoni Pegu
results.takeaway: Fish pigmentation is influenced by genetic, morphological, physiological, and environmental factors, with genes and nutritional contents playing crucial roles in pigment production and color change.

results.title: Evidence for the participation of a melanin-concentrating hormone in physiological colour change in the eel.
results.author: I. Gilham
results.takeaway: The control of physiological color change in eels is largely hormonal, involving the antagonistic effects of alpha-MSH and a melanin-concentrating agent, likely MCH.

results.title: Effects of background adaptation on the skin color of Malaysian red tilapia
results.author: Lan-mei Wang
results.takeaway: Malaysian red tilapia's skin color is more affected by background color than body color, with white background resulting in paler skin and black background resulting in darker skin.

results.title: Thyroid hormone regulates both melanin and non-melanin pigmentation in Sinibrama taeniatus via three types of chromophores
results.author: Xiaoqin Liu
results.takeaway: Thyroid hormone regulates fish body color through both melanin-based and non-melanin-based mechanisms, providing valuable information for cultivating desirable body colors in aquaculture.

results.title: Interrelation between melanocyte-stimulating hormone and melanin-concentrating hormone in physiological body color change: roles emerging from barfin flounder Verasper moseri.
results.author: K. Mizusawa
results.takeaway: Barfin flounder Verasper moseri show that melanin-concentrating hormone plays a more significant role in regulating pigment migration in the skin than melanocyte-stimulating hormone, with MSH showing less response to color changes.

results.title: Cell Junction and Vesicle Trafficking-Mediated Melanosome/Melanin Transfer Are Involved in the Dynamic Transformation of Goldfish Carassius auratus Skin Color
results.author: Lili Liu
results.takeaway: Cell junction and vesicle trafficking play key roles in melanosome/melanin transfer, which leads to the color transformation of cyan goldfish.

results.title: Endocrine Regulation of Pigmentation in Fish
results.author: F. Abbott
results.takeaway: Fish color change is influenced by both nervous and hormonal factors, with pigment dispersion favoring dark morphological color and pigment aggregation favoring light morphological color.

results.title: Triphenyltin induced darker body coloration by disrupting melanocortin system and pteridine metabolic pathway in a reef fish, Amphiprion ocellaris.
results.author: Song Liu
results.takeaway: Triphenyltin exposure disrupts the melanocortin system and pteridine metabolic pathway, leading to darker body coloration in Amphiprion ocellaris reef fish.

results.title: Melanin‐concentrating hormone like and somatolactin. A teleost‐specific hypothalamic‐hypophyseal axis system linking physiological and morphological pigmentation
results.author: G. Bertolesi
results.takeaway: In teleosts, MCHL and somatolactin link physiological and morphological pigmentation, providing a previously unknown link between these two responses.

results.title: Expression of genes for melanotropic peptides and their receptors for morphological color change in goldfish Carassius auratus.
results.author: K. Mizusawa
results.takeaway: Goldfish's morphological color changes are influenced by melanotropic peptides and their receptors, with mc5r playing a major role in reinforcing the function of MSH in morphological color changes.

results.title: Taisho-Sanshoku koi have hardly faded skin and show attenuated melanophore sensitivity to adrenaline and melanin-concentrating hormone
results.author: Yukari Shinohara
results.takeaway: Taisho-Sanshoku koi show weak sensitivity of scale melanophores to melanin-concentrating hormone and adrenaline, potentially explaining their lack of skin color change in response to background color.

results.title: Regulation of pigmentation in zebrafish melanophores.
results.author: D. Logan
results.takeaway: Zebrafish serve as an excellent model for studying physiological color change and the molecular basis of pigment translocation, with cAMP and intact microtubules playing key roles.

results.title: Untangling the structural and molecular mechanisms underlying colour and rapid colour change in a lizard, Agama atra
results.author: M. Nicolaï
results.takeaway: Melanin, iridophores, and pteridines are the main color-producing mechanisms in Agama atra lizards, with rapid color change achieved through melanin dispersal and iridophore organization.

results.title: Exploring the mechanisms and impacts of melatonin on fish colouration
results.author: Md. Mubashshir
results.takeaway: Melatonin plays a diverse role in fish coloration, leading to pigment aggregation and dispersion, and can serve as in vitro test models for pharmacological applications.

results.title: Biochemical regulation of pigment motility in vertebrate chromatophores: a review of physiological color change mechanisms
results.author: Russell A. Ligon
results.takeaway: Different hormones, neurotransmitters, and signals direct pigment reorganization in vertebrate chromatophores, enabling physiological color change in various species.

results.title: Melatonin modulates the hypothalamic-pituitary neuroendocrine axis to regulate physiological color change in teleost fish
results.author: Jiaqian Feng
results.takeaway: Melatonin regulates physiological color change in large yellow croaker fish by modulating the hypothalamic-pituitary neuroendocrine axis through multiple intracellular signaling pathways.

results.title: Rapid color change in fish and amphibians – function, regulation, and emerging applications
results.author: Helen Nilsson Sköld
results.takeaway: Fish and amphibians exhibit rapid color change for background matching, thermoregulation, and signaling, with external factors playing a key role in regulating this process.

results.title: Transcriptome Analysis Reveals the Complex Regulatory Pathway of Background Color in Juvenile Plectropomus leopardus Skin Color Variation
results.author: F. Song
results.takeaway: The molecular mechanism underlying the darkened skin color pattern in juvenile Plectropomus leopardus fish is linked to the background color, with key genes involved in melanogenesis being upregulated.

results.title: The Effect of Background Color on Skin Color Variation of Juvenile Plectropomus leopardus
results.author: Feibiao Song
results.journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
results.takeaway: A white background is more conducive to maintaining red skin color in juvenile Plectropomus leopardus, while black and red backgrounds cause darkening in response to other colors.

results.title: Genetic deciphering of the antagonistic activities of the melanin-concentrating hormone and melanocortin pathways in skin pigmentation
results.author: Romain Madelaine
results.takeaway: The antagonistic activity between melanin-concentrating hormone and melanocortin pathways in skin pigmentation may contribute to human skin disorders and cancers.

results.title: Molecular delineation of a retina-dependent photoneuroendocrine pathway in zebrafish.
results.author: Krasimir Slanchev
results.takeaway: The retina-dependent photoneuroendocrine pathway in zebrafish allows for dynamic adjustment of body color to the perceived brightness of the ambient background, resulting in a pale or dark appearance.

results.title: Sws2 Gene Positively Regulates Melanin Production in Plectropomus leopardus Skin via Direct Regulation of the Synthesis of Retinoic Acid
results.author: Haoran Yu
results.takeaway: The SWS2 gene positively regulates melanin production in Plectropomus leopardus fish skin by affecting vitamin synthesis and melanin-related gene expression levels.

results.title: ERK1/2 regulates melanin synthesis in fish: a case study on a colourful variety, leopard coral grouper (Plectropomus leopardus)
results.author: Min Yang
results.takeaway: ERK1/2 plays a role in regulating melanogenesis in leopard coral grouper (Plectropomus leopardus) by interacting with MITF, leading to variable skin coloration in this coral reef fish.

results.title: Effects of background color and feeding status on the expression of genes associated with body color regulation in the goldfish Carassius auratus.
results.author: Tingshu Yang
results.takeaway: Goldfish body color is influenced by background color and feeding status, with somatolactin (SL) and prolactin (PRL) potentially playing limited roles in regulating body color.

results.title: Plasticity for colour adaptation in vertebrates explained by the evolution of the genes pomc, pmch and pmchl
results.author: G. Bertolesi
results.takeaway: The evolution of pomc, pmch, and pmchl genes in vertebrates explains different mechanisms for background adaptation in skin color, enabling different camouflage strategies.

results.title: STUDY OF LOSE AND GAIN OF MELANOPHORES (MORPHOLOGICAL STUDY) AFTER PROLONGED ADAPTATION IN BLACK AND WHITE BACKGROUND IN FRESH WATER FISH PUNTIUS
results.author: Agrawal Sita
results.takeaway: Fish Puntius can camouflage themselves by changing their skin color to match their environment, with increased melanophores in black backgrounds and decreased melanophores in white backgrounds.

results.title: Effects of Water Temperature on the Body Color and Expression of the Genes Related to Body Color Regulation in the Goldfish
results.author: Tingshu Yang
results.takeaway: Higher water temperatures in goldfish lead to paler body color and increased expression of genes related to body color regulation, with darker background colors potentially stabilizing these functions.

results.title: Response of body color change rearing under different light intensity conditions in farmed red spotted grouper, Epinephelus akaara
results.author: Song-Hee Choi
results.takeaway: POMC mRNA and MC1R mRNA in red spotted grouper are involved in melanin synthesis and are controlled by light intensity, affecting body color changes during development.

results.title: Functions of thyroid hormone signaling in regulating melanophore, iridophore, erythrophore, and pigment pattern formation in spotted scat (Scatophagus argus)
results.author: Yongguan Liao
results.takeaway: Thyroid hormone signaling plays a critical role in regulating pigment cell development and body color patterns in spotted scat fish, with different mechanisms affecting different pigment cells.

results.title: Involvement of melanin-concentrating hormone 2 in background color adaptation of barfin flounder Verasper moseri.
results.author: K. Mizusawa
results.takeaway: In barfin flounder Verasper moseri, MCH1 and MCH2 play a role in responding to changes in background colors during chromatophore control.

22.state: Regulation of melanin during colour change is modern melanophore biology: fish and tadpoles rapidly and reversibly adjust pigmentation under hormonal control (alpha-MSH/MCH), through both redistribution and synthesis. The point that the amount of melanin itself changes is incorporated.

facets.q: Do muscle contractile proteins adapt their thermal stability to an animal's body temperature?
Is the thermal stability of muscle proteins fixed or thermally acclimated across species?

results.title: Thermal Stability of Contractile Proteins in Bat Wing Muscles Explains Differences in Temperature Dependence of Whole-Muscle Shortening Velocity
results.author: A. Rummel
results.takeaway: Bat wing muscles' contractile properties are influenced by the thermal stability of myofibrillar ATPase, with forearm muscles having less thermal stability than pectoralis muscles, but no significant difference in acute temperature dependence of enzyme activity.

results.title: Adaptation of fish to different environmental temperature by qualitative and quantitative changes in gene expression
results.author: G. Goldspink
results.takeaway: Fish adapt to different environmental temperatures by changing gene expression, leading to increased force and power in muscles acclimated to low temperatures.

results.title: Temperature adaptation by the myotomal muscle of fish
results.author: R. Penney
results.takeaway: Fish can adapt their contractile proteins to suit a specific temperature, but trout may not have the ability to do so due to their relatively unchanging temperature environment.

results.title: Thermal conditions experienced during differentiation affect metabolic and contractile phenotypes of mouse myotubes.
results.author: A. Little
results.journal: American journal of physiology. Regulatory, integrative and comparative physiology
results.takeaway: Developing muscle can compensate for cold exposure by increasing metabolic rates, ATP production, and glycolytic flux, complementing central thermoregulation and allowing animals to maintain function in cold environments.

results.title: Muscle and Metabolic Genes Are Differentially Expressed During Thermal Acclimation by the Brook Trout Myotome.
results.author: D. Coughlin
results.journal: Journal of experimental zoology. Part A, Ecological and integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Brook trout show a robust response to elevated temperature, with more than 10,000 genes showing differential expression with temperature, potentially aiding their survival in climate change-related challenges.

results.title: TEMPERATURE ADAPTATION IN FISH MUSCLE
results.author: R. Penney
results.takeaway: Fish muscle adapts to low temperatures by having high specific nyofibrillar ATPase activity, but some species can acclimate to a wide range of temperatures, with similar calcium transport composition and temperature dependence.

results.title: Switches in fish myosin genes induced by environment temperature in muscle of the carp.
results.author: G. Goldspink
results.journal: Symposia of the Society for Experimental Biology
results.takeaway: Carp can adapt to seasonal temperature changes by expressing different myosin heavy chain isoform genes, allowing them to swim at warm or cold temperatures.

results.title: Regulatory proteins and thermostability of myofibrillar ATPase in acclimated goldfish
results.author: R. Penney
results.takeaway: Goldfish adapt to different temperatures by altering their contractile proteins, with regulatory proteins playing a more involved role in muscle contraction than a simple on/off switch.

results.title: Effects of thermal acclimation on ventricle size, protein composition, and contractile properties of crucian carp heart
results.author: V. Pelouch
results.takeaway: Thermal acclimation to cold temperatures increases extracellular matrix protein concentration but reduces myocyte protein concentration in crucian carp hearts.

results.title: Biochemical responses to temperature in the contractile protein complex of striped bass Morone saxatilis.
results.author: T. Moerland
results.takeaway: Striped bass's red muscle, powered by ATPase, is more sensitive to cold temperatures than white muscle, potentially impairing their sustained swimming capacity at cold temperatures.

results.title: Physical and molecular bases of protein thermal stability and cold adaptation.
results.author: F. Pucci
results.takeaway: Protein thermal and cold stability and adaptation are complex and challenging, requiring further investigation for large-scale rational protein design.

results.title: Loss of α-actinin-3 during human evolution provides superior cold resilience and muscle heat generation
results.author: V. Wyckelsma
results.takeaway: Loss of -actinin-3 in humans leads to improved cold resilience and muscle heat generation during cold-water immersion due to changes in skeletal muscle thermogenesis.

results.title: Heat stability of fish muscle proteins
results.author: R. Poulter
results.journal: International Journal of Food Science and Technology
results.takeaway: Fish muscle proteins show varying heat stability, with more stable proteins found in fish found in higher ambient temperatures, while actins show similar thermal stability across all species.

results.title: Thermal acclimation in rainbow smelt, Osmerus mordax, leads to faster myotomal muscle contractile properties and improved swimming performance
results.author: J. Woytanowski
results.takeaway: Rainbow smelt can maintain activity at cold temperatures by adjusting muscle contractile properties and modifying muscle function to enable active locomotion in cold water.

results.title: Movements of vastly different performance have similar underlying muscle physiology
results.author: J. Olberding
results.takeaway: Elastic tongue projection in salamanders leads to higher performance and thermal robustness, while muscle contractile properties remain relatively unchanged.

results.title: Thermal denaturation of proteins in post rigor muscle tissue as studied by differential scanning calorimetry
results.author: E. Stabursvik
results.journal: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture
results.takeaway: Strict pH control is crucial for reproducible muscle thermograms, as myosin and actin denaturation peaks in post-rigor muscle tissue are pH-dependent and actin denaturation is salt-dependent.

results.title: Changing face of contractile activation in striated muscle at physiological temperature
results.author: Alf Månsson
results.takeaway: Calcium binding to troponin and myosin binding to actin play a crucial role in contractile regulation of striated muscle at physiological temperature (35-40°C).

results.title: Adaptations in Mg2+‐activated myofibrillar ATPase activity induced by temperature acclimation
results.author: W. Davison
results.takeaway: Goldfish with a wide thermal tolerance range show increased Mg2+-activated myofibrillar ATPase activity at low temperatures, suggesting a strong correlation between thermostability and environmental temperature.

results.title: Dissecting the key residues crucial for the species-specific thermostability of muscle-type creatine kinase.
results.author: Y. Gao
results.takeaway: Two residues near the dimer interface of muscle-type creatine kinase, the 46th and 146th residue, are crucial for species-specific thermal stability.

results.title: A comparison of thermal sensitivities of wing muscle contractile properties from a temperate and tropical bat species.
results.author: A. Rummel
results.takeaway: Temperature sensitivities in bat muscles are generally similar, but the pectoralis muscle in Eptesicus fuscus is less sensitive to temperature changes than in Carollia perspicillata, suggesting better function at low temperatures.

results.title: Meltome atlas—thermal proteome stability across the tree of life
results.author: A. Jarząb
results.takeaway: This study reveals that evolutionary conservation of protein complexes is reflected by similar thermal stability of their proteins, with protein sequence, composition, and size affecting thermal stability in prokaryotes and eukaryotes.

results.title: Adaptation of protein stability to thermally heterogeneous environments
results.author: Tadeas Priklopil
results.takeaway: Protein stability adaptation in complex thermal environments involves both mean temperature and thermal fluctuations, with local adaptation more likely for proteins with stability profiles limited to narrow temperature ranges.

results.title: Fish fast skeletal muscle tropomyosins show species-specific thermal stability.
results.author: Ming-Chih Huang
results.journal: Comparative biochemistry and physiology. Part B, Biochemistry & molecular biology
results.takeaway: Fish fast skeletal muscle tropomyosins show species-specific thermal stability, with only a few amino acid substitutions and post-translational modifications potentially affecting their stability.

results.title: Species dependence of fish myosin stability to heat and frozen storage
results.author: J. Davies
results.journal: International Journal of Food Science and Technology
results.takeaway: Fish myosin stability varies between habitat temperatures, with red snapper showing significant changes in whole muscle after frozen storage, while cod shows similar changes in whole muscle and isolated myosin.

results.title: Phylogenetic history and temperature adaptation contribute to structural and functional stability of proteins in marine mollusks
results.author: Xin-lei Zhang
results.takeaway: Phylogenetic history influences thermal adaptation of proteins in marine mollusks, and multi-level analysis of orthologous proteins should be considered for a comprehensive understanding of protein thermal adaptation.

results.title: Finding the generalized molecular principles of protein thermal stability
results.author: Suman Hait
results.takeaway: Five "nearly universal" signatures of thermal adaptation have been identified, and certain amino acid changes can potentially enhance protein thermal stability.

results.title: Effects of thermal acclimation on the proteome of the planarian Crenobia alpina from an alpine freshwater spring
results.author: J. Ebner
results.takeaway: The planarian Crenobia alpina retains plasticity in response to elevated temperatures, challenging the notion that stable environments result in muted plasticity.

results.title: Thermostability of tropomyosins from the fast skeletal muscles of tropical fish species
results.author: Ming-Chih Huang
results.takeaway: Milkfish tropomyosin has lower refolding ability after thermal denaturation compared to tilapia, grouper, and mudskipper, but their amino acid sequences are similar.

results.title: Influence of seasonal variations on the physicochemical properties and thermal stability of myofibrillar proteins in snakehead (Channa argus).
results.author: Han Chen
results.takeaway: Snakehead muscle proteins show seasonal variations in composition, physicochemical properties, and thermal stability, with summer snakehead being more thermally stable and exhibiting better protein quality.

results.title: Myosin thermal stability in fish muscle
results.author: J. Davies
results.journal: Journal of the Science of Food and Agriculture
results.takeaway: Cod myosin has a lower thermal melting transition than snapper myosin, but this difference may be due to differences in aggregation characteristics.

results.title: Sequence-Based Analysis of Thermal Adaptation and Protein Energy Landscapes in an Invasive Blue Mussel (Mytilus galloprovincialis)
results.author: N. Saarman
results.takeaway: The invasive blue mussel M. galloprovincialis shows adaptive increases in protein stability, contributing to its superior tolerance to heat stress compared to cold-tolerant M. trossulus.

results.title: Pervasive Divergence in Protein Thermostability is Mediated by Both Structural Changes and Cellular Environments
results.author: Nilima Walunjkar
results.takeaway: Both structural changes and cellular environments contribute to the divergence in protein thermostability between closely related species, with 85% of S. cerevisiae proteins being more stable than their S. uvarum homologs.

results.title: Thermal acclimation in fish: conservative and labile properties of swimming muscle
results.author: H. Guderley
results.takeaway: Fish swimming performance and metabolic systems show thermal compensation, with optimal performance and lowest thermal sensitivity within the range of temperatures frequently encountered by the organism.

results.title: Structural flexibility and protein adaptation to temperature: Molecular dynamics analysis of malate dehydrogenases of marine molluscs
results.author: Yun‐wei Dong
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
results.takeaway: Molecular dynamics simulations reveal that temperature adaptation in marine molluscs' malate dehydrogenases is influenced by structural flexibility, with cold-adapted species showing the greatest heat-induced changes in mobile regions.

results.title: Review: Protein function at thermal extremes: balancing stability and flexibility.
results.author: P. A. Fields
results.journal: Comparative biochemistry and physiology. Part A, Molecular & integrative physiology
results.takeaway: Protein function at thermal extremes is achieved through subtle redistributions of intramolecular interactions, rather than new amino acids or structural motifs, maintaining stability and flexibility.

102.state: Whether muscle proteins adapt their thermal stability to body temperature is live thermal-adaptation physiology: fish adjust contractile-protein expression and force with temperature (though some, like trout, may not), and myofibrillar ATPase thermal stability varies. The 'fixed coagulation point' is the question, now answered with nuance.

facets.q: Can birds regenerate beak or bill tissue, and is the avian beak a model for regeneration?
How far do warm-blooded vertebrates such as birds retain regenerative capacity?

results.title: Hallmarks of Regeneration
results.author: K. Poss
results.takeaway: Regeneration in various animal species involves activation of a cell source, initiation of regenerative programs, interplay with supporting cells, and control of tissue size and function.

results.title: Neurobiological basis of sensory perception: welfare implications of beak trimming.
results.author: W. Kuenzel
results.takeaway: Beak trimming in the poultry industry can prevent head, feather, and vent pecking, but should be conservative and limited to young birds to avoid neuromas and deformed beaks.

results.title: Cellular, Molecular, and Genetic Mechanisms of Avian Beak Development and Evolution
results.author: R. Schneider
results.takeaway: Avian beak development and evolution are influenced by cellular, molecular, and genetic mechanisms, with neural crest mesenchyme playing a key role in species-specific patterning and function.

results.title: Stem Cells and the Bird Cochlea-Where Is Everybody?
results.author: Amanda Janesick
results.journal: Cold Spring Harbor perspectives in medicine
results.takeaway: Avian cochlea regeneration relies on quiescent supporting cells, but the identity of these cells and their potential as stem cells remain unclear.

results.title: Repair and Regeneration.
results.author: B. Purnell
results.takeaway: This special issue highlights research on repair and regeneration mechanisms, with an eye towards therapeutic applications, to enhance the body's ability to repair and regenerate damaged tissues and organs.

results.title: Regeneration in Stentor coeruleus
results.author: W. Marshall
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: Single cells can regenerate missing structures, resembling animal development and regeneration processes, and may have evolved by exploiting pre-existing subcellular developmental strategies from unicellular ancestors.

results.title: Mammalian Models of Adult Tissue Regeneration.
results.author: Kuo Liao
results.takeaway: Mammalian species with adaptations for tissue regeneration can provide insights for future therapies and understanding of mammalian complexity.

results.title: Sexually dimorphic sail feathers in the Mandarin duck as a model for lifelong developmental modulation
results.author: Pin-Chi Tang
results.takeaway: Mandarin duck sail feathers show lifelong developmental modulation, integrating local morphogenetic programs, epigenetic regulation, and hormonal cues to create sexually dimorphic and seasonally dynamic feather morphogenesis.

results.title: Review: Biological and Molecular Differences between Tail Regeneration and Limb Scarring in Lizard An Inspiring Model Addressing Limb Regeneration in Amniotes
results.author: L. Alibardi
results.journal: Journal of Experimental Zoology Part B: Molecular and Developmental Evolution
results.takeaway: Lizards show a unique model for tissue regeneration and scarring in amniotes, with tail regeneration resulting from low inflammation and high cell proliferation, while limb scarring occurs due to intense inflammation and lack of FGFs.

results.title: Model systems for regeneration: Xenopus
results.author: Lauren S. Phipps
results.takeaway: Xenopus is a key model organism for regeneration research, as it can achieve scar-free healing and tissue regeneration during larval stages, offering insights into the mechanisms governing regeneration.

results.title: Tail Tales: What We Have Learned About Regeneration from Xenopus Laevis Tadpoles
results.author: Jessica Lara
results.takeaway: Tadpoles provide unique insights into regenerative biology, offering a model for understanding tissue repair constraints in higher organisms and shedding light on evolutionary loss of regenerative abilities.

results.title: Model systems for regeneration: zebrafish
results.author: Ines J. Marques
results.takeaway: Zebrafish are an accessible model organism for studying regeneration, providing insights into tissue recovery strategies for species with poor regenerative capacity.

results.title: Cellular studies of auditory hair cell regeneration in birds.
results.author: J. Stone
results.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
results.takeaway: Avian auditory hair cell regeneration has progressed significantly, with potential applications in understanding auditory perception in mammals.

results.title: Feather regeneration as a model for organogenesis
results.author: Sung-Jan Lin
results.takeaway: Feather regeneration serves as a unique model for understanding organogenesis, with its episodic regenerative process and response to injuries providing valuable insights into morphogenesis and stem cell activation.

results.title: Using Beak Manipulations and Their Effects on Performance, Injury Responses and Some Physiological Traits in Japanese Quails Under Upper Egypt Conditions.
results.author: A. Abuoghaba

results.title: Model systems for regeneration: salamanders
results.author: A. Joven
results.takeaway: Salamanders are key models for regeneration, demonstrating remarkable abilities to regrow tissues, organs, and even whole body parts, providing insights into cell, tissue, and organ regeneration mechanisms.

results.title: Hair cell regeneration, reinnervation, and restoration of hearing thresholds in the avian hearing organ
results.author: Mitsuo P. Sato
results.takeaway: Birds can regenerate auditory hair cells and recover from hearing loss, with auditory thresholds recovering after 4-5 weeks in sisomicin-deafened chickens.

results.title: Light and Electron Morphological Studies on the Upper Beak of the Little Owl (Athene Noctua)
results.author: R. A. Ali

results.title: Tissue repair and regeneration with endogenous stem cells
results.author: H. Xia
results.takeaway: Endogenous stem cells can play a key role in tissue repair and regeneration, with potential for improved applications using natural and synthetic biomaterials.

results.title: The Cells that Fill the Bill: Neural Crest and the Evolution of Craniofacial Development
results.author: Andrew H Jheon
results.takeaway: Avian embryos provide valuable insights into the development and evolution of the craniofacial skeleton, aiding in the development of novel clinical approaches to treat and prevent human craniofacial diseases.

results.title: Why Has the Ability to Regenerate Following CNS Injury Been Repeatedly Lost Over the Course of Evolution?
results.author: S. Blackshaw
results.takeaway: Evolutionarily, birds and mammals have lost the ability to regenerate after CNS injury, possibly due to selection for greater resistance to intra-CNS infections.

results.title: Neuronal regeneration: Vertebrates comparative overview and new perspectives for neurodegenerative diseases
results.author: Alessio Alesci
results.takeaway: Neuronal regeneration in various vertebrates can provide insights for developing new therapies for neurodegenerative disorders.

results.title: OUP accepted manuscript
results.takeaway: Regeneration in purple shore crabs restores fitness in feeding and locomotion, but may lead to tradeoffs in reproduction, potentially leading to the loss of regenerative abilities in derived lineages like mammals and birds.

results.title: Evolutionary constraints impose multiple checkpoints that block mammalian sensory hair cell regeneration
results.author: Xiaoyi Zhang
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology

results.title: Bioinformatics Screening of Genes Specific for Well-Regenerating Vertebrates Reveals c-answer, a Regulator of Brain Development and Regeneration
results.author: D. Korotkova
results.takeaway: C-answer, a gene essential for regeneration in cold-blooded animals, may contribute to the higher regenerative capacity of warm-blooded animals by regulating regeneration and brain development.

results.title: The Secreted Protein Disulfide Isomerase Ag1 Lost by Ancestors of Poorly Regenerating Vertebrates Is Required for Xenopus laevis Tail Regeneration
results.author: A. S. Ivanova
results.journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
results.takeaway: The loss of the secreted protein disulfide isomerase Ag1 in warm-blooded vertebrates may contribute to their poorer regenerative ability, as it is essential for frog tail regeneration.

results.title: Vertebrate cardiac regeneration: evolutionary and developmental perspectives
results.author: S. Cutie
results.takeaway: Cardiac regeneration in vertebrates has evolved and developed into a permanent trait, with factors like oxygen-rich environments, thermogenesis, immune systems, and cancer risk trade-offs contributing to this loss.

results.title: Evolution, comparative biology and ontogeny of vertebrate heart regeneration
results.author: Céline J. Vivien
results.takeaway: Cardiac regeneration in adult mammals is limited due to adaptations during aquatic to terrestrial life and birth, and correlates with low metabolic state, inability to regulate body temperature, low heart pressure, hypoxia, immature cardiomyocyte structure, and immature immune system.

results.title: Bioinformatic screening of genes present only in well regenerating vertebrates reveals novel FGF and purinergic signaling modulator - c-Answer
results.author: D. Korotkova
results.takeaway: C-Answer, a gene essential for regeneration in fishes, amphibians, and reptiles, may contribute to the higher regenerative capacity of these animals compared to birds and mammals.

results.title: Stem Cells in the Vertebrate Retina
results.author: T. Reh
results.takeaway: Birds possess an unexpected capacity for retinal regeneration, with a zone of cells at the retinal margin analogous to the ciliary marginal zone in cold-blooded vertebrates.

results.title: MAPK/ERK Pathway as a Central Regulator in Vertebrate Organ Regeneration
results.author: X. Wen
results.takeaway: The MAPK/ERK pathway plays a central role in vertebrate organ regeneration, underlying the differential regenerative capacities among species and offering potential applications in tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

results.title: Invited Letter. Organ regeneration occurs in vertebrates with aquatic-related life cycles including metamorphosis and was lost during land transition.
results.author: Lorenzo Alibardi
results.takeaway: Organ regeneration in amphibians and fish is influenced by their aquatic life cycle and evolutionary history, while in terrestrial vertebrates, wound healing and scarring are limited.

results.title: What Determines the Regenerative Capacity in Animals
results.author: Andong Zhao
results.takeaway: Regenerative capacity varies greatly across animals, with factors such as stem cell properties, dedifferentiation and transdifferentiation potentials, gene expression, epigenetic regulators, and immune responses playing a role.

results.title: The price of staying warm
results.author: B. Purnell
results.takeaway: Mammals lose heart regenerative ability as a trade-off for increased metabolism needed for endothermy, as seen in zebrafish and salamanders.

results.title: Changes in regeneration-responsive enhancers shape regenerative capacities in vertebrates
results.author: Wei Wang
results.takeaway: Changes in AP-1-enriched regeneration-responsive enhancers are a crucial source of loss of regenerative capacities in vertebrates, with caudal fin regeneration and cardiac regeneration being affected in teleost fish.

results.title: Cross-species metabolomic analysis identifies uridine as a potent regeneration promoting factor
results.author: Zunpeng Liu
results.takeaway: Uridine, a pyrimidine nucleoside, can rejuvenate aged human stem cells and promote tissue regeneration across various species, offering new avenues for metabolic intervention in tissue repair.

results.title: Evolution of Regeneration in Animals: A Tangled Story
results.author: A. Elchaninov
results.takeaway: The evolution of regenerative capacity in animals is complex and not always beneficial, driven by signaling pathways and adaptive immunity advances.

results.title: Animal models to study cardiac regeneration
results.author: Michael Weinberger
results.takeaway: Cardiac regeneration in various vertebrates is regulated by complex cellular and molecular mechanisms, with some species showing more efficient regeneration than others.

results.title: The blastema and epimorphic regeneration in mammals
results.author: A. Seifert
results.takeaway: Regenerative failure in mammals is likely influenced by the cellular response to the microenvironment after traumatic injury, providing a roadmap for advancing human regeneration.

results.title: Zebrafish as a translational regeneration model to study the activation of neural stem cells and role of their environment
results.author: Marcello Ceci
results.takeaway: Zebrafish show effective neuro-regeneration, restoring motor function, while mammals have reduced neurogenesis and poor axonal regeneration, with inflammation being a main inhibitor of neuronal regeneration.

43.verdict: Open — birds are the hard case
43.state: How far warm-blooded vertebrates retain regeneration is live, with birds the limiting case: avian beak development is well mapped (neural crest) and cochlear regeneration exists, but adult appendage regeneration is generally lost in amniotes. The waterfowl beak-regeneration claim sits at this frontier.
